title: Integrate nested command detection into recursive analyzer
description:
Add validation for semantics database entries to catch malformed operation specs early.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (in jf/bash-extract-operations-from-positional-args)

Current problem:
The extraction function trusts the semantics database completely without validating that returned operation specs are well-formed. Malformed entries could cause:
- Cryptic errors deep in call stack
- Invalid operation types propagating through system
- Difficult debugging when semantics entries are wrong

Implementation steps:
1. Create jf/bash--validate-semantics-entry function:
   - Input: semantics plist from database
   - Check :operations field is one of: list, :complex, :flag-dependent, :custom
   - For list specs: validate each operation spec has required fields
   - For :complex: validate :subcommand-handlers exists and is alist
   - For :flag-dependent: validate :flag-handlers exists and is list
   - For :custom: validate :handler exists and is symbol
   - Return t if valid, signal user-error with helpful message if not

2. Call validation in jf/bash-extract-operations-from-positional-args:
   - After (let ((semantics (jf/bash-lookup-command-semantics command-name))))
   - Before (let ((ops-spec (plist-get semantics :operations))))
   - Wrap in condition-case to log warning instead of crashing

3. Add logging with jf/bash-debug flag (if exists, else use message):
   - Log malformed entries for debugging
   - Include command name and problematic fields

Design rationale: Fail fast with clear errors instead of cryptic downstream crashes. Semantics database is manually curated so validation catches typos and structural errors early. Better developer experience when adding new command handlers.

Verification:
- Add test with intentionally malformed semantics entry
- Verify helpful error message appears
- Verify normal operations still work
- All existing tests pass

title: Create behavioral tests for write and edit tools
description:
Complete test coverage with comprehensive Buttercup tests for write_file_in_scope and edit_file_in_scope tools.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/filesystem-tools-scope-expansion-spec.el

Implementation steps:
1. Add write_file_in_scope test scenarios (~12 tests):
   - Path validation: write in write scope, write fails in read-only scope
   - Expansion workflow: out of scope triggers expansion
   - Approval actions: add-to-scope updates paths.write, allow-once grants permission
   - Directory creation: creates parent directories, respects scope
   - Errors: directory creation out of scope
   - Allow-once lifecycle: permission consumed after use
2. Add edit_file_in_scope test scenarios (~12 tests):
   - Path validation: edit in write scope, edit fails in read-only scope
   - Git metadata: git-tracked files, non-git-tracked files (metadata available)
   - String replacement: successful edit, string not found error, file not found error
   - Expansion workflow: out of scope triggers expansion
   - Approval actions: add-to-scope updates paths.write, allow-once grants permission
   - Allow-once lifecycle: permission consumed after use
3. Add transient action handler tests (~5 tests):
   - Add-to-scope updates correct scope section (paths.read for read, paths.write for write/edit)
   - Deny returns user_denied
   - Allow-once grants temporary permission
4. Add metadata display tests:
   - Git-tracked status shown in expansion UI
   - File existence shown in expansion UI
5. Run all tests: ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral

Design rationale:
Write and edit tools share similar validation logic (both require write scope) but have different business logic (write creates files/directories, edit requires git-tracked files and string matching). Testing them together after read_file establishes patterns reduces duplication. Transient action handler tests verify the expansion UI workflow end-to-end.

Design pattern:
Mirror test structure from read_file tests. Reuse setup/helpers. Add tool-specific assertions (directory creation for write, string matching for edit). Verify metadata flows through validation to expansion UI.

Verification:
- All write_file_in_scope scenarios pass: ~12 tests green
- All edit_file_in_scope scenarios pass: ~12 tests green
- Transient action handlers tested: ~5 tests green
- Metadata display tests pass
- Total: ~40 test scenarios green
- Full test suite runs in <5 seconds
- Coverage: all three tools, all workflows, all error cases

Context: design.md § Phase 5 (Behavioral tests), architecture.md § Scenario Mapping, specs/filesystem-behavioral-tests/spec.md

title: Package manager commands allow write operations in read_only category
description:
## Issue

The bash command validator only checks the base command (first token) against categories, ignoring subcommands. This allows write operations for package managers listed in read_only category.

## Example

System-explorer preset lists 'brew' in read_only commands (presets/system-explorer.md line 57).

When Claude calls: run_bash_command("brew install tree", "/Users/jefffarr")
- Parser extracts base command: "brew" (scope-core.el line 569-570)
- Categorization: read-only (line 589)
- Result: ALLOWED with read scope

But 'brew install' MODIFIES system state (installs packages).

## Root Cause

Command parsing at scope-core.el lines 569-570:
```elisp
(let* ((command-parts (split-string command-full "[ |><;&]" t))
       (base-command (car command-parts)))
```

Only the first token is checked against categories. Subcommands are ignored.

## Security Impact

HIGH - Package managers in read_only category can:
- brew install/uninstall/upgrade (system modification)
- apt install/remove/update (system modification)
- pip install/uninstall (Python environment modification)
- npm install/uninstall (Node environment modification)

All of these require elevated permissions or modify system/project state.

## Solutions

1. **Remove package managers from read_only** (safest, breaks use cases)
2. **Add subcommand validation** (complex, requires maintaining allowed subcommands)
3. **Move to dangerous category** (requires explicit approval for each use)
4. **Add documentation warning** (doesn't solve security issue)

## Recommendation

Option 3: Move all package managers from read_only to dangerous category. Users who need package queries can explicitly approve them via scope expansion.

## Files
- config/gptel/presets/system-explorer.md (lines 56-65)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (lines 569-570, 582-600)
close_reason:
Fixed P0 security bug: package managers removed from read_only category and added to deny lists.

Root cause: Package manager commands (npm, pip, brew, cargo, gem, composer, go, etc.) were incorrectly categorized as read_only in system-explorer.md preset. The bash_tools validation only checks the base command (first token), not subcommands, so 'npm' in read_only allowed both 'npm list' (safe) and 'npm install' (writes files).

Changes made:
1. system-explorer.md preset: Removed all package managers from read_only category and added to deny list (brew, apt, apt-get, yum, dnf, pip, pip3, npm, yarn, pnpm, gem, cargo, composer, go)
2. Updated documentation to explain why package managers are denied and provide alternatives (read manifest files directly: package.json, requirements.txt, Gemfile, etc.)
3. Updated all example code to use alternative approaches
4. Added package managers to deny lists in scope profiles: coding.yml, research.yml, bash-enabled.yml

Security impact: Without this fix, any preset with package managers in read_only could execute write operations (npm install, pip3 install, brew install) if paths.write was configured. Even with empty paths.write, package managers might write to cache directories or temp locations.

All files updated without tangling issues (these are YAML/MD preset files, not literate org files).

title: Fix question mark glob pattern to exclude directory separators
description:
Fix critical security bug where ? wildcard incorrectly matches directory separators in glob patterns.

**Problem:**
The jf/bash--glob-segment-to-regex function converts ? to . (any character) instead of [^/] (any character except slash). This allows patterns like '/workspace/file?.txt' to match '/workspace/file/.txt', bypassing security rules.

**Files to modify:**
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-glob.el (line 71, line 116)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-glob.org (corresponding sections)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-glob-matching.el (add test case)

**Implementation steps:**
1. In jf/bash--glob-segment-to-regex at line 71, change:
   ((eq ch ??)
    (setq regex (concat regex "."))  ; BUG: matches /
   To:
   ((eq ch ??)
    (setq regex (concat regex "[^/]"))  ; CORRECT: excludes /

2. Make the same fix in jf/bash--glob-to-regex at line 19 (already correct, verify consistency)

3. Add test case to test-glob-matching.el:
   (ert-deftest test-glob-question-mark-no-match-directory-separator ()
     "Test that ? does NOT match directory separator /"
     (should-not (jf/bash-glob-match-p "/workspace/file/.txt" "/workspace/file?.txt"))
     (should-not (jf/bash-glob-match-p "/a/b" "/?/?")))

4. Tangle both .org files to regenerate .el files
5. Run validation: ./bin/tangle-org.sh bash-parser-glob.org

**Design rationale:**
In standard glob semantics, ? matches exactly one character but never crosses directory boundaries. This is critical for security validation - a rule allowing '/workspace/*.txt' should not allow '/workspace/../etc/passwd' through ? expansion. The fix ensures ? behaves like * (which correctly uses [^/]*) but for single characters.

**Verification:**
- Run: make test-glob-matching
- Verify new test passes
- Verify existing tests still pass (35 tests total)
- Check security implications: pattern '/workspace/file?' should NOT match paths with embedded slashes

title: Fix exec block extraction to include find operations
description:
Fix jf/bash-extract-from-exec-blocks to extract operations from both find command and exec blocks.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (lines 853-902)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-file-operations.el

Current behavior:
Function only extracts operations from exec-block commands (e.g., rm in 'find . -exec rm {} \;') but does NOT extract operations from the find command itself (directory traversal, pattern matching).

Spec requirement (openspec/specs/bash-file-operations/spec.md lines 38-47):
'Scenario: Find with exec rm
- WHEN extracting from "find . -name '*.log' -exec rm {} \;"
- THEN system returns operations from BOTH find (:read-directory on ".") 
  AND rm (:delete on matched files)'

Implementation steps:
1. Before processing exec-blocks, extract find operations using semantics database:
   - Call jf/bash-extract-operations-from-positional-args on parsed-command
   - This will invoke custom handler jf/bash--extract-find-operations
   - Captures :read-directory on search path and :match-pattern on -name argument

2. Then extract exec-block operations as currently implemented

3. Merge both operation lists before returning

4. Add test specifically for 'find . -name "*.log" -exec rm {} \;':
   - Verify 3 operations returned
   - Check for :read-directory on "."
   - Check for :match-pattern on "*.log"
   - Check for :delete on "{}" with :indirect t

Design rationale: The exec blocks are nested operations, but the find command itself performs file system operations that must be extracted for complete security validation. Missing the directory traversal operation means we can't validate that find is allowed to search that directory.

Verification:
- Test 'find . -name "*.log" -exec rm {} \;' returns 3 operations
- Test 'find /workspace -name "*.txt" -exec cat {} \;' validates workspace access
- All existing tests pass

title: Update script execution test expectations for PWD resolution
description:
Update test-corpus-script-execution.el test expectations to account for PWD-based relative path resolution.

DEPENDENCY:
Must be done AFTER emacs-g4f6 (relative path resolution) is implemented. The implementation will change parser behavior for relative paths when PWD is in var-context, so test expectations must be updated accordingly.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-script-execution.el

Background:
Currently, the script execution corpus tests contain entries like:
  (:command "./script.sh" :expect-ops ((:file "./script.sh" ...)))

After implementing PWD resolution (emacs-g4f6), when var-context contains PWD, relative paths will be resolved to absolute paths. Tests need to be updated to expect resolved paths when PWD is provided.

Implementation steps:

1. Identify affected test cases:
   - Search for test cases with commands starting with "./" or containing "." as path
   - Examples: exec-self-relative-001, exec-self-relative-002, exec-self-relative-003
   - Lines ~171-200 in test-corpus-script-execution.el

2. Update test expectations pattern:
   
   Before (current):
   (:id "exec-self-relative-001"
    :command "./script.sh"
    :expect-ops ((:file "./script.sh" :operation :execute ...)))
   
   After (with PWD resolution):
   (:id "exec-self-relative-001"
    :command "./script.sh"
    :var-context ((PWD . "/base/dir"))
    :expect-ops ((:file "/base/dir/script.sh" :operation :execute ...)))

3. Update test cases to include var-context:
   - Add :var-context ((PWD . "/test/dir")) to test case properties
   - Update :expect-ops :file field to expected absolute path
   - Choose appropriate test directory (e.g., "/test/dir", "/base/project")
   - Ensure consistency: same PWD value should produce same absolute path

4. Cases needing updates:
   - exec-self-relative-001: "./script.sh" → "/base/dir/script.sh"
   - exec-self-relative-002: "./script.sh arg1 arg2" → "/base/dir/script.sh"
   - exec-self-relative-003: "./deploy.sh staging us-west-2" → "/base/dir/deploy.sh"
   - exec-variable-003: "./ input.txt" → may need PWD context
   - exec-pipeline-002: "./script.sh | python filter.py" → "/base/dir/script.sh"
   - exec-chain-002: "./configure && make && ./bin/app" → multiple paths
   - Any other cases with relative paths

5. Add test cases WITHOUT var-context for backward compatibility:
   - Duplicate some relative path tests without :var-context
   - These should still expect unresolved relative paths (./script.sh)
   - Ensures parser doesn't require PWD context - it should gracefully handle both cases
   
   Example:
   (:id "exec-self-relative-001-no-context"
    :command "./script.sh"
    :var-context nil  ; Explicit no context
    :expect-ops ((:file "./script.sh" :operation :execute ...)))

6. Update snapshot after implementation:
   - Run: make test-directory DIR=config/experiments/bash-parser --snapshot
   - Review changes to test-results.txt
   - Verify updated expectations match new behavior

Design rationale:
The test corpus serves as specification for parser behavior. When PWD resolution is implemented, tests must reflect the new expected behavior: relative paths resolve to absolute when PWD is available. However, tests must also verify backward compatibility: without PWD context, relative paths remain unresolved. This dual testing ensures the parser handles both scoped (with directory) and unscoped (without directory) execution contexts.

Verification approach:
- Before making changes: Run tests, observe failures
- Update expectations: Modify test corpus entries
- Run tests again: Should pass with new expectations
- Snapshot comparison: git diff test-results.txt shows updated behavior
- All 467 corpus tests should still pass (just with different expectations)

Test discovery:
Find all test cases with relative paths:
  grep -n '":command.*\"\./\|:command.*\" \.' config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-script-execution.el

Example updated test case:
  (:id "exec-self-relative-002"
   :command "./script.sh arg1 arg2"
   :note "Self-executing with arguments (PWD-resolved)"
   :var-context ((PWD . "/project/bin"))
   :expect-ops ((:file "/project/bin/script.sh"
                 :operation :execute
                 :confidence :low  ; Still low - self-execution detection unchanged
                 :source :command-name
                 :self-executing t
                 :script-args ("arg1" "arg2"))))

  (:id "exec-self-relative-002-no-pwd"
   :command "./script.sh arg1 arg2"
   :note "Self-executing without PWD context (unresolved)"
   :var-context nil
   :expect-ops ((:file "./script.sh"
                 :operation :execute
                 :confidence :low
                 :source :command-name
                 :self-executing t
                 :script-args ("arg1" "arg2"))))

title: Data flow gap: preset registration bash_tools not propagated to scope.yml
description:
## Issue

When a preset with bash_tools is registered:

1. **Preset Registration** (preset-registration.el):
   - Parses preset .md file with bash_tools section
   - Normalizes bash_tools → :bash-tools
   - Extracts :bash-tools to jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults alist
   - Stores as (preset-name . (:bash-tools ...))

2. **Scope Profile Resolution** (scope-profiles.el):
   - jf/gptel-scope-profile--resolve looks up preset in jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults
   - Returns the scope plist including :bash-tools
   - jf/gptel-scope-profile--write-scope-yml converts :bash-tools back to bash_tools
   - Writes to scope.yml

3. **Scope Loading** (scope-core.el):
   - jf/gptel-scope--load-config reads scope.yml
   - Parses YAML and normalizes bash_tools → :bash-tools
   - Returns config plist with :bash-tools

4. **Validation** (scope-shell-tools.el):
   - jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command gets config :bash-tools
   - Accesses nested :categories → :read-only → :commands

## Verification Points

✓ Key normalization is recursive (preset-registration.el line 60-77)
✓ scope-profiles.el writes snake_case keys (line 155-158)
✓ scope-core.el normalizes on load (line 222-242)

## Potential Issue

The example scope.yml files found do NOT contain bash_tools sections, only paths, org_roam_patterns, and shell_commands. This suggests:
- Either bash_tools is not being written to scope.yml
- Or the preset used didn't have bash_tools defined
- Or scope-profiles.el's YAML writer doesn't handle bash_tools nested structure

## Action Required

Verify that scope-profiles.el's jf/gptel-scope-profile--plist-to-yaml correctly serializes the bash_tools nested structure with triple nesting:
```yaml
bash_tools:
  categories:
    read_only:
      commands: ["ls"]
```

## Files
- config/gptel/preset-registration.el
- config/gptel/scope-profiles.el (jf/gptel-scope-profile--plist-to-yaml)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el
notes: Reviewed data flow for bash_tools propagation. Preset registration normalizes recursively (line 72-76). Scope profiles YAML writer serializes nested plists recursively (line 163). Default template structure is correct (lines 190-193). All three nesting levels (bash_tools, categories, read_only/safe_write/dangerous) handled correctly.

title: Add test coverage for bash command parsing edge cases
description:
## Issue

The jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool function parses commands to extract the base command (lines 606-607 in scope-core.el), but there's no verification that this handles all edge cases correctly.

## Edge Cases to Test

1. **Command with pipes**: "grep foo | head" → should extract "grep"
2. **Command with redirects**: "ls > output.txt" → should extract "ls"
3. **Command with semicolons**: "cd /tmp; ls" → should extract "cd"
4. **Command with &&**: "mkdir foo && cd foo" → should extract "mkdir"
5. **Command with leading/trailing whitespace**: "  ls  " → should extract "ls"
6. **Command with complex composition**: "find . -name '*.el' | xargs grep -l 'defun' | head" → should extract "find"
7. **Commands with quoted arguments containing spaces**: "echo 'hello world'" → should extract "echo"

## Current Implementation

```elisp
(let* ((command-parts (split-string command-full "[ |><;&]" t))
       (base-command (car command-parts)))
```

This uses a simple regex split which may not handle all cases correctly.

## Verification

Compare with scope-shell-tools.el jf/gptel-bash--parse-command (lines 31-42) to ensure consistency.

## Impact
MEDIUM - Incorrect parsing could allow dangerous commands or block safe ones
notes: Reviewed bash command parsing. Found inconsistent parsing between scope-core and scope-shell-tools (emacs-w9h5). No test coverage exists (emacs-ewt9). Parser handles most edge cases but should be unified.

title: scope-expansion YAML reader doesn't normalize bash_tools keys from preset.md
description:
## Problem

scope-expansion.org lines 362-363 read bash_tools from parsed YAML using both hyphenated and underscored forms, but doesn't apply the same normalization that preset-registration.org uses.

## Location
File: config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.org
Lines: 362-363

```elisp
(bash-tools (or (plist-get parsed :bash-tools)
                (plist-get parsed :bash_tools)
                (list)))
```

## Context
- preset-registration.org has jf/gptel-preset--normalize-keys (line 110) that recursively normalizes ALL keys
- scope-expansion parses YAML directly without normalization (line 353)
- This creates inconsistency: categories might be :read_only instead of :read-only

## Impact
When scope-expansion reads an existing scope.yml that was written with underscored keys (bash_tools, read_only, safe_write), it may fail to access nested category keys because:
1. It accepts both :bash-tools and :bash_tools at the top level (line 362-363)
2. But the NESTED categories aren't normalized (read_only -> read-only)
3. Later code expects :read-only, :safe-write, :dangerous (hyphenated)

## Fix
Apply normalization after YAML parsing in jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope:

```elisp
(let* ((content (with-temp-buffer
                  (insert-file-contents scope-file)
                  (buffer-string)))
       (parsed (condition-case err
                   (let ((raw (yaml-parse-string content
                                                 :object-type 'plist
                                                 :object-key-type 'keyword
                                                 :sequence-type 'list)))
                     ;; Apply normalization like preset-registration does
                     (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys raw))
                 (error
                  (user-error "Failed to parse scope.yml (%s): %s"
                              scope-file (error-message-string err)))))
       (bash-tools (plist-get parsed :bash-tools))  ;; Only need hyphenated form now
       ...)
```

Then remove the fallback on line 363 since normalization guarantees :bash-tools.

## Verification
1. Create scope.yml with bash_tools (underscored) and read_only category
2. Call jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope to add a command
3. Verify categories are accessible and command is added to correct category
notes: RESOLVED: scope-expansion.el line 288 already calls jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys after YAML parsing. The normalization is applied correctly.

title: Test parse completeness enforcement (stage 2)
description:
Implement behavioral tests for parse completeness enforcement (strict vs permissive modes).

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/run-bash-command-spec.el

Implementation steps:

1. Create describe block: 'run_bash_command tool - Parse completeness enforcement (stage 2)'

2. Test: Strict mode rejects incomplete parse (syntax error)
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: true
   - Command: 'function incomplete() {'
   - Mock jf/bash-parse: Return {:parse-complete nil :parse-errors 'Unexpected EOF'}
   - Assert: :error 'parse_incomplete', :parse-errors contains 'EOF'

3. Test: Strict mode rejects incomplete loop
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: true
   - Command: 'for i in ; do echo $i; done'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete nil :parse-errors 'Syntax error in for-loop'}
   - Assert: :error 'parse_incomplete'

4. Test: Strict mode rejects incomplete conditional
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: true
   - Command: 'if [ -f file.txt ; then cat'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete nil}
   - Assert: :error 'parse_incomplete'

5. Test: Strict mode allows valid complex syntax
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: true
   - Command: 'for f in /workspace/*.el; do grep TODO $f; done'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete t}
   - Mock semantics: Return file-ops for read operations
   - Assert: Success (proceeds to file validation)

6. Test: Strict mode allows valid conditionals
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: true
   - Command: 'if [ -f /workspace/file ]; then cat /workspace/file; fi'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete t}
   - Assert: Success (complete parse)

7. Test: Permissive mode allows incomplete parse with warning
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: false
   - Command: 'function incomplete() {'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete nil :parse-errors 'Unexpected EOF'}
   - Assert: :success t (continues despite incomplete parse)
   - Assert: :warnings contains incomplete parse notice

8. Test: Permissive mode allows partial syntax
   - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: false
   - Command: 'for i in ; do echo $i'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete nil}
   - Assert: Success with warning

9. Test: Default mode is strict
   - Setup: Scope with NO security section (uses defaults)
   - Command: 'function incomplete() {'
   - Mock parse: {:parse-complete nil}
   - Assert: :error 'parse_incomplete' (strict is default)

10. Test: Parse completeness checked before deny list
    - Setup: Scope with security.enforce_parse_complete: true
    - Command: 'sudo incomplete syntax {'
    - Mock parse: {:parse-complete nil}
    - Assert: :error 'parse_incomplete' (fails at stage 2, before stage 3 deny list check)

Design rationale: Parse completeness enforcement is a security feature that prevents execution of commands bash-parser cannot fully understand. If the parser cannot extract complete semantics, the command may contain hidden operations that bypass validation.

Strict mode (default): Reject unparseable commands entirely. This is the secure default - if we can't parse it, we can't validate it safely.

Permissive mode: Allow with warning. Useful during development or when working with edge-case bash syntax that tree-sitter doesn't fully support. User accepts risk that semantic extraction may be incomplete.

The :parse-complete flag comes from bash-parser, indicating whether tree-sitter successfully parsed the entire command without errors.

Test pattern: Mock jf/bash-parse to return :parse-complete nil with error messages, verify enforcement matches security.enforce_parse_complete setting.

Verification:
- Run ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral
- All 10 tests pass
- Coverage: Strict mode rejection, permissive mode warning, default behavior, various syntax errors

- for f in $(ls *.log); do process $f; done

Understanding pattern flow is essential for:

title: Rewrite session open hook for upstream preset restore
description:
Rewrite session open hook for upstream preset restore

## Files to modify
- `config/gptel/sessions/commands.org` — rewrite `jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer`

## Implementation steps

1. Update file detection in `jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer` to match TWO patterns:
   - Branch sessions: `*/branches/*/session.md` — session-dir is `../../` from the file
   - Agent sessions: `*/agents/*/session.md` — session-dir is the agent directory itself (flat, no branches)
2. For agent sessions: set `jf/gptel--session-dir` to agent directory, `jf/gptel--branch-name` to `"main"`, `jf/gptel--branch-dir` to same as session-dir
3. Change session state detection from `gptel-backend` to `(local-variable-p 'gptel--preset)`:
   - This is correct because upstream's differential save always writes `gptel--preset`
   - `gptel-backend` may NOT be in Local Variables if it matches the preset default
4. **Existing session path** (has `gptel--preset` in Local Variables):
   - Set buffer-local session variables (session-id, session-dir, branch-name, branch-dir)
   - Enable `gptel-mode` — this triggers `gptel--restore-state` (gptel.el line 885) which applies preset and overlays saved overrides
   - Load scope from `scope.yml` (via `jf/gptel--scope-file-path`)
   - Do NOT call any custom preset loading functions
5. **New session path** (no Local Variables — just created):
   - Set buffer-local session variables
   - Read preset name from `metadata.yml` (via `jf/gptel--metadata-file-path`)
   - Apply preset via `(gptel--apply-preset preset-name (lambda (var val) (set (make-local-variable var) val)))`
   - Enable `gptel-mode`
   - Load scope from `scope.yml`
   - On first save, upstream writes `gptel--preset` to Local Variables
6. **Legacy session path** (has `preset.md` but no `gptel--preset` in Local Variables):
   - Detect via: no `gptel--preset` Local Variable AND `preset.md` exists in branch directory
   - Read preset.md YAML frontmatter to determine preset name (match against registered presets)
   - Apply matching preset via `gptel--apply-preset` with buffer-local setter
   - Create `scope.yml` from preset.md's scope sections (paths, org_roam_patterns, shell_commands)
   - Enable gptel-mode
   - Log warning: "Legacy session auto-migrated. Next save will write Local Variables."
   - If no matching registered preset: log warning, fall back to raw settings from preset.md, do NOT set `gptel--preset`
7. Keep fast guard: exit early if buffer-file-name doesn't end with `session.md`
8. Tangle and validate

## Design rationale

Delegates restore entirely to upstream for existing sessions — `gptel--restore-state` applies preset and overlays saved overrides. This eliminates our custom `jf/gptel--load-preset-from-file` and `jf/gptel--apply-session-preset`. New sessions need explicit `gptel--apply-preset` because Local Variables don't exist yet — the find-file-hook reads the preset name from metadata.yml (written by session creation). Legacy fallback ensures existing sessions work during migration.

The state detection change (gptel-backend → gptel--preset) is critical: upstream's differential save only writes `gptel--preset` plus values that differ from the preset. `gptel-backend` may not be in Local Variables if it matches the preset default.

## Verification

- `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/sessions/commands.org` passes
- Existing session opens correctly: preset applied, tools available, system message set
- New session (just created): preset applied from metadata.yml, Local Variables written on first save
- Legacy session with preset.md: auto-migrated, warning logged
- Agent session matches `*/agents/*/session.md` pattern and initializes correctly
- Non-session files open without delay (fast guard)

Context: design.md § Decision 6, sessions-persistence.md § Open lifecycle

title: Add Buttercup tests for no-op allowance scenarios
description:
Create comprehensive Buttercup test suite for no-op command allowance with one test per spec scenario.

Files to create:
- config/gptel/tools/test/unit/no-op-allowance-spec.el
- config/gptel/tools/test/test-helpers.el (if doesn't exist)

Implementation steps:
1. Create test-helpers.el with:
   - make-test-scope-config (creates minimal scope config for testing)
   - make-no-op-semantics (returns semantics with empty :filesystem domain)
   - make-file-op-semantics (returns semantics with file operations)
   - make-deny-list-config (creates config with deny list)
2. Create no-op-allowance-spec.el with describe/it structure
3. Map each scenario from no-op-command-allowance/spec.md to one test
4. Use spy-on to mock jf/bash-extract-semantics for controlled testing
5. Test all 6 requirements with traceability docstrings

Design rationale: Buttercup's BDD syntax (describe/it/expect) maps naturally to spec scenarios. One test per scenario enables automated traceability reports and makes test failures easier to understand. The spy system allows controlled testing without running actual bash-parser (faster, more reliable tests).

Test structure pattern:
```elisp
(describe "No-Op Command Allowance"
  (before-each
    (setq test-config (make-test-scope-config)))

  (it "allows version check commands"
    "Scenario: no-op-command-allowance/spec.md § 'Version check command allowed'"
    (spy-on 'jf/bash-extract-semantics :and-return-value (make-no-op-semantics))
    (expect (validate "python3 --version" "/tmp" test-config) :to-be nil))

  (it "blocks no-op command in deny list"
    "Scenario: no-op-command-allowance/spec.md § 'No-op command in deny list still blocked'"
    (spy-on 'jf/bash-extract-semantics :and-return-value (make-no-op-semantics))
    (let ((config (make-deny-list-config '("sudo"))))
      (expect (validate "sudo --version" "/tmp" config) :to-match "command_denied"))))
```

Verification:
- Run: make test-buttercup-directory DIR=config/gptel/tools/test/unit
- All 6+ tests pass (covering all spec scenarios)
- Test output shows traceability to spec scenarios
- Tests fail appropriately when validation broken
- Spy system works correctly (no actual bash-parser calls)

Context: design.md § Decision 4: Buttercup for New Tests, § Decision 5: One Test Per Spec Scenario

title: [MEDIUM] PersistentAgent: Empty array path behavior differs from spec
description:
## Problem

Spec says allowed_paths=[] should mean "no path restrictions", but implementation treats it same as omitting the parameter (inherits from parent).

## Spec Requirement

**openspec/specs/gptel/persistent-agent.md lines 31-37:**
> Scenario: Optional path inheritance from parent
> - WHEN allowed_paths is not provided (nil)
> - THEN the agent inherits allowed read paths from parent's preset.md
> - WHEN allowed_paths is provided as empty array []
> - THEN the agent has no path restrictions (reads from anywhere)
> - WHEN allowed_paths is provided with patterns
> - THEN the agent uses exactly those patterns (no inheritance)

## Current Implementation

**config/gptel/tools/persistent-agent.org lines 598-616:**
```elisp
(effective-allowed-paths
 (or allowed-paths-list  ; Empty list is falsy, falls through
     ;; Inherit from parent
     (let ((parent-preset-file ...))
       (plist-get parent-config :paths-read))))
```

When allowed_paths=[] (empty array):
1. JSON deserializes to empty vector []
2. `(append [] nil)` converts to empty list nil
3. `(or nil ...)` evaluates falsy → falls through to parent inheritance

## Impact

- Users cannot create unrestricted agents by passing []
- Must use wildcard pattern ["/**"] instead
- Behavior doesn't match spec intention to distinguish "not provided" from "empty array provided"

## Fix

Distinguish nil from empty list:
```elisp
(effective-allowed-paths
 (if (null allowed-paths)
     ;; Parameter not provided → inherit from parent
     (let ((parent-preset-file (expand-file-name "preset.md" jf/gptel--session-dir)))
       (when (file-exists-p parent-preset-file)
         (let ((parent-config (jf/gptel-scope--parse-preset-config parent-preset-file)))
           (plist-get parent-config :paths-read))))
   ;; Parameter provided (even if empty list) → use as-is (empty = unrestricted)
   (append allowed-paths nil)))
```

## Alternative

Update spec to say both nil and [] inherit from parent (simpler, matches current behavior).

## Verification

Agent ID: ac91998

title: Bash validator in scope-core not accessing commands correctly
description:
The jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool function in config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org (lines 651-653) accesses category commands incorrectly.

Current code:
  (read-only (plist-get categories :read-only))
  (safe-write (plist-get categories :safe-write))
  (dangerous (plist-get categories :dangerous))

Expected structure per design.md:
bash_tools:
  read_only:
    commands: ["ls", "grep", ...]

Should be:
  (read-only (plist-get (plist-get categories :read-only) :commands))
  (safe-write (plist-get (plist-get categories :safe-write) :commands))
  (dangerous (plist-get (plist-get categories :dangerous) :commands))

Alternatively, should use the helper function jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command from scope-shell-tools.el which already implements this correctly.

title: Add missing interpreter commands (ruby, perl, php, exec)
description:
The semantics database has excellent coverage of interpreters (python, node, bash, sh, zsh, go) but is missing several common scripting languages and the exec built-in.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.el (generated)
- test/test-command-semantics.el

Add these interpreter entries:

1. Ruby interpreter:
```elisp
(ruby . (:operations :flag-dependent
         :flag-handlers ((("-e") . ())  ; -e executes inline code
                        (() . ((:source :positional-args :operation :execute :index 0))))))
```

2. Perl interpreter:
```elisp
(perl . (:operations :flag-dependent
         :flag-handlers ((("-e" "-E") . ())  ; -e/-E execute inline code
                        (() . ((:source :positional-args :operation :execute :index 0))))))
```

3. PHP interpreter:
```elisp
(php . (:operations :flag-dependent
        :flag-handlers ((("-r") . ())  ; -r executes inline code
                       (() . ((:source :positional-args :operation :execute :index 0))))))
```

4. exec built-in:
```elisp
(exec . (:operations ((:source :positional-args :operation :execute :index 0))))
```

Implementation steps:
1. Edit bash-parser-semantics.org
2. Add ruby, perl, php after the existing interpreter section (after zsh)
3. Add exec after source and . (dot) entries
4. Run ./bin/tangle-org.sh to regenerate .el file
5. Add test cases for each interpreter

Design rationale: Ruby, Perl, and PHP are still widely used scripting languages in legacy systems and specific domains (Perl for sysadmin, Ruby for Rails/Chef/Puppet, PHP for web). The exec built-in is important because it replaces the current shell process with the executed command, creating an :execute operation on the target script.

Pattern: Follow the same flag-dependent pattern as python/node/bash. Each interpreter has flags for inline code execution that should NOT create file operations. Only when executing a script file should the :execute operation be created.

Testing:
Add test cases following the existing interpreter test pattern:

```elisp
(ert-deftest test-semantics-ruby-interpreter ()
  (let* ((result (jf/bash-lookup-command-semantics "ruby"))
         (ops-spec (plist-get result :operations)))
    (should (eq ops-spec :flag-dependent))
    (let* ((flag-handlers (plist-get result :flag-handlers))
           (default-handler (cdr (car (last flag-handlers))))
           (op (car default-handler)))
      (should (eq (plist-get op :operation) :execute))
      (should (= (plist-get op :index) 0)))))

(ert-deftest test-semantics-exec-builtin ()
  (let* ((result (jf/bash-lookup-command-semantics "exec"))
         (ops (plist-get result :operations))
         (op (car ops)))
    (should (eq (plist-get op :operation) :execute))
    (should (= (plist-get op :index) 0))))
```

Verification:
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-semantics-.*-interpreter'
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-semantics-exec-builtin'
- All new interpreter tests should pass

title: format-tool-error discards bash validator's structured error fields
description:
jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error doesn't preserve bash-specific error fields from the validator.

Bash validator returns (scope-core.el lines 643-666):
- :reason "directory_not_in_scope"
- :command (the command string)
- :directory (the directory path)
- :required-scope ("read" or "write")
- :allowed-patterns (list of path patterns)
- :message (descriptive text)

But format-tool-error (scope-core.el lines 774-789) ONLY extracts:
- :patterns (maps to :allowed-patterns) 
- :deny-patterns
- :error (from :reason)
- :message

The :command, :directory, and :required-scope fields are LOST.

Impact: LLM receives incomplete error information. The spec (scope-shell-tools.org lines 110-119) shows errors should include directory, required_scope, and allowed_patterns - but the implementation drops critical fields.

Fix: Either:
1. format-tool-error should pass through all check-result fields, OR
2. Bash validator should use standard field names (:patterns instead of :allowed-patterns)

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (format-tool-error and bash validator)
close_reason:
Preserve bash-specific fields in format-tool-error

Modified jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error to:
- Support both :allowed-patterns (bash) and :patterns (other validators)
- Support both :reason (bash) and :error (other validators)  
- Pass through bash-specific fields: :command, :directory, :required-scope

This ensures LLM receives complete error information including directory path, required scope level, and command details when bash tools are denied.

title: Phase 3: Behavioral workflow tests for scope validation
description:
Implement 10-15 behavioral workflow tests for end-to-end scope validation scenarios with mocked gptel sessions and user interactions.

## Context

After unit tests (Phase 1) cover individual functions and integration tests (Phase 2) cover module orchestration, Phase 3 adds behavioral tests for complete user workflows. These tests simulate real Claude-user interactions including tool calling, scope expansion, and error recovery.

Behavioral tests validate:
- End-to-end command execution workflows
- Scope expansion user experience (deny, add-to-scope, allow-once)
- Error message quality and actionability
- Multi-turn conversations with state management
- Real gptel session behavior (mocked backend)

These tests use full gptel session mocking to simulate LLM tool calls and user responses.

## Files to Create

config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/
└── workflows-spec.el    (15 tests - end-to-end user scenarios)

## Implementation Steps

### 1. Create workflows-spec.el (15 tests)

Test complete user workflows with gptel mocking:

```elisp
;;; workflows-spec.el --- Behavioral tests for scope validation workflows -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

(require 'buttercup)
(require 'test-scope-helpers)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-shell-tools)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-core)

(describe "User workflow: Allowed command execution"
  (it "executes command and returns output to user"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      ;; Setup: Create temp scope.yml with read permissions
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"ls\"]\n"))
          
          ;; Mock successful command execution
          (test-scope--mock-call-process 0 "file1.txt\nfile2.txt\n")
          
          ;; Execute: LLM calls run_bash_command
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "ls -la" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Success with output
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be t)
            (expect (plist-get result :output) :to-match "file1.txt")
            (expect (plist-get result :exit_code) :to-equal 0)))))))

(describe "User workflow: Denied command with helpful error"
  (it "rejects command and guides LLM to request scope expansion"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"ls\"]\n")
            (insert "  deny: [\"rm\"]"))
          
          ;; Execute: LLM tries denied command
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "rm file.txt" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Structured error with actionable message
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be nil)
            (expect (plist-get result :error) :to-match "command")
            (expect (plist-get result :message) :to-match "request_scope_expansion")))))))

(describe "User workflow: Command timeout with clear feedback"
  (it "times out long-running command and suggests filters"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"find\"]\n"))
          
          ;; Mock timeout
          (spy-on 'call-process :and-call-fake
                  (lambda (&rest args)
                    (sleep-for 35)  ; Exceed timeout
                    0))
          
          ;; Execute: Long-running command
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "find / -name '*.txt'" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Timeout error with helpful suggestion
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be t)  ; Command executed but timed out
            (expect (plist-get result :warnings) :to-have-length-of 1)
            (expect (car (plist-get result :warnings)) :to-match "more specific")))))))

(describe "User workflow: Truncated output with filter suggestion"
  (it "truncates long output and suggests using filters"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"cat\"]\n"))
          
          ;; Mock huge output
          (let ((huge-output (make-string 20000 ?x)))
            (test-scope--mock-call-process 0 huge-output))
          
          ;; Execute: Command with huge output
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "cat huge-file.txt" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Truncated with suggestion
            (expect (plist-get result :truncated) :to-be t)
            (expect (plist-get result :output) :to-match "truncated")
            (expect (plist-get result :output) :to-match "grep\|head\|tail")))))))

(describe "Scope expansion workflow: User denies request"
  (it "rejects expansion and LLM receives denial error"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        ;; Setup: Minimal scope
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: []\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: []\n"))
          
          ;; Mock user denies expansion
          (test-scope--mock-scope-expansion-approval 'deny)
          
          ;; Execute: Request expansion
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "request_scope_expansion")
                                nil "run_bash_command"
                                ["tree"]
                                "Need to visualize directory structure")))
            
            ;; Verify: Expansion denied
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be nil)
            (expect (plist-get result :error) :to-equal "expansion_denied")))))))

(describe "Scope expansion workflow: User adds to scope"
  (it "permanently updates scope.yml and allows command"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          ;; Setup: Minimal scope
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: []\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: []\n"))
          
          ;; Mock user approves and adds to scope
          (test-scope--mock-scope-expansion-approval 'add-to-scope)
          
          ;; Execute: Request expansion
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "request_scope_expansion")
                                nil "run_bash_command"
                                ["tree"]
                                "Need tree command")))
            
            ;; Verify: Expansion approved
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be t)
            (expect (plist-get result :patterns-added) :to-equal '("tree"))
            
            ;; Verify: scope.yml updated
            (with-temp-buffer
              (insert-file-contents scope-file)
              (expect (buffer-string) :to-match "tree"))))))))

(describe "Scope expansion workflow: User allows once"
  (it "grants temporary permission for current turn only"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          ;; Setup: Minimal scope
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: []\n"))
          
          ;; Mock user approves allow-once
          (test-scope--mock-scope-expansion-approval 'allow-once)
          
          ;; Execute: Request expansion
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "request_scope_expansion")
                                nil "run_bash_command"
                                ["tree"]
                                "Temporary tree access")))
            
            ;; Verify: Allow-once granted
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be t)
            (expect (plist-get result :allowed-once) :to-be t)
            
            ;; Verify: Now can run tree command once
            (test-scope--mock-call-process 0 "directory structure")
            (let ((run-result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                      "tree" temp-dir)))
              (expect (plist-get run-result :success) :to-be t))
            
            ;; Verify: Second attempt fails (permission consumed)
            (let ((run-result-2 (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                        "tree" temp-dir)))
              (expect (plist-get run-result-2 :success) :to-be nil))))))))

(describe "Multi-turn workflow: Allow-once cleared after response"
  (it "clears temporary permissions when LLM response completes"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        ;; Grant allow-once
        (jf/gptel-scope-add-to-allow-once-list "run_bash_command" "tree:/tmp")
        (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-have-length-of 1)
        
        ;; Simulate gptel response completion
        (jf/gptel-scope--clear-allow-once)
        
        ;; Verify: List cleared
        (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-be nil)))))

(describe "Error recovery workflow: Parse error with helpful guidance"
  (it "provides clear error for unparseable commands"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"cat\"]\n")
            (insert "security:\n  enforce_parse_complete: true\n"))
          
          ;; Mock parse error
          (spy-on 'jf/bash-parse :and-return-value
                  '(:parse-complete nil :parse-errors "Unexpected token at position 5"))
          
          ;; Execute: Invalid syntax
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "cat < >" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Clear parse error
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be nil)
            (expect (plist-get result :error) :to-equal "parse_incomplete")
            (expect (plist-get result :message) :to-match "Unexpected token")))))))

(describe "Pipeline bypass prevention workflow"
  (it "prevents dangerous command bypass via pipeline"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"ls\", \"xargs\"]\n")
            (insert "  deny: [\"rm\"]"))
          
          ;; Execute: Attempt bypass with ls | xargs rm
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "ls | xargs rm" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Pipeline validation catches rm
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be nil)
            (expect (plist-get result :error) :to-match "command")
            (expect (plist-get result :message) :to-match "rm")))))))

(describe "Cloud auth workflow: Detection and warning"
  (it "detects cloud auth and provides appropriate warning"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"aws-vault\"]\n")
            (insert "cloud:\n  auth_detection: warn\n"))
          
          ;; Mock cloud auth detection
          (spy-on 'jf/bash-extract-semantics :and-return-value
                  '(:domains (:cloud-auth (:provider :aws
                                          :command "aws-vault exec prod"))))
          
          ;; Execute: Cloud auth command
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "aws-vault exec prod" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Warning included (warn mode is non-blocking)
            (expect (plist-get result :warning) :to-equal "cloud_auth_detected")
            (expect (plist-get result :message) :to-match "aws")))))))

(describe "File path validation workflow: Deny precedence"
  (it "denies access to paths in deny list despite read permissions"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"/**\"]\n")  ; Allow read anywhere
            (insert "  deny: [\"/etc/**\"]\n")        ; Except /etc
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"cat\"]\n"))
          
          ;; Execute: Try to read denied path
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                "cat /etc/passwd" temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Denied despite broad read permission
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be nil)
            (expect (plist-get result :error) :to-match "path")
            (expect (plist-get result :message) :to-match "denied")))))))

(describe "Complex workflow: Multi-stage validation"
  (it "validates command through all applicable stages"
    (test-scope--with-mock-gptel-session
      (test-scope--with-temp-directory temp-dir
        (let ((scope-file (expand-file-name "scope.yml" temp-dir)))
          (with-temp-file scope-file
            (insert "paths:\n  read: [\"" temp-dir "/**\"]\n")
            (insert "  write: [\"/tmp/**\"]\n")
            (insert "bash_tools:\n  categories:\n    read_only:\n      commands: [\"cat\", \"grep\"]\n")
            (insert "    safe_write:\n      commands: [\"echo\"]\n")
            (insert "cloud:\n  auth_detection: allow\n")
            (insert "security:\n  enforce_parse_complete: true\n"))
          
          ;; Mock successful parse and semantics
          (spy-on 'jf/bash-parse :and-return-value
                  '(:parse-complete t
                    :all-commands ((:command-name "cat")
                                  (:command-name "grep")
                                  (:command-name "echo"))))
          (spy-on 'jf/bash-extract-semantics :and-return-value
                  '(:domains (:filesystem ((:operation :read
                                           :path (expand-file-name "data.txt" temp-dir)
                                           :command-name "cat")
                                          (:operation :write
                                           :path "/tmp/output.txt"
                                           :command-name "echo")))))
          
          ;; Mock successful execution
          (test-scope--mock-call-process 0 "filtered data")
          
          ;; Execute: Complex command
          (setq-local jf/gptel--branch-dir temp-dir)
          (let ((result (funcall (gptel-tool-function "run_bash_command")
                                (format "cat %s/data.txt | grep pattern | echo result > /tmp/output.txt"
                                       temp-dir)
                                temp-dir)))
            
            ;; Verify: Success through all validation stages
            (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be t)
            (expect (plist-get result :output) :to-equal "filtered data")))))))

(provide 'behavioral-workflows-spec)
```

## Design Rationale

**Full gptel session mocking:**
Behavioral tests need to simulate real Claude-user interactions. This requires:
- Mocking gptel buffer and session context
- Mocking tool function execution
- Mocking user responses (scope expansion UI)
- Tracking tool calls and results

**Test user experience, not implementation:**
Behavioral tests focus on what users see and experience:
- Are error messages helpful?
- Does scope expansion work smoothly?
- Are warnings clear and actionable?
- Does the system recover from errors?

**Real workflow scenarios:**
Tests simulate actual use cases:
- Claude runs allowed command → sees output
- Claude tries denied command → gets helpful error → requests expansion
- User approves/denies → system updates correctly
- Multi-turn conversations with state management

**Error message quality:**
Tests verify error messages guide the LLM correctly:
- Structured errors with actionable suggestions
- Clear indication of what went wrong
- Guidance on how to resolve (request_scope_expansion)
- No confusing or ambiguous messages

**State management:**
Tests verify lifecycle of temporary permissions:
- Allow-once works for single use
- Permissions cleared after response
- No permission leakage across turns

## Verification

After completing behavioral tests:

1. **All tests pass:**
```bash
make test-behavioral
# Should run 15 tests, all passing
```

2. **Test file exists:**
```bash
ls -la config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/
# Should show: workflows-spec.el
```

3. **Full test suite passes:**
```bash
make test
# Should run 220+ tests (60 unit + 160 integration + 15 behavioral)
```

4. **Coverage complete:**
Check testing-gap-analysis.md:
- Target: 33/35 functions tested (94%)
- Unit: 60 tests
- Integration: 160 tests  
- Behavioral: 15 tests
- Total: 220+ tests

5. **User workflows validated:**
```bash
./bin/run-tests.sh -t behavioral | grep "User workflow"
# Should show all workflow scenarios tested
```

## Dependencies

Requires: emacs-op2t (Phase 2 integration tests) - provides integration patterns and combined validation tests

## Completion Criteria

After this phase:
- ✅ Comprehensive test suite (220+ tests)
- ✅ Proper testing pyramid (60 unit, 160 integration, 15 behavioral)
- ✅ All critical functions tested (94% coverage)
- ✅ User workflows validated end-to-end
- ✅ Error messages verified for quality
- ✅ Scope expansion workflows tested
- ✅ Multi-turn state management verified

title: bash-parser: Inconsistent Package-Requires headers across modules
description:
Some bash-parser modules have complete package headers while others are missing Author, Keywords, and Package-Requires fields.

Modules WITH complete headers:
- bash-parser-protocol.el (lines 25-36)
- bash-parser-file-ops.el (lines 22-34)
- bash-parser-recursive.el (lines 38-55)
- bash-parser-extensions.el (line 19)

Modules MISSING headers:
- bash-parser.el (only lexical-binding, line 38)
- bash-parser-core.el (only lexical-binding, line 70)
- bash-parser-glob.el (only lexical-binding, line 31)
- bash-parser-security.el (only lexical-binding, line 25)
- bash-parser-semantics.el (only lexical-binding, line 25)
- bash-parser-variables.el (only lexical-binding, line 22)

Files to modify (all .org files):
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-glob.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-security.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org

Standard header template:
```elisp
;;; module-name.el --- Brief description -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

;; Author: Jeff Farr
;; Keywords: bash, parser, [module-specific-keywords]
;; Package-Requires: ((emacs "27.1"))

;;; Commentary:

;; Module-specific commentary explaining purpose and capabilities.

;;; Code:
```

Design rationale:
Consistent package headers improve discoverability, follow Emacs packaging conventions, and enable tools like package-lint and checkdoc to validate the codebase.

Module-specific keywords:
- bash-parser.el: bash, parser, loader
- bash-parser-core.el: bash, parser, core, tree-sitter
- bash-parser-glob.el: bash, parser, glob, patterns
- bash-parser-security.el: bash, parser, security, validation
- bash-parser-semantics.el: bash, parser, semantics, database
- bash-parser-variables.el: bash, parser, variables, resolution

Verification:
- Tangle each modified file
- Run checkdoc on generated .el files
- Verify no new warnings

title: Add rmdir to deny lists - can remove directories
description:
Add 'rmdir' command to deny lists in both presets.

Risk: Similar to rm, can remove directories causing data loss.

title: Handle YAML array format for tags field
description:
Fix YAML tag parsing to handle bracket array format.

**Location:** config/gptel/commands/commands-core.el:210

**Issue:** YAML array format 'tags: [tag1, tag2]' (with brackets) may not parse correctly with current regex split.

title: scope-expansion.el writes underscored keys instead of normalized snake_case
description:
In scope-expansion.el, the jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope function writes :bash_tools key back to parsed plist (line 307) but jf/gptel-scope--write-yaml-plist doesn't convert keys from kebab-case to snake_case for YAML output.

The issue:
- Line 307: (setq parsed (plist-put parsed :bash_tools bash-tools))
- This uses underscored :bash_tools directly
- But jf/gptel-scope--write-yaml-plist (line 314+) writes keys as-is without conversion
- Result: Inconsistent key format in written YAML

Expected behavior:
- All internal operations use kebab-case (:bash-tools)
- YAML writer converts back to snake_case (bash_tools)
- Currently mixing direct underscore usage

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el (lines 286-287, 307, 354)

Fix needed:
- Remove fallback 'or' for :bash_tools vs :bash-tools (lines 286-287)
- Use :bash-tools consistently internally
- Ensure jf/gptel-scope--write-yaml-plist converts keys properly (check if it does)
