title: Break down large bash-parser functions into focused helpers
description:
Decompose 3 large functions (>100 lines) into focused single-responsibility helpers.

Large functions mix abstraction levels and are harder to understand, test, and maintain. Breaking them into helpers improves code quality.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.org (and .el)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (and .el)  
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (and .el)

Large function 1: jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive (284 lines)
Location: bash-parser-recursive.el:82-366

Current structure mixes:
- Orchestration (lines 82-129)
- Substitution processing (lines 131-159)
- Pattern flow operations (lines 161-166)
- Pipeline handling (lines 168-173)
- Chain handling with context (lines 175-233) - very complex
- Loop processing (lines 235-303)
- Conditional processing (lines 306-310)
- Subshell processing (lines 312-326)
- Post-processing (lines 328-364)

Extract helpers:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash--process-command-substitutions (parsed-command var-context depth)
  "Process command substitutions recursively.
Returns (operations . substitution-patterns)."
  ...)

(defun jf/bash--process-chain-with-context (parsed-command var-context depth)
  "Process command chain with variable and directory tracking.
Returns list of operations from all chain commands."
  ...)

(defun jf/bash--process-loop-with-binding (parsed-command var-context depth)
  "Process for-loop with variable binding in body.
Returns list of operations from loop body with loop metadata."
  ...)

(defun jf/bash--normalize-dynamic-paths (operations)
  "Normalize dynamic file paths in OPERATIONS.
Handles heredoc + variables, command substitutions in loops.
Returns updated operations list."
  ...)

;; Main function becomes orchestration only
(defun jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive (parsed-command var-context &optional depth)
  "Recursively extract file operations."
  (let ((depth (or depth 0))
        (operations nil))
    (when (>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
      (error "Max recursion depth exceeded"))
    
    ;; Extract from this level
    (when (fboundp 'jf/bash--extract-from-single-command)
      (setq operations (jf/bash--extract-from-single-command 
                       parsed-command var-context)))
    
    ;; Process nested structures using helpers
    (let ((subst-result (jf/bash--process-command-substitutions 
                        parsed-command var-context depth)))
      (setq operations (append operations (car subst-result)))
      (when-let ((patterns (cdr subst-result)))
        (setq operations (append operations
                                (jf/bash--extract-pattern-flow-operations
                                 parsed-command patterns var-context)))))
    
    ;; Delegate to structure-specific helpers
    (pcase (plist-get parsed-command :type)
      (:pipeline ...)
      (:chain (setq operations (append operations
                                      (jf/bash--process-chain-with-context
                                       parsed-command var-context depth))))
      ...)
    
    ;; Normalize paths
    (jf/bash--normalize-dynamic-paths operations)))
```

Large function 2: jf/bash-parse--parse-single-command-node (125 lines)
Location: bash-parser-core.el:963-1087

Mixed concerns:
- Control flow routing (for/if/subshell vs command)
- Command substitution extraction
- Redirection handling
- Variable assignment vs command detection
- Wrapper/exec/normal command parsing

Extract:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash--route-statement-node (node)
  "Route statement NODE to appropriate handler.
Returns symbol: for-statement, if-statement, subshell, command, variable-assignment."
  ...)

(defun jf/bash--parse-command-with-metadata (command-node redirections command-substitutions)
  "Parse command node and attach metadata.
Returns command plist with redirections and substitutions."
  ...)
```

Large function 3: jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs (95 lines)
Location: bash-parser-file-ops.el:593-688

Mixed concerns:
- Index range resolution
- Skip indices filtering
- Command substitution pre-resolution
- Path resolution
- Operation construction

Extract:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash--resolve-target-indices (spec positional-args)
  "Resolve index specification to list of indices.
Handles :index, :indices, :skip-indices from SPEC.
Returns list of integer indices to process."
  ...)

(defun jf/bash--build-file-operation (file-path operation command-name &optional extras)
  "Build operation plist for FILE-PATH.
EXTRAS is optional plist of additional fields.
Returns operation plist with resolved path and metadata."
  ...)
```

Implementation approach:
1. Pick one function to decompose
2. Identify single-responsibility chunks (10-40 lines each)
3. Extract helper with clear name and docstring
4. Update caller to use helper
5. Tangle and test after each extraction
6. Move to next function

Design rationale:
- Functions >100 lines mix abstraction levels
- Hard to understand flow and test in isolation
- Helpers improve code clarity and reusability
- Easier to add logging/debugging to focused helpers
- Single Responsibility Principle

Target structure:
- Main function: 30-50 lines (orchestration only)
- Helpers: 10-40 lines (single responsibility)
- Clear names that describe what, not how

Verification:
- Run full test suite after each extraction
- No behavior changes expected
- Main functions become more readable
- Helper functions can be unit tested independently

title: Spec uses wrong field name for error responses
description:
**Location:** bash-tools/spec.md lines 181-187

**Spec claims:**
- Line 183: "system returns :error 'command_not_allowed'"
- Line 187: "system returns :error 'command_denied'"

**Actual implementation** (scope-core.el):
- Line 577: `(list :allowed nil :reason "denied" ...)`
- Line 597: `(list :allowed nil :reason "command_not_allowed" ...)`
- Line 607: `(list :allowed nil :reason "dangerous_command" ...)`

**Issue:**
The validation functions return `:reason` field, NOT `:error` field.

**Fix needed:**
Update all error response scenarios in bash-tools/spec.md to use `:reason` instead of `:error".

Also note the value mismatch:
- Spec says "command_denied" but code uses "denied"

title: Error type naming inconsistency across validators
description:
Error :reason values are inconsistent across different validators in scope-core.org:

Path validator (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool):
- :reason 'denied-pattern' (line 454)
- :reason 'not-in-scope' (line 462)

Pattern validator (jf/gptel-scope--validate-pattern-tool):
- :reason 'not-in-org-roam-patterns' (line 512, 531, 543)
- :reason 'unknown-org-roam-tool' (line 551)

Command validator (jf/gptel-scope--validate-command-tool):
- :reason 'denied-command' (line 586)
- :reason 'not-in-allowlist' (line 595)

Bash validator (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool):
- :reason 'command_not_allowed' (line 638, 671) <-- SNAKE_CASE!
- :reason 'denied' (line 651, 696) <-- INCONSISTENT with 'denied-pattern'/'denied-command'
- :reason 'dangerous_command' (line 681) <-- SNAKE_CASE!
- :reason 'directory_not_in_scope' (line 711, 724) <-- SNAKE_CASE!

Issues:

1. Most validators use kebab-case (denied-pattern, not-in-scope) but bash validator uses snake_case (command_not_allowed, dangerous_command)

2. Bash validator uses generic 'denied' (line 651, 696) while other validators use specific reasons (denied-pattern, denied-command)

3. No documented list of all possible reason values - developers must search code

4. Format-tool-error (line 835) uses :error field not :reason field, creating more confusion about which field contains what

Looking at format-tool-error:
  Line 842: (error-type (or (plist-get check-result :error) "scope-violation"))
  
So validators return :reason but format-tool-error maps this to :error for LLM response.

This is confusing: validators use :reason, but formatted errors use :error.

Recommendation:
1. Standardize all reason values to kebab-case
2. Document all possible reason/error values in a constant or docstring
3. Clarify :reason (internal) vs :error (LLM-facing) distinction
close_reason: Standardize error reasons to kebab-case across all validators

title: scope-core validator duplicates bash validation logic instead of delegating
description:
The bash validation is implemented in TWO places with different approaches:

1. scope-core.el jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool (lines 608-669)
   - Used by gptel-make-scoped-tool macro
   - Inline implementation parsing commands and validating directories

2. scope-shell-tools.el helper functions (lines 51-121)
   - jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command
   - jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category
   
The scope-core validator should DELEGATE to scope-shell-tools helpers rather than duplicating the logic.

Benefits of delegating:
- Single source of truth for categorization logic
- Easier to maintain and test
- Behavior consistency guaranteed
- Helper functions can be unit tested independently

Current issue: scope-core duplicates command parsing (split-string "[ |><;&]+") that's already in jf/gptel-bash--parse-command.

Related: emacs-g6yp tracks the same issue from a different angle.

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (validator implementation)
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el (helper functions)
close_reason:
Duplication already resolved by removing unused helpers (emacs-t01a)

Same as emacs-g6yp - the bash validation duplication was resolved by bead emacs-t01a, which removed the unused helper functions from scope-shell-tools.org.

The validator jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool in scope-core.org is now the authoritative implementation. The only helper still used is jf/gptel-bash--parse-command for command parsing, which is appropriate since it's bash-specific utility logic rather than validation logic.

Keeping validation consolidated in scope-core provides a single source of truth and avoids spreading validation logic across multiple modules.

title: Test operation-specific path validation (stage 6)
description:
Implement behavioral tests for operation-specific path validation (read/write/execute/modify scoping).

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/run-bash-command-spec.el

Implementation steps:

1. Create describe block: 'run_bash_command tool - Operation-specific path validation (stage 6)'

2. Test: Read operation allowed in paths.read scope
   - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/workspace/**', '/tmp/**']
   - Command: 'cat /workspace/file.txt'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :read :path '/workspace/file.txt'}
   - Assert: Success (read operation matches read scope)

3. Test: Read operation allowed in paths.write scope (hierarchical permissions)
   - Setup: Scope with paths.write: ['/workspace/**']
   - Command: 'cat /workspace/output.txt'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :read :path '/workspace/output.txt'}
   - Assert: Success (write scope includes read capability)

4. Test: Read operation denied outside scope
   - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/workspace/**']
   - Command: 'cat /etc/passwd'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :read :path '/etc/passwd'}
   - Assert: :error 'path_out_of_scope', :path '/etc/passwd', :operation :read

5. Test: Write operation requires paths.write
   - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/tmp/**'] (read only, no write)
   - Command: 'echo test > /tmp/output.txt'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :write :path '/tmp/output.txt'}
   - Assert: :error 'path_out_of_scope' (write requires write scope)

6. Test: Write operation allowed in paths.write
   - Setup: Scope with paths.write: ['/workspace/**']
   - Command: 'mkdir /workspace/newdir'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :write :path '/workspace/newdir'}
   - Assert: Success

7. Test: Execute operation requires paths.execute
   - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/workspace/**'], no paths.execute
   - Command: 'bash /workspace/scripts/deploy.sh'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :execute :path '/workspace/scripts/deploy.sh'}
   - Assert: :error 'path_out_of_scope', :operation :execute

8. Test: Execute operation allowed in paths.execute scope
   - Setup: Scope with paths.execute: ['/workspace/scripts/**']
   - Command: 'python3 /workspace/scripts/deploy.py'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :execute :path '/workspace/scripts/deploy.py'}
   - Assert: Success

9. Test: Modify operation requires paths.modify
   - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/workspace/**'], no paths.modify
   - Command: 'sed -i "s/foo/bar/" /workspace/config.yml'
   - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :modify :path '/workspace/config.yml'}
   - Assert: :error 'path_out_of_scope', :operation :modify

10. Test: Modify operation allowed in paths.modify scope
    - Setup: Scope with paths.modify: ['/workspace/config/**']
    - Command: 'sed -i "s/foo/bar/" /workspace/config/app.yml'
    - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :modify :path '/workspace/config/app.yml'}
    - Assert: Success

11. Test: Modify operation allowed in paths.write scope (hierarchical)
    - Setup: Scope with paths.write: ['/workspace/**']
    - Command: 'sed -i "s/foo/bar/" /workspace/data.txt'
    - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :modify :path '/workspace/data.txt'}
    - Assert: Success (write scope includes modify capability)

12. Test: Deny patterns override all allow patterns
    - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/workspace/**'], paths.deny: ['~/.ssh/**']
    - Command: 'cat /workspace/.ssh/id_rsa'
    - Mock semantics: Return file-op {:operation :read :path '/workspace/.ssh/id_rsa'}
    - Assert: :error 'path_denied' (deny takes precedence over read)

13. Test: Multiple file operations validated independently
    - Command: 'cp /workspace/src.txt /tmp/dst.txt'
    - Mock semantics: Return two file-ops:
      * {:operation :read :path '/workspace/src.txt'}
      * {:operation :write :path '/tmp/dst.txt'}
    - Setup: Scope with paths.read: ['/workspace/**'], paths.write: ['/workspace/**']
    - Assert: :error 'path_out_of_scope' for dst.txt (write outside write scope)

14. Test: Multiple operations all in scope succeed
    - Command: 'cp /workspace/src.txt /workspace/dst.txt'
    - Mock semantics: Return two file-ops (read src, write dst)
    - Setup: Scope with paths.write: ['/workspace/**']
    - Assert: Success (write scope includes read, both operations allowed)

Design rationale: Operation-specific path validation is the core v4 security model. Instead of category-based allowlists (v3), v4 validates each file operation against operation-specific scope patterns. This provides fine-grained control:
- Read-only access to logs: paths.read: ['/var/log/**']
- Write access to workspace: paths.write: ['/workspace/**']
- Execute only in scripts dir: paths.execute: ['/workspace/scripts/**']
- Modify only config files: paths.modify: ['/workspace/config/**']

Hierarchical permissions reduce configuration burden:
- Write scope includes read and modify capabilities
- Deny patterns override all allow patterns

Test pattern: Mock bash-parser semantics to return file operations with specific operation types, verify path validation against scope configuration.

Verification:
- Run ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral
- All 14 tests pass
- Coverage: All operation types (read/write/execute/modify), hierarchical permissions, deny precedence, multiple operations

title: Fix position tracking bug in two-pass expansion
description:
Fix position offset tracking in commands-injection late expansion.

**Location:** config/gptel/commands/commands-injection.el:150-153

**Issue:** Forward processing of collected positions without offset tracking causes wrong replacement positions when multiple commands are in buffer.

**Example:**
- Buffer has /cmd1 at position 10, /cmd2 at position 50
- Replace /cmd1 → inserts 100-char block → buffer grows by 80 chars
- Try to replace position 50 → but actual position is now 130

**Current Behavior:** Second and subsequent commands may be replaced at wrong positions.

**Fix Options:**
1. Process in reverse order (latest positions first, earliest last)
2. Use buffer markers instead of raw positions
3. Track position offsets as buffer changes

**Recommended:** Option 1 (reverse processing) - simplest and most robust.

**Test:**
- Create buffer with multiple commands: /opsx:explore followed by /opsx:new
- Send without immediate expansion (trigger late expansion)
- Verify both commands expanded at correct positions

title: Variable Resolution
description:
Resolve variable references against provided context.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "Variable Handling - Resolution"
2. Implement `jf/bash-resolve-variables` function
3. Accept file path and variable context (alist of name → value)
4. Replace variable references with values from context:
   - $VAR → context value for VAR
   - ${VAR} → context value for VAR
5. Support partial resolution:
   - "$WORKSPACE/$FILE" with only WORKSPACE → "/workspace/$FILE"
6. Mark unresolved variables:
   - Return (:resolved-path "..." :unresolved-vars ("FILE"))
7. Handle missing context gracefully (return unresolved status)

Design rationale:
Resolution uses provided context (explicit variable declarations). Partial resolution is important - some vars may be known, others not. Unresolved variables marked for security checker to reject (fail-safe). Simple string replacement approach handles 80% of real-world cases without full shell semantics complexity.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-resolve-variables (file-path var-context)
  "Resolve variables in FILE-PATH using VAR-CONTEXT. Return (resolved-path unresolved-vars)."
  (let ((resolved file-path)
        (unresolved nil))
    (save-match-data
      (let ((var-refs (jf/bash-detect-variable-references file-path)))
        (when (car var-refs)  ; has variables
          (dolist (var-name (cdr var-refs))
            (let ((value (alist-get (intern var-name) var-context)))
              (if value
                  ;; Replace $VAR or ${VAR} with value
                  (setq resolved (replace-regexp-in-string
                                 (format "\\${%s}\\|\\$%s\\b" var-name var-name)
                                 value resolved))
                ;; Track unresolved
                (push var-name unresolved)))))))
    (list :resolved-path resolved :unresolved-vars (nreverse unresolved))))
```

Verification:
- "$WORKSPACE/file.txt" with context (WORKSPACE . "/workspace") → "/workspace/file.txt"
- "$UNKNOWN/file.txt" with empty context → (:unresolved-vars ("UNKNOWN"))
- "$WORKSPACE/$FILE" with only WORKSPACE → "/workspace/$FILE" with :unresolved-vars ("FILE")
- Test all resolution scenarios from bash-file-operations/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 7: Variable Tracking"
close_reason: Implemented variable resolution with detection and resolution functions. All test cases pass.

title: Design Docker operations visitor
description:
## Context

With the visitor infrastructure in place, we can now add domain-specific analyzers. The Docker operations visitor will extract information about container operations from docker commands.

## Goal

Create a visitor that extracts Docker-specific operations (container create/exec/delete, image operations, volume/network operations) to demonstrate extensibility of the visitor pattern.

## Requirements

1. **Docker operations visitor**:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash--docker-operations-visitor (node context depth)
     "Extract Docker operations from NODE."
     ;; Returns list of docker operation plists
     )
   ```

2. **Operation types to extract**:
   - **docker run**: image, container name, ports, volumes, env vars, network
   - **docker exec**: container name, command executed
   - **docker rm/stop/kill**: container names
   - **docker build**: image tag, Dockerfile path, build context
   - **docker pull/push**: image names
   - **docker network create/rm**: network names
   - **docker volume create/rm**: volume names

3. **Operation plist format**:
   ```elisp
   (:operation :container-create
    :image "nginx:latest"
    :container-name "web-server"
    :ports (("80" . "8080") ("443" . "8443"))
    :volumes (("/data" . "/var/data"))
    :env-vars (("ENV" . "production"))
    :network "app-network"
    :command "docker"
    :visitor-type 'docker-operations
    :confidence :high)
   ```

4. **Handle common patterns**:
   - Short flags: `-p 80:8080`
   - Long flags: `--port 80:8080`
   - Multiple values: `-p 80:8080 -p 443:8443`
   - Named containers: `--name web-server`
   - Environment variables: `-e KEY=VALUE`

## Implementation Plan

1. Create `bash-parser-docker.el` module
2. Implement Docker semantics extractor functions
3. Implement `jf/bash--docker-operations-visitor`
4. Add Docker command patterns to test corpus
5. Write comprehensive tests

## Test Cases

```elisp
;; docker run
"docker run -d -p 80:8080 --name web nginx:latest"
=> (:operation :container-create :image "nginx:latest" 
    :container-name "web" :ports (("80" . "8080")) ...)

;; docker exec
"docker exec web-server ls /var/log"
=> (:operation :container-execute :container "web-server" 
    :exec-command ("ls" "/var/log") ...)

;; docker build
"docker build -t myapp:v1.0 ."
=> (:operation :image-build :tag "myapp:v1.0" 
    :build-context "." ...)

;; docker network
"docker network create app-network"
=> (:operation :network-create :network-name "app-network" ...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Extracts container create operations from `docker run`
- [ ] Extracts exec operations from `docker exec`
- [ ] Extracts delete operations from `docker rm/stop/kill`
- [ ] Parses port mappings correctly
- [ ] Parses volume mounts correctly
- [ ] Parses environment variables
- [ ] Works alongside file-operations visitor
- [ ] Comprehensive test coverage

## Files to Create

- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-docker.el (new)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-docker-visitor.el (new)

## Dependencies

Requires: emacs-six3 (visitor infrastructure)

## Notes

This demonstrates the visitor pattern's extensibility. Docker operations are completely independent from file operations but can be extracted from the same AST traversal.
close_reason: Superseded by plugin-architecture approach. Docker operations will be implemented as plugin in future, not visitor.

title: File Operations Extraction Tests
description:
Create comprehensive ERT tests for file operations extraction.

Files to create:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-file-operations.el

Implementation steps:
1. Create test file with ERT setup
2. Implement tests for each extraction scenario from spec:
   - Simple read command (cat /workspace/foo.txt)
   - Commands with multiple file arguments (cp src dest)
   - Commands with no file operations (echo hello)
   - Extraction from redirections (>, >>, <, 2>)
   - Extraction from find -exec blocks
   - Multi-command constructs (pipelines, chains)
   - Confidence level classification
   - Operation metadata (source, command, context)
   - Deduplication of operations
   - Glob patterns in arguments (preserved, not expanded)
   - Relative and absolute paths
   - Variable references (detection, resolution, tracking)
   - Variable assignments in chains
   - Unresolved variable handling
3. Use naming convention: test-extraction-* or test-variable-*
4. Include spec reference in docstring

Design rationale:
Extraction is core functionality - comprehensive tests critical. Test all sources (redirections, positional args, exec blocks) independently and combined. Variable handling tests verify Phase 1 variable support. Tests use parsed commands as input (depends on parser but tests extraction logic).

Design pattern:
```elisp
(ert-deftest test-extraction-simple-read-command ()
  "Scenario: bash-file-operations § 'Simple read command'"
  (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "cat /workspace/foo.txt"))
         (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
    (should (= (length ops) 1))
    (should (equal (plist-get (car ops) :file) "/workspace/foo.txt"))
    (should (eq (plist-get (car ops) :operation) :read))
    (should (eq (plist-get (car ops) :confidence) :high))
    (should (eq (plist-get (car ops) :source) :positional-arg))))

(ert-deftest test-variable-resolve-declared-variable ()
  "Scenario: bash-file-operations § 'Resolve declared variable'"
  (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "cat $WORKSPACE/file.txt"))
         (context '((WORKSPACE . "/workspace")))
         (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed context)))
    (should (equal (plist-get (car ops) :file) "/workspace/file.txt"))))
```

Verification:
- All test-extraction-* and test-variable-* tests pass
- Coverage for all scenarios in bash-file-operations/spec.md
- Tests verify extraction from all sources and variable handling

Context: architecture.md § "Testing Approach" + specs/bash-file-operations/spec.md

title: Enhance expansion UI with metadata display

title: Document AST node lifetime limitations in bash-parser-core
description:
Document that :ast field tree-sitter nodes become invalid after parsing completes.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (Main Entry Point section)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (after tangling)

Problem:
The jf/bash-parse function returns tree-sitter AST nodes in the :ast field, but these nodes become invalid after the function returns because parsing happens in a with-temp-buffer that is killed when parsing completes.

Current docstring (line 6-30) documents :ast field but doesn't warn about lifetime:
  :ast - tree-sitter root node (for debugging)

This can cause crashes if callers try to use the AST after parsing:
  (let ((result (jf/bash-parse "ls -la")))
    ;; This may crash or return garbage:
    (treesit-node-text (plist-get result :ast)))

Implementation steps:

1. Update jf/bash-parse docstring to include warning:

Change:
  :ast - tree-sitter root node (for debugging)

To:
  :ast - tree-sitter root node (for debugging)
         WARNING: AST nodes are only valid during parsing. Do not use
         this field after jf/bash-parse returns, as the underlying
         buffer is destroyed. The AST is provided for debugging only.
         All necessary information is extracted into other fields.

2. Add module-level documentation in the Introduction section:

Add to bash-parser-core.org Introduction:

** AST Node Lifetime

The parser returns tree-sitter AST nodes in the :ast field for debugging
purposes. These nodes are ONLY valid during parsing and become invalid
when the function returns.

Why: Parsing happens in with-temp-buffer (line 48). When jf/bash-parse
returns, the temp buffer is killed and all tree-sitter nodes tied to
that buffer become invalid.

Recommendation: Do not use :ast field in production code. All necessary
information is extracted into structured fields like :command-name,
:positional-args, :redirections, etc.

If you need to inspect AST for debugging:
1. Use it immediately in same call stack
2. Or add logging inside jf/bash-parse--internal
3. Or use persistent buffers (not recommended for production)

3. Add comment at with-temp-buffer site (line 48):

Add comment before with-temp-buffer:
  ;; Parse in temp buffer. Note: AST nodes returned in :ast field
  ;; become invalid after this function returns. All needed data
  ;; is extracted into the result plist.

Design rationale:
Tree-sitter nodes are tied to buffers and become invalid when buffers die. This is fundamental to tree-sitter's design for memory efficiency. We parse in temp buffers to avoid polluting the buffer list and to ensure clean state.

The :ast field exists for debugging (e.g., inspecting parse tree during development), not for production use. All production-relevant data is extracted into structured fields before the buffer dies.

Alternative considered but rejected:
- Use persistent buffers: Leaks memory, complicates cleanup
- Deep-copy AST: Expensive, defeats tree-sitter efficiency
- Remove :ast field: Useful for debugging, just needs documentation

Verification:
1. Read updated docstring: emacs-lisp-mode shows full docstring
2. Verify warning is visible in:
   - M-x describe-function jf/bash-parse
   - Eldoc in editor
   - Generated HTML documentation
3. Add test that demonstrates the limitation:
   (ert-deftest test-ast-node-lifetime ()
     "Demonstrate AST nodes are invalid after parse."
     (let* ((result (jf/bash-parse "ls"))
            (ast (plist-get result :ast)))
       ;; Document expected behavior: node may be invalid
       ;; This test exists to document the limitation, not assert behavior
       (message "AST node type: %S" (ignore-errors (treesit-node-type ast)))))

4. Check literate documentation renders correctly in HTML/PDF

Priority: P1 - Prevents user confusion and potential crashes.
close_reason: Implemented via parallel orchestrator - Wave 1 documentation sprint

title: Implement scope.yml v4 schema structure and validation
description:
Add v4 schema support with operation-specific paths (read/write/execute/modify), cloud authentication config, and security settings. Implement schema validation and safe defaults.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.org (schema loading section)
- Add jf/gptel-scope-v4-defaults constant
- Add jf/gptel-scope--load-v4-schema function
- Add jf/gptel-scope--validate-schema-v4 function
- Add jf/gptel-scope--load-cloud-config function
- Add jf/gptel-scope--load-security-config function

Implementation steps:
1. Define jf/gptel-scope-v4-defaults constant with safe defaults (paths empty, cloud.auth-detection "warn", security.enforce-parse-complete t, max-coverage-threshold 0.8)
2. Implement schema loader that merges YAML with defaults (missing sections get defaults)
3. Implement schema validator that checks present sections for invalid values (fail fast on invalid)
4. Validate cloud.auth-detection is "allow", "warn", or "deny"
5. Validate security.max-coverage-threshold is 0.0-1.0 float
6. Validate all path sections are lists
7. Add YAML snake_case to elisp kebab-case normalization (auth_detection → :auth-detection)
8. Tangle and validate with ./bin/tangle-org.sh

Design rationale: V4 schema enables operation-specific security (execute requires explicit permission) and cloud awareness. Defaults for missing sections enable gradual adoption - users can add new sections incrementally without breaking existing configs. Strict validation of present sections catches errors at load time (fail fast) rather than runtime. This balances flexibility (gradual adoption) with safety (explicit is better than implicit).

Design pattern: Schema defaults with strict validation pattern. Missing = safe default, present = must be valid. Example in description field

Verification:
- Load v4 schema with all sections present - validates successfully
- Load v4 schema with missing cloud section - gets "warn" default
- Load v4 schema with invalid auth_detection value - errors immediately
- Load v4 schema with invalid threshold - errors with clear message
- YAML snake_case correctly normalizes to elisp kebab-case

Context: design.md § Decision 4 (Schema v4 loading), specs/scope-schema-v4/spec.md

title: Add no-op allowance stage to validation pipeline
description:
Implement no-op check at Stage 4 (after deny list, before file validation) to allow commands with zero file operations by default.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.org

Implementation steps:
1. Add helper function jf/gptel-scope--check-no-op:
   - Extract :filesystem domain from semantics
   - Return nil (success) if file-ops list is empty or nil
   - Return continue signal if file-ops exist
2. Insert no-op check in validation pipeline after deny list check (Stage 4)
3. Short-circuit validation if no-op check succeeds (cl-return-from validate nil)
4. Ensure deny list checked BEFORE no-op (sudo --version still denied)
5. Update stage numbering: file validation becomes Stage 5

Design rationale: Commands with no file system operations are safe to execute by default. This eliminates the need to maintain explicit allowlists of "safe" commands (python3 --version, node --help, etc.). The deny list still catches dangerous commands regardless of operations (security first), but most introspection commands now work without configuration.

Design pattern: No-op short-circuit
```elisp
(defun jf/gptel-scope--check-no-op (semantics)
  "Return nil if command has no file operations (allowed), t otherwise."
  (let ((file-ops (plist-get (plist-get semantics :domains) :filesystem)))
    (if (null file-ops)
        nil    ; Success - no operations, allow command
      t)))     ; Has operations - continue to file validation

;; In validation pipeline:
(when-let ((error (validate-deny-list commands scope-config)))
  (cl-return-from validate error))

;; Stage 4: No-op allowance (NEW)
(unless (jf/gptel-scope--check-no-op semantics)
  (cl-return-from validate nil))  ; Success - no operations

;; Stage 5: File operations validation
(when-let ((error (validate-file-operations semantics directory scope-config)))
  (cl-return-from validate error))
```

Verification:
- Test: python3 --version extracts no ops → allowed
- Test: sudo --version extracts no ops but in deny list → denied
- Test: python3 script.py extracts execute op → proceeds to file validation
- Test: my-custom-tool --info (unknown command, no ops) → allowed
- All spec scenarios from no-op-command-allowance/spec.md pass
- Existing file operation tests still pass

Context: design.md § Decision 1: No-Op Allowance at Stage 4

title: Fix test-recursive-single-substitution regression from command substitution skip
description:
Fix regression in test-recursive-single-substitution caused by Agent 2's command substitution skip logic.

**ROOT CAUSE:**

Commit 7b79dd8 (emacs-vcrm) added logic to skip command substitutions in jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs:

```elisp
;; Skip command substitutions - they are processed recursively
;; and handled by pattern flow operations
(unless (and file-path (string-prefix-p "$(" file-path))
  ...)
```

This prevents creating an operation where :file contains the substitution string "$(find . -name config.yml)". 

**THE FAILING TEST:**

Test: test-recursive-single-substitution in config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-bash-parser-recursive.el
Command: cat $(find . -name config.yml)

Expected behavior (lines 51-55):
- Should find an operation where :file matches "find" and :operation is :read
- This represents the outer cat command operating on the substitution result

Actual behavior after Agent 2's change:
- Operations returned: [(:file "." ...), (:file "config.yml" :operation :match-pattern ...), (:file "config.yml" :operation :read :command "cat" ...)]
- No operation with :file containing "find" exists

**TEST STATUS:**
- PASSING at commit 1bdec57 (before Agent 2)
- FAILING at commit 7b79dd8 (Agent 2 - emacs-vcrm)
- FAILING at commit 7ccce20 (Agent 3 - emacs-iapv) - not Agent 3's fault

**ANALYSIS:**

There are two competing requirements:

1. Agent 2's fix (emacs-vcrm): Prevent duplicate operations for command substitutions
   - Problem solved: Avoid creating $(find ...) as a literal file path operation
   - Side effect: Broke test expecting an operation referencing the substitution

2. Test expectation: Track that outer command operates on substitution result
   - The test expects to find HOW the substitution is consumed
   - Pattern: :file should reference the substitution, :operation should show what the outer command does

**DESIGN QUESTION:**

Which behavior is correct?

Option A: Test expectation is wrong
- Pattern flow operations (:file "config.yml" :operation :read) already show consumption
- The :file field containing the substitution string is redundant
- Fix: Update test to check for pattern flow operation instead

Option B: Skip logic is too aggressive  
- We should still create an operation representing substitution consumption
- The operation should have :file referencing the substitution (or a marker)
- Fix: Create a special operation type for substitution consumption

Option C: Hybrid approach
- Keep the skip to avoid literal file path operations
- Add explicit tracking in pattern flow operations to link back to substitution
- Fix: Enhance pattern flow metadata to satisfy test requirements

**RECOMMENDED APPROACH: Option A (Update Test)**

The pattern flow operation already captures the semantic intent:
- (:file "config.yml" :operation :read :command "cat" :pattern-source ...)

This tells us:
- cat reads the pattern config.yml
- The pattern came from a substitution (:pattern-source)

The test's expectation of :file containing "find" is testing an implementation detail rather than semantic correctness.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-bash-parser-recursive.el (lines 51-55)

Implementation:
1. Replace the third assertion (lines 51-55) with a check for the pattern flow operation
2. Verify :file is "config.yml" (the pattern)
3. Verify :operation is :read
4. Verify :command is "cat"
5. Optionally verify :pattern-source or :from-substitution metadata

New assertion should be:
```elisp
;; cat reads the pattern result from substitution
(should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                   (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "config.yml")
                        (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read)
                        (string= (plist-get op :command) "cat")))
                 ops))
```

Verification:
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-recursive-single-substitution$'
- Should pass after fix
- Verify test still validates recursive analysis works correctly
- Check that semantic intent (cat operates on find's pattern) is still captured

Design rationale:
Agent 2's skip logic was correct - we should not treat $(find ...) as a literal file path. The test was checking for implementation detail (substitution string in :file) rather than semantic correctness (outer command operates on pattern from substitution). The pattern flow operation already captures this relationship properly.

title: Architecture: scope-shell-tools bypass of gptel-make-scoped-tool macro
description:
## Observation

scope-shell-tools.el defines run_bash_command using the gptel-make-scoped-tool macro (line 186), which automatically:
- Loads scope config
- Validates tool permission
- Normalizes arguments
- Executes body only if allowed

However, the bash validator (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool in scope-core.el) is called through the macro's permission check, not directly.

## Data Flow

1. LLM calls run_bash_command(command, directory)
2. gptel-make-scoped-tool macro intercepts
3. Macro calls jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission(config, "run_bash_command", args)
4. Dispatcher routes to jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool based on 'bash validation type
5. Validator categorizes command and validates directory
6. If allowed, macro executes tool body (jf/gptel-bash--execute-command)

## Architectural Consistency

✓ Follows same pattern as other scoped tools (filesystem, org-roam)
✓ Uses centralized validation dispatch (scope-core.el line 698)
✓ Separates validation logic from execution logic

## Potential Concern

The bash validator has complex nested access:
```elisp
(let* ((categories (plist-get bash-config :categories))
       (read-only (plist-get (plist-get categories :read-only) :commands)))
```

If key normalization fails at ANY level, this will silently return nil and categorize all commands as 'unknown.

## Recommendation

Add defensive checks or logging when bash-config or categories is nil to detect normalization failures early.

## Files
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool)
close_reason: Created ARCHITECTURE.md documenting that scope-shell-tools DOES use gptel-make-scoped-tool macro with validation type 'bash', clarifying the dispatcher architecture and routing to bash validator

title: Implement subshell directory context isolation
description:
Implement subshell context isolation - directory changes in subshells should not affect parent.

TESTS TO ENABLE (3 tests):
- test-subshell-cd-isolation: (cd /sub && cat sub.txt) && cat main.txt
- test-subshell-pwd-assignment-isolation: (PWD=/sub && cat sub.txt) && cat main.txt
- test-nested-subshells: Nested subshells each have isolated context

SECURITY REQUIREMENT:
Subshells create isolated variable and directory contexts. Changes in subshell (cd, PWD=) must NOT affect parent context after subshell exits.

FILES TO MODIFY:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.org (source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (tangled)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Detect subshell boundaries in AST
   - Subshells are marked with :type :subshell or similar
   - Check existing parser output for subshell markers

2. Create context copy on subshell entry
   - Copy var-context for subshell
   - Copy current-pwd for subshell
   - Process subshell commands with copied context

3. Restore parent context after subshell
   - Discard subshell context changes
   - Continue with original parent context

4. Handle nested subshells
   - Each level gets its own context copy
   - Changes propagate inward but not outward

EXAMPLE:
  (cd /sub && cat file1.txt) && cat file2.txt
  
  Should extract:
  - file1.txt in /sub context (inside subshell)
  - file2.txt in original context (outside subshell)

REFERENCE:
See DIRECTORY-CONTEXT-TRACKING.md Phase 3 section.
Test file: test-directory-changing-commands.el

VERIFICATION:
After implementation, all 3 subshell isolation tests should pass.

title: Create system-explorer preset for machine state discovery
description:
Create a new preset that grants broad read-only permissions for discovering local machine state, operating system details, package managers, installed packages, and system configuration.

Purpose: Enable agents to safely explore and document system state without risk of modifications. Use cases: environment audits, dependency discovery, configuration documentation, troubleshooting setup issues.

Configuration requirements:

1. Broad read paths with deny patterns for sensitive files
2. System discovery commands (OS info, package managers, file exploration)
3. No write or dangerous commands enabled
4. Temperature 0.3 for deterministic queries

File to create: config/gptel/presets/system-explorer.md

Include comprehensive read_only commands for:
- OS info: uname, sw_vers, lsb_release, cat /etc/os-release
- Package managers: brew, apt, yum, pip, npm, gem, cargo (list operations only)
- File exploration: ls, find, file, stat, cat, grep, head, tail
- Command discovery: which, whereis, type
- Process/network info: ps, top, ifconfig, netstat

Deny patterns for sensitive files:
- **/.ssh/**, **/.gnupg/**, **/*_history
- **/credentials*, **/*.key, **/*.pem
- **/shadow, **/sudoers
- **/node_modules/**, **/.git/objects/**

Example commands showing OS discovery, package queries, and system exploration.

title: Update proposal: scope-shell-tools not scope-bash-tools
description:
## Problem

The proposal.md states the bash tools file is named "scope-bash-tools" but the actual implementation uses "scope-shell-tools".

File: openspec/changes/scoped-bash-command-execution/proposal.md
Line: 44

title: Implement directory stack tracking (pushd/popd)
description:
Implement directory stack management for pushd/popd commands.

TESTS TO ENABLE (2 tests):
- test-pushd-absolute-path: pushd /dir && cat file.txt
- test-popd-returns-to-previous: pushd /dir1 && pushd /dir2 && popd && cat file.txt

FILES TO MODIFY:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (detection and extraction)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.org (stack tracking)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Add pushd command detection
   - Create jf/bash--is-pushd-command-p (similar to is-cd)
   - Detect 'pushd' and 'builtin pushd'

2. Add popd command detection
   - Create jf/bash--is-popd-command-p
   - Detect 'popd' and 'builtin popd'

3. Track directory stack in chain processor
   - Maintain dir-stack list in chain context
   - Initialize as empty list

4. Handle pushd
   - Extract target directory (like cd)
   - Push current-pwd onto dir-stack
   - Update current-pwd to target
   - Update PWD in var-context

5. Handle popd
   - If dir-stack is empty, treat as :unresolved
   - Pop directory from dir-stack
   - Update current-pwd to popped value
   - Update PWD in var-context

EXAMPLE:
  pushd /dir1 && pushd /dir2 && popd && cat file.txt
  
  Sequence:
  1. pushd /dir1: stack=[/original], PWD=/dir1
  2. pushd /dir2: stack=[/original, /dir1], PWD=/dir2
  3. popd: stack=[/original], PWD=/dir1 (popped)
  4. cat file.txt: resolves in /dir1

EDGE CASES:
- popd when stack is empty: conservative - treat as staying in current dir
- pushd with no args: bash swaps top two dirs (complex - maybe skip)
- pushd +N / popd +N: directory stack rotation (low priority)

PRIORITY:
Lower priority than other features - pushd/popd less common in security-sensitive contexts.

REFERENCE:
Test file: test-directory-changing-commands.el
See bash man page for directory stack semantics

VERIFICATION:
After implementation, both pushd/popd tests should pass.

title: Implement PWD variable assignment semantics

Need to add support for:

1. Simple assignment: PWD=/path; cmd
   - Currently handled by variable extraction but might not update directory context
   - Should update context for subsequent commands in chain

2. Inline assignment: PWD=/path cmd
   - Bash semantics: only affects cmd's environment, not subsequent commands
   - For security analysis: conservative approach - may update context or not
   - Document decision in code

DESIGN DECISION NEEDED:
Should inline 'PWD=/path cmd' update context for later commands?
- Bash behavior: No (env var only for that command)
- Security conservative: Maybe yes (report all possible paths)
- Recommendation: Match bash behavior for correctness

See DIRECTORY-CONTEXT-TRACKING.md Phase 2 section.

- test-pwd-assignment-inline

title: Implement loop context tracking with variable binding
description:
Track loop variables and their sources to understand what files are operated on in loop bodies, especially when loop source is a command substitution.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (enhance for-loop parsing)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (track loop context)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.el (resolve loop variables)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-loop-context.el (new test file)

Implementation steps:
1. Enhance for-loop parsing in bash-parser-core.el to extract loop source:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash--parse-for-loop (node)
     "Parse for-loop with loop source tracking."
     (let ((loop-var nil)
           (loop-list nil)
           (loop-body nil)
           (loop-source-type nil)  ; NEW: :literal, :substitution, :glob
           (loop-source-subst nil))  ; NEW: Substitution if source-type is :substitution
       
       ;; Extract loop variable (e.g., "file" in "for file in ...")
       (setq loop-var (jf/bash--extract-loop-variable node))
       
       ;; Extract loop list (e.g., "*.txt" or "$(find ...)")
       (setq loop-list (jf/bash--extract-loop-list node))
       
       ;; Determine loop source type
       (cond
        ((string-match-p "\\$(" (or loop-list ""))
         (setq loop-source-type :substitution)
         ;; Extract the substitution
         (when (string-match "\\$(\\(.*\\))" loop-list)
           (setq loop-source-subst (match-string 1 loop-list))))
        
        ((jf/bash--has-glob-pattern-p loop-list)
         (setq loop-source-type :glob))
        
        (t
         (setq loop-source-type :literal)))
       
       ;; Extract loop body
       (setq loop-body (jf/bash--extract-loop-body node))
       
       (list :command-name "for"
             :loop-variable loop-var
             :loop-list loop-list
             :loop-source-type loop-source-type  ; NEW
             :loop-source-substitution loop-source-subst  ; NEW
             :loop-body loop-body)))
   ```

2. Add loop context tracking to recursive analyzer:
   ```elisp
   ;; In bash-parser-recursive.el
   (defun jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive (parsed-command var-context &optional depth)
     "Recursive analysis with loop context tracking."
     (let ((depth (or depth 0))
           (operations nil)
           (substitution-patterns nil))
       
       ;; ... existing code ...
       
       ;; NEW: Process for-loops with variable binding
       (when (string= (plist-get parsed-command :command-name) "for")
         (let* ((loop-var (plist-get parsed-command :loop-variable))
                (loop-source-type (plist-get parsed-command :loop-source-type))
                (loop-body (plist-get parsed-command :loop-body))
                (loop-var-value nil))
           
           ;; Determine what the loop variable represents
           (cond
            ;; Loop source is substitution - bind to pattern from substitution
            ((eq loop-source-type :substitution)
             ;; Find the pattern produced by the substitution
             (let ((pattern-op (seq-find 
                               (lambda (op) 
                                 (and (eq (plist-get op :operation) :match-pattern)
                                      (plist-get op :from-substitution)))
                               operations)))
               (when pattern-op
                 (setq loop-var-value (plist-get pattern-op :file)))))
            
            ;; Loop source is glob - bind to glob pattern
            ((eq loop-source-type :glob)
             (setq loop-var-value (plist-get parsed-command :loop-list)))
            
            ;; Loop source is literal - bind to literal values
            ((eq loop-source-type :literal)
             (setq loop-var-value :literal-list)))
           
           ;; Parse and analyze loop body with loop variable in context
           (when loop-body
             (let* ((loop-body-parsed (jf/bash-parse loop-body))
                    ;; Add loop variable to context
                    (loop-context (append (list (cons (intern loop-var) loop-var-value))
                                         var-context))
                    (loop-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                              loop-body-parsed loop-context (1+ depth))))
               ;; Mark all loop operations with :loop-context
               (setq loop-ops (mapcar (lambda (op)
                                       (let ((new-op (copy-sequence op)))
                                         (plist-put new-op :loop-context t)
                                         (plist-put new-op :loop-variable loop-var)
                                         new-op))
                                     loop-ops))
               (setq operations (append operations loop-ops))))))
       
       operations))
   ```

3. Enhance variable resolution to handle loop variables:
   ```elisp
   ;; In bash-parser-variables.el
   (defun jf/bash-resolve-variables (path var-context)
     "Resolve variables with loop variable support."
     (let ((resolved-path path)
           (unresolved-vars nil))
       
       ;; Match $VAR or ${VAR}
       (while (string-match "\\$\\({\\([^}]+\\)}\\|\\([a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*\\)\\)" 
                           resolved-path)
         (let* ((match-str (match-string 0 resolved-path))
                (var-name (or (match-string 2 resolved-path)
                             (match-string 3 resolved-path)))
                (var-symbol (intern var-name))
                (var-value (alist-get var-symbol var-context)))
           
           (if var-value
               ;; Variable resolved
               (setq resolved-path (replace-match (if (stringp var-value)
                                                     var-value
                                                   (format "{%s}" var-name))
                                                 t t resolved-path))
             ;; Variable unresolved
             (push var-name unresolved-vars)
             (setq resolved-path (replace-match (format "{%s}" var-name) t t resolved-path)))))
       
       (if unresolved-vars
           (list :path resolved-path :unresolved (nreverse unresolved-vars))
         resolved-path)))
   ```

4. Create test-loop-context.el with loop variable tests:
   ```elisp
   (ert-deftest test-loop-variable-from-substitution ()
     "Test loop variable bound to pattern from substitution."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "for file in $(find . -name '*.txt'); do rm $file; done"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; find produces pattern *.txt
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.txt")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :match-pattern)))
                        ops))
       ;; rm deletes *.txt (resolved from loop variable)
       (let ((rm-op (seq-find (lambda (op)
                               (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.txt")
                                    (eq (plist-get op :operation) :delete)
                                    (plist-get op :loop-context)))
                             ops)))
         (should rm-op)
         (should (string= (plist-get rm-op :loop-variable) "file")))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-loop-variable-from-glob ()
     "Test loop variable bound to glob pattern."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "for file in *.log; do cat $file; done"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; cat reads *.log (resolved from loop variable)
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.log")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read)
                               (plist-get op :loop-context)))
                        ops))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-nested-loop-variables ()
     "Test nested loops with multiple loop variables."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "for dir in */; do for file in $dir/*.txt; do cat $file; done; done"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; Should resolve $file to pattern from inner loop
       ;; This is complex - may need placeholder for $dir
       (should ops)))
   ```

5. Update corpus with loop context expectations:
   ```elisp
   ;; In corpus-parse-for-loop.el or test-corpus-file-operations.el
   (:id "loop-context-001"
    :command "for file in $(find . -name '*.el'); do rm $file; done"
    :note "Loop variable bound to find pattern"
    :expect-ops ((:file "." :operation :read-directory :command "find")
                 (:file "*.el" :operation :match-pattern :command "find" :from-substitution t)
                 (:file "*.el" :operation :delete :command "rm" :loop-context t :loop-variable "file")))
   ```

Design rationale: Loops are critical for understanding batch file operations. When we see `for file in $(find . -name "*.txt"); do rm $file; done`, we need to understand that:
1. find produces the pattern *.txt
2. The loop variable `file` is bound to this pattern
3. rm operates on *.txt (via the loop variable)

By tracking loop variable bindings in the variable context, we can resolve `$file` to the actual pattern when analyzing the loop body. The :loop-context flag distinguishes operations that happen in loops (potentially multiple times) from direct operations.

This enables accurate analysis of common patterns like "find all files and process them in a loop".

Verification:
- Run: make test && make test TEST=test-loop-context
- Test: Parse "for file in \$(find . -name '*.txt'); do rm \$file; done"
- Verify: rm operation shows file "*.txt" resolved from loop variable
- Verify: Operation has :loop-context t and :loop-variable "file"
- Test: Parse loop with glob "for file in *.log; do cat \$file; done"
- Verify: cat operation shows file "*.log" resolved from loop variable

title: Tool error responses use snake_case keys instead of consistent format
description:
In scope-core.el, jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error returns error plists with snake_case keys, inconsistent with internal kebab-case convention.

The issue:
- Lines 766-776 in scope-core.el return error plist with:
  :allowed_patterns
  :deny_patterns
- These are snake_case, not kebab-case
- Used in JSON responses to LLM, so may need snake_case for API compatibility
- But inconsistent with internal elisp convention

Context:
- These keys go into JSON responses sent to gptel
- JSON convention is snake_case
- But elisp convention is kebab-case

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (lines 771-772)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-org-roam-tools.el (line 185 uses :allowed_patterns)
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el (lines 99, 108 use :required_scope)

Decision needed:
1. Is this intentional for JSON API compatibility?
2. Should we normalize at JSON serialization boundary instead?
3. Document the convention: elisp-internal = kebab, JSON-external = snake

Recommendation:
- Keep internal keys kebab-case throughout elisp
- Convert to snake_case only at JSON serialization boundary
- Requires checking how gptel serializes plists to JSON

title: scope-profiles.el plist-to-yaml doesn't handle bash_tools nested structure
description:
The jf/gptel-scope-profile--plist-to-yaml function in scope-profiles.el only handles simple nested plists (2 levels deep). The bash_tools section requires 4 levels of nesting (bash_tools -> categories -> read_only/safe_write/dangerous -> commands).

Current behavior:
- Only handles generic nested plists recursively
- Works for paths, org_roam_patterns, shell_commands (2-3 levels)
- Fails for bash_tools which has categories with nested command lists

Expected behavior:
- Should serialize bash_tools structure correctly:
  bash_tools:
    categories:
      read_only:
        commands: ["ls", "cat"]
      safe_write:
        commands: ["mkdir"]
    deny: ["rm"]

The scope-expansion.el module has a comprehensive jf/gptel-scope--write-yaml-plist function (lines 353-378) that properly handles bash_tools. The scope-profiles.el module needs similar logic.

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope-profiles.org (lines 208-239)
- config/gptel/scope-profiles.el (generated)

Impact: HIGH - When scope profiles are written to scope.yml, bash_tools structure will be incorrectly formatted or incomplete.

title: Final corpus validation and refinement check
description:
After all implementation is complete, review corpus files again to ensure they comprehensively test the implemented features and add any missing edge cases.

Files to review:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-command-substitution.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-combined-patterns.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-conditional.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-for-loop.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-heredoc.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-file-operations.el

Implementation steps:
1. Run comprehensive corpus validation:
   ```bash
   # Run all tests
   make test-bash-parser
   
   # Validate corpus file ops
   emacs --batch -l config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.el \
         -f jf/test-corpus-file-ops-validate
   
   # Check test coverage
   emacs --batch -l config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.el \
         --eval "(progn (require 'ert) (ert-run-tests-batch-and-exit))"
   ```

2. Review test coverage for each feature:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/test-feature-coverage-report ()
     "Generate coverage report for bash-parser features."
     (interactive)
     (let ((features '((:recursive-analysis "Recursive substitution parsing")
                      (:pattern-flow "Pattern flow tracking")
                      (:loop-context "Loop variable resolution")
                      (:conditional-context "Conditional branch tracking")
                      (:heredoc-context "Heredoc context disambiguation")))
           (corpus-files '(corpus-parse-command-substitution
                          corpus-parse-combined-patterns
                          corpus-parse-conditional
                          corpus-parse-for-loop
                          corpus-parse-heredoc))
           (coverage-stats nil))
       
       ;; Count test cases for each feature
       (dolist (feature features)
         (let ((feature-key (car feature))
               (feature-name (cadr feature))
               (test-count 0))
           ;; Search corpus for tests with this feature
           (dolist (corpus-file corpus-files)
             (require corpus-file)
             (let ((corpus-var (intern (concat "jf/bash-" 
                                              (symbol-name corpus-file) 
                                              "-corpus"))))
               (when (boundp corpus-var)
                 (dolist (test-case (symbol-value corpus-var))
                   (when (plist-get test-case feature-key)
                     (setq test-count (1+ test-count)))))))
           (push (list feature-name test-count) coverage-stats)))
       
       ;; Display report
       (with-current-buffer (get-buffer-create "*coverage-report*")
         (erase-buffer)
         (insert "Feature Coverage Report\n")
         (insert "=======================\n\n")
         (dolist (stat coverage-stats)
           (insert (format "%-40s %3d tests\n" (car stat) (cadr stat))))
         (display-buffer (current-buffer)))))
   ```

3. Check for missing edge cases in each category:
   
   **Command Substitutions:**
   - Triple nesting: `$(cmd1 $(cmd2 $(cmd3)))`
   - Multiple substitutions in single arg: `"$(date)-$(hostname).log"`
   - Substitution in redirect target with pattern: `> log-$(date).txt`
   - Empty substitution: `$()`
   
   **Combined Patterns:**
   - Loop + conditional + substitution (all three)
   - Xargs with substitution: `find $(pwd) -name '*.txt' | xargs rm`
   - Find -exec with substitution in command
   
   **Conditionals:**
   - Multiple file tests: `if [ -f file1 ] && [ -d dir1 ]; then`
   - Command-based test with substitution: `if grep -q $(whoami) file; then`
   - Elif chains with different operations in each branch
   
   **For-Loops:**
   - Nested loops with different patterns
   - Loop with break/continue affecting operations
   - Loop with parameter expansion: `${var%suffix}`
   
   **Heredocs:**
   - Multiple heredocs in chain: `cat <<EOF1 ... EOF1 && cat <<EOF2 ... EOF2`
   - Heredoc with redirect and embedded substitution
   - Heredoc to database command (mysql, psql)

4. Add missing edge case tests if identified:
   ```elisp
   ;; Example: Add triple nesting test if missing
   (:id "cmdsub-edge-triple-nesting"
    :command "cat $(dirname $(dirname $(which emacs)))"
    :expect-file-ops (;; which reads PATH to find emacs
                      ;; dirname processes paths (no file op)
                      ;; cat reads final directory (but dirname output is path, not file)
                      ;; May have zero file ops or read PATH
                      )
    :notes "Triple nesting - extreme but valid")
   ```

5. Verify error handling for malformed commands:
   ```elisp
   (ert-deftest test-error-handling-malformed ()
     "Test parser handles malformed commands gracefully."
     (should-not (jf/bash-extract-file-operations 
                  (jf/bash-parse "cat $(")))  ; Unclosed substitution
     (should-not (jf/bash-extract-file-operations
                  (jf/bash-parse "for file in; do rm $file; done"))))  ; Empty loop list
   ```

6. Document any limitations or known issues:
   ```elisp
   ;; Add to bash-parser.el or README.md
   ;;
   ;; Known Limitations:
   ;; 1. Arithmetic expansion $((expr)) not analyzed for file operations
   ;; 2. Brace expansion {a,b,c} treated as literal, not expanded
   ;; 3. Process substitution <() handled but not deeply analyzed
   ;; 4. Indirect variable expansion ${!var} not resolved
   ;; 5. Maximum nesting depth of 10 levels
   ```

7. Create final validation checklist:
   ```markdown
   # Validation Checklist
   
   ## Core Features
   - [ ] Simple commands (cat, rm, cp) extract operations correctly
   - [ ] Redirections (>, >>, <, 2>) create write/read operations
   - [ ] Pipelines process all commands in chain
   - [ ] Command chains track variable assignments
   
   ## Recursive Features
   - [ ] Single-level substitutions extract nested operations
   - [ ] Double-nested substitutions work (e.g., dirname $(which cmd))
   - [ ] Triple-nested substitutions work
   - [ ] Substitutions in positional args
   - [ ] Substitutions in redirect targets
   - [ ] Substitutions in loop lists
   
   ## Pattern Flow
   - [ ] find pattern flows to outer command
   - [ ] ls glob pattern flows to outer command
   - [ ] grep -l pattern flows to outer command
   - [ ] Pattern source is tracked correctly
   - [ ] Multiple patterns in single command
   
   ## Loop Context
   - [ ] Loop variable resolves to literal list
   - [ ] Loop variable resolves to glob pattern
   - [ ] Loop variable resolves to substitution pattern
   - [ ] Operations in loop body have :loop-context flag
   - [ ] Nested loops work correctly
   
   ## Conditional Context
   - [ ] File test operators extract :read-metadata operations
   - [ ] Then branch operations marked with :conditional :branch :then
   - [ ] Else branch operations marked with :conditional :branch :else
   - [ ] Elif branches handled correctly
   - [ ] Command-based tests (grep -q) extract operations
   
   ## Heredoc Context
   - [ ] Heredoc with redirect creates file
   - [ ] Heredoc to command (python, mysql) does not create file
   - [ ] Heredoc in git commit does not create file
   - [ ] Heredoc piped to command does not create file
   - [ ] Embedded substitutions in heredoc analyzed
   
   ## Error Handling
   - [ ] Malformed commands don't crash parser
   - [ ] Unclosed substitutions handled gracefully
   - [ ] Empty structures (loop list, heredoc) handled
   - [ ] Maximum depth limit prevents infinite recursion
   
   ## Backward Compatibility
   - [ ] All existing test-corpus-file-operations tests pass
   - [ ] Simple commands return expected operations
   - [ ] Existing code doesn't break with new context flags
   ```

8. Generate final test report:
   ```bash
   # Run all tests and capture output
   make test-bash-parser 2>&1 | tee test-report.txt
   
   # Count passes/failures
   grep -c "passed" test-report.txt
   grep -c "failed" test-report.txt
   
   # Generate coverage report
   emacs --batch -l config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.el \
         -f jf/test-feature-coverage-report
   ```

Design rationale: After implementing all features, this final validation pass ensures:
1. No gaps in test coverage
2. Edge cases are handled
3. Error conditions don't crash
4. Documentation reflects reality
5. Backward compatibility is maintained

This is a quality gate before considering the work complete. Any issues found here should be addressed before closing all beads.

Verification:
- Run: All tests pass
- Review: Coverage report shows good distribution across features
- Check: All validation checklist items checked
- Verify: No critical edge cases missing
- Confirm: Error handling works for malformed input
- Review: Documentation is accurate and complete
- Decision: Add any missing tests as follow-up beads if needed
