3. Update jf/gptel-scope-profile--load in scope-profiles.org to parse bash_tools section (nested :categories structure)
4. Update jf/gptel-scope-profile--create-scope-file to write bash_tools to session scope.yml
5. Tangle scope-profiles.org and validate with ./bin/tangle-org.sh

Design rationale: bash_tools config lives in scope profiles (not presets) because scope is mutable per-session while presets are immutable. Category-based grouping (read_only/safe_write/dangerous) makes the security model semantic and intuitive - read commands naturally require read paths, write commands require write paths. Configuration is concise and categories evolve independently.

YAML structure:
bash_tools:
  categories:
    read_only:
      commands: ["ls", "grep", "find", ...]
    safe_write:
      commands: ["mkdir", "touch", "echo", ...]
    dangerous:
      commands: []
  deny:
    - "rm"
    - "sudo"

Verification:
- Profile YAML parses without errors
- bash_tools section has correct nested structure (categories.read_only, categories.safe_write, categories.dangerous, deny)
- Session creation from profile writes bash_tools to scope.yml
- Existing profiles (coding.yml, research.yml, restricted.yml) load correctly

Context: design.md § Alignment with Preset System, § Implementation Plan Step 1

title: Update agent definitions to use run_bash_command
description:
Update agent .md files to reference run_bash_command instead of run_approved_command.

**Context:**
Agent definitions in config/gptel/agents/ document available tools and provide usage examples. Removing run_approved_command requires updating references to new run_bash_command tool.

**Files to modify:**
- config/gptel/agents/*.md (any files referencing run_approved_command)

**Implementation steps:**

1. Search for run_approved_command references:

```bash
grep -r "run_approved_command" config/gptel/agents/
```

2. For each agent file, replace tool references:

Old pattern:
```markdown
- `run_approved_command`: Execute shell commands from approved list
  Example: run_approved_command('ls -la')
```

New pattern:
```markdown
- `run_bash_command`: Execute shell command in specified directory with scope validation
  Example: run_bash_command('ls -la', '/Users/jefffarr/emacs')
  Example: run_bash_command('grep -r TODO . | head -20', '/Users/jefffarr/projects/myapp')
```

3. Update tool descriptions to explain:
   - Explicit directory argument required
   - Commands categorized (read_only, safe_write, dangerous)
   - Directory must be in scope for command category
   - Shell composition allowed (pipes, redirects)

4. Add examples showing common patterns:

```markdown
Common bash command patterns:

Explore codebase:
  run_bash_command('find . -name "*.el" -type f', '/Users/jefffarr/emacs/config')
  run_bash_command('grep -r "defun jf/" . | wc -l', '/Users/jefffarr/emacs')

Check git status:
  run_bash_command('git status --short', '/Users/jefffarr/emacs')
  run_bash_command('git log --oneline -10', '/Users/jefffarr/emacs')

Create temporary files:
  run_bash_command('mkdir -p test-output', '/tmp')
  run_bash_command('echo "test data" > test.txt', '/tmp/test-output')
```

5. Document scope expansion flow:

```markdown
If command not in allowed list or directory not in scope, use request_scope_expansion:

  request_scope_expansion(
    tool_name="run_bash_command",
    patterns=["rm"],  # command to add, or directory pattern
    justification="Need to remove temporary build artifacts"
  )
```

**Agent files likely affected:**
- claude-explore.md (uses shell commands for codebase exploration)
- claude-coding.md (uses git commands)
- Any agent with shell_commands in preset

**Design rationale:**
Agent definitions serve as LLM instructions. Clear examples teach proper tool usage. Documenting scope expansion flow reduces friction when LLM encounters permission errors.

**Verification:**
- All references to run_approved_command removed
- Agent files render correctly (valid markdown)
- Examples demonstrate both read_only and safe_write commands
- Scope expansion flow documented

**Acceptance criteria:**
- No grep matches for "run_approved_command" in config/gptel/agents/
- All agent files updated with run_bash_command examples
- Examples show explicit directory arguments
- Scope expansion flow documented in at least one agent file

title: Review key normalization consistency across modules
description:
The codebase uses kebab-case for elisp (bash-tools) but YAML uses snake_case (bash_tools).

Review all modules to ensure:
- YAML parsing normalizes snake_case → kebab-case consistently
- All plist access uses kebab-case keys (:bash-tools :read-only :safe-write)
- YAML serialization converts back to snake_case for scope.yml
- No mixed usage of snake_case and kebab-case in elisp code
- Nested structure normalization is correct (categories.read_only → :categories :read-only)
- Vector to list conversion happens consistently (jf/gptel-scope--vectorp-to-list)

This is related to the two P0 CRITICAL beads (emacs-ags, emacs-1n7) but broader in scope.

title: Remove bash_tools fallback in scope-expansion line 286-287
description:
The code at config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el lines 286-287 contains backwards-compatibility fallback:

```elisp
(bash-tools (or (plist-get parsed :bash-tools)
                (plist-get parsed :bash_tools)
                (list)))
```

This fallback checks both hyphenated (:bash-tools) and underscored (:bash_tools) formats. However, since normalization was added in commit 425b6c5, all YAML parsing should go through jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys.

The issue is that scope-expansion.el does its own YAML parsing (line 279: yaml-parse-string) instead of using the centralized jf/gptel-scope--parse-scope-yml function which includes normalization.

Resolution options:
1. Remove fallback and apply normalization to locally-parsed YAML
2. Refactor to use centralized parsing function
3. Keep fallback as defensive programming (document as intentional, not migration)

Context: Function jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope in config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el

title: Verify scope-core normalization handles deeply-nested bash_tools structure
description:
The jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys function in scope-core recursively normalizes keys, but needs verification that it properly handles the 4-level nesting in bash_tools.

Structure to normalize:
Level 1: :bash_tools (top-level key)
Level 2: :categories, :deny (under bash_tools)
Level 3: :read_only, :safe_write, :dangerous (under categories)
Level 4: :commands (under each category)

Current implementation (scope-core.org lines 288-308):
- Recursively processes nested plists
- Checks (keywordp (car value)) to detect nested plists
- Should handle arbitrary depth

Verification needed:
- Test that parsing bash-enabled.yml produces correct elisp structure
- Verify :bash-tools -> :categories -> :read-only -> :commands
- Check that values (command lists) aren't incorrectly processed as plists

Test approach:
- Load bash-enabled.yml with jf/gptel-scope--load-config
- Inspect resulting plist structure
- Verify (plist-get (plist-get (plist-get config :bash-tools) :categories) :read-only) returns expected plist

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org (lines 288-308)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (generated)

Impact: HIGH - If normalization fails, bash command validation won't work
notes: VERIFIED: scope-core.el lines 244-246 recursively normalize nested plists by checking if (keywordp (car value)). This correctly handles 4-level bash_tools.categories.read_only.commands structure.

title: Extend preset-registration to extract bash_tools
description:
Extend scope key extraction to recognize and extract bash_tools from preset files.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/preset-registration.org

Implementation steps:
1. Add :bash-tools to scope keys list in jf/gptel-preset--extract-scope-keys function
2. Update extraction logic to preserve nested structure:
   - Input: (:bash-tools (:categories (:read-only [...] :safe-write [...]) :deny [...]))
   - Preserve full nested plist structure during extraction
3. Store extracted bash_tools in jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults alist:
   (preset-name . (:bash-tools (:categories (:read-only [...] :safe-write [...]) :deny [...])))
4. Ensure bash_tools is stripped from preset plist before calling gptel-make-preset
5. Handle key normalization: snake_case YAML keys → kebab-case elisp keys
   - bash_tools → :bash-tools
   - read_only → :read-only
   - safe_write → :safe-write
6. Tangle preset-registration.org and validate with ./bin/tangle-org.sh

Design rationale: Presets can define inline bash_tools (extracted to scope defaults during registration) but scope profiles take precedence during session creation. This separation maintains the preset-alignment architecture: presets remain immutable in gptel--known-presets while scope.yml is mutable per-session. Key normalization ensures consistency with elisp conventions.

Pattern example:
Before extraction (parsed preset plist):
(:name "executor" :model 'claude-opus-4-6 
 :bash-tools (:categories (:read-only ["ls" "grep"] :safe-write ["mkdir"]) :deny ["rm"]))

After extraction:
Preset plist (passed to gptel-make-preset):
(:name "executor" :model 'claude-opus-4-6)

Scope defaults alist:
(executor . (:bash-tools (:categories (:read-only ["ls" "grep"] :safe-write ["mkdir"]) :deny ["rm"])))

Verification:
- Preset file with bash_tools section registers successfully
- bash_tools extracted to jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults with correct structure
- Registered preset in gptel--known-presets does NOT contain :bash-tools key
- Nested categories structure preserved (not flattened)
- Key normalization works (snake_case → kebab-case)

Context: design.md § Alignment with Preset System, § Implementation Plan Step 2

title: VERIFICATION NEEDED: Recursive key normalization for bash_tools.categories
description:
## Critical Path

Data flow for bash_tools from preset to validator:

**Step 1: Preset File (bash-tools-example.md)**
```yaml
bash_tools:
  categories:
    read_only:      # SNAKE_CASE
      commands: ["ls"]
    safe_write:     # SNAKE_CASE
      commands: []
```

**Step 2: Preset Registration (preset-registration.el)**
- yaml-parse-string creates: :bash_tools (:categories (:read_only (:commands ...)))
- jf/gptel-preset--normalize-keys converts: :bash_tools → :bash-tools
- BUT: Does it recurse to convert :read_only → :read-only?

Code (line 60-77):
```elisp
(normalized-val (if (and (listp val)
                         (not (null val))
                         (keywordp (car val)))
                    (jf/gptel-preset--normalize-keys val)
                  val))
```

✓ YES - It DOES recurse on nested plists!

**Step 3: Scope Profile Write (scope-profiles.el)**
- Receives normalized plist: :bash-tools (:categories (:read-only ...))
- jf/gptel-scope-profile--kebab-to-snake converts: :read-only → :read_only
- Writes to scope.yml:
```yaml
bash_tools:
  categories:
    read_only:
      commands: ["ls"]
```

**Step 4: Scope Load (scope-core.el)**
- Reads scope.yml
- yaml-parse-string creates: :bash_tools (:categories (:read_only ...))
- jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys (line 222-242) converts ALL keys recursively

Code analysis:
```elisp
(normalized-value (if (and (listp value)
                          (keywordp (car value)))
                     (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys value)
                   value))
```

✓ YES - Recursion happens!

**Step 5: Validator Access (scope-core.el line 584-593)**
```elisp
(let ((read-only (plist-get categories :read-only))
      (safe-write (plist-get categories :safe-write)))
```

This expects :read-only (hyphenated), which should exist after normalization.

## Verification Test Needed

Create a test that:
1. Writes a preset with bash_tools using snake_case keys
2. Registers it
3. Creates a session
4. Verifies scope.yml has snake_case keys
5. Loads scope.yml
6. Verifies config plist has hyphenated keys
7. Calls validator
8. Verifies command is categorized correctly

## Risk Assessment

HIGH - If normalization doesn't recurse properly:
- Commands will ALWAYS be categorized as 'unknown
- All bash commands will be denied
- Feature will be completely broken

## Files
- config/gptel/preset-registration.el (jf/gptel-preset--normalize-keys)
- config/gptel/scope-profiles.el (jf/gptel-scope-profile--plist-to-yaml)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys)

title: Decide whether to keep pattern matching operations from find/grep/ls
description:
Parser now extracts :match-pattern operations from find/grep/ls commands, causing test failures where expectations don't include these operations. Need to decide if this is a feature to keep or a bug to fix.

**Current behavior:**
Command: find . -name '*.log' -exec rm {} \;

Previous (expected by tests): 2 operations
1. (:file "." :operation :read-directory)
2. (:file "{}" :operation :delete)

Current (actual): 3 operations
1. (:file "." :operation :read-directory)
2. (:file "*.log" :operation :match-pattern) ← NEW
3. (:file "{}" :operation :delete)

**Affected tests** (8 failures):
- test-corpus-find-001
- test-corpus-find-003
- test-corpus-glob-004
- test-corpus-integration-001 through -004
- test-find-pattern-metadata

**Decision needed:**

**Option A: Keep the feature (recommended)**
- Pattern matching operations provide useful semantic information
- Shows which patterns are being searched for
- Enables better analysis of file operation flows
- Action: Update all test expectations to include :match-pattern operations

**Option B: Remove the feature**
- Revert to previous behavior (no pattern operations)
- Tests pass as-is without modification
- Action: Modify extraction logic to skip pattern operations from find/grep/ls

**Files affected:**
If keeping feature:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-file-operations.el (update expectations)
- Add :match-pattern operations to all find/grep/ls test cases

If removing feature:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (modify extraction logic)
- Identify where pattern operations are added and disable them

**Design considerations:**

For Option A (keep):
- Consistency: All file operations should be captured, including pattern matching
- Completeness: Pattern operations show search intent, not just final file access
- Traceability: Easier to trace why certain files were operated on

For Option B (remove):
- Simplicity: Fewer operations to process and track
- Backward compatibility: Existing code expecting N operations won't break
- Noise reduction: Pattern matching may be an implementation detail

**Verification:**
After decision:
```bash
make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-corpus-find-'
make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-corpus-integration-'
```

**Recommendation:**
Option A (keep the feature). Pattern matching operations provide valuable semantic information about what the command is searching for. Update tests to reflect this richer operation model.
notes:
**Option A implementation - partial completion**

Successfully updated test expectations to include :match-pattern operations for:
- ✅ test-corpus-find-001: Added :match-pattern for '*.log' pattern
- ✅ test-corpus-glob-004: Added :match-pattern for brace expansion pattern  
- ⚠️ test-corpus-find-003: Added :match-pattern, but STILL FAILING due to separate exec block extraction bug

**Integration tests analysis:**
- integration-001 through -004 already HAD :match-pattern in their expectations
- These tests are failing due to DIFFERENT issues (loop/conditional body operations not being extracted)
- This is beyond the scope of the :match-pattern feature decision

**Additional bugs discovered:**
1. **Exec block extraction bug** (find-003): When find command has both -name pattern AND multiple exec blocks, the exec blocks are NOT extracted
2. **Loop/conditional context bug** (integration tests): Operations from loop bodies and conditional branches are not being extracted

**Conclusion:**
The :match-pattern feature is working as designed. Test expectations have been updated where applicable. Remaining failures are due to separate bugs that need individual investigation.


**Final status: Option A implemented successfully**

Test updates completed:
- ✅ test-corpus-find-001: PASSING (added :match-pattern)
- ✅ test-corpus-glob-004: PASSING (added :match-pattern)
- ⚠️ test-corpus-find-003: Updated expectations but still failing due to separate exec block bug → Created emacs-crh6

Integration tests (001-004) already had :match-pattern in expectations, failing for different reasons (loop/conditional extraction).

**Decision confirmed:** Keep the :match-pattern feature. It provides valuable semantic information about search intent.

title: Inconsistent kebab-case vs snake_case handling across modules
description:
The codebase has inconsistent handling of kebab-case vs snake_case:

1. YAML files use snake_case (org_roam_patterns, shell_commands, bash_tools)
2. Internal plists use kebab-case (:org-roam-patterns, :shell-commands, :bash-tools)
3. Conversion happens in jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys (scope-core.org lines 287-307)
4. Reverse conversion in jf/gptel-scope--kebab-to-snake (scope-expansion.org lines 398-401)

Issues found:

A. In scope-expansion.org line 199, documentation says:
   'YAML files use snake_case... When writing back, these updaters use the original snake_case format'
   
   BUT there's no verification that the conversion is bidirectional and lossless.

B. Tool category constant (scope-core.org lines 60-106) uses string tool names like 'run_bash_command' (snake_case) but validation type uses symbols like 'bash, 'path (no case convention).

C. Error messages return tool names as strings (snake_case) but internal code uses symbols for validation types.

D. In scope-expansion.org, all YAML writing uses jf/gptel-scope--kebab-to-snake but there's no round-trip test to ensure reading and writing produces identical YAML.

Recommendation: 
1. Document the case conversion contract clearly in scope-core.org
2. Add validation that kebab-to-snake is inverse of normalize-plist-keys
3. Consider adding a test that round-trips YAML through read->normalize->write->read
close_reason: Document round-trip property and assumptions for kebab-case/snake_case conversion

title: Verify scope.yml loading handles missing bash_tools gracefully
description:
## Issue

According to the spec (bash-tools/spec.md scenario "Missing bash tools section handled"), when scope.yml exists but has no bash_tools section, the system should use empty allow lists (deny all commands by default).

## Current Implementation

Check jf/gptel-scope--parse-scope-yml (lines 252-274 in scope-core.el):
- Returns `:bash-tools bash-tools` where bash-tools is the parsed value
- If section is missing, bash-tools will be nil

Check jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool (lines 562-567):
- Extracts `:bash-tools` from config
- Tries to access categories and deny from it
- If bash-tools is nil, this will fail

## Expected Behavior

When bash_tools is nil:
1. categories should default to empty structure: `(:read-only (:commands nil) :safe-write (:commands nil) :dangerous (:commands nil))`
2. deny should default to nil
3. All commands should be denied with "command_not_allowed" error

## Verification Steps

1. Check if nil handling exists in parse or validate function
2. Test with scope.yml that has no bash_tools section
3. Verify appropriate error is returned (not elisp error)

## Impact
HIGH - This is a required spec scenario. Missing bash_tools should deny all, not crash.

title: Decide: touch operation classification :create vs :create-or-modify
description:
## Problem

Two tests are failing because touch is classified as :create-or-modify but tests expect :create:

**test-corpus-write-001**: touch newfile.txt
- Expected: (:file "newfile.txt" :operation :create)
- Actual: (:file "newfile.txt" :operation :create-or-modify)

**test-corpus-chain-001**: rm temp.txt && touch new.txt  
- Expected: (:file "new.txt" :operation :create)
- Actual: (:file "new.txt" :operation :create-or-modify)

## Context

The touch command in Unix/Linux has dual semantics:
1. If file doesn't exist → create empty file
2. If file exists → update modification timestamp

So :create-or-modify is technically accurate.

## Resolution Options

**Option A: Update command semantics** (changes parser behavior)
- Change touch to return :create operation
- Pro: Matches test expectations, simpler classification
- Con: Loses semantic accuracy (touch does modify existing files)

**Option B: Update test expectations** (changes tests)  
- Update tests to expect :create-or-modify
- Pro: Semantically accurate, matches actual touch behavior
- Con: Less specific classification for security/sandbox use cases

**Option C: Context-aware classification**
- Return :create when file definitely doesn't exist (e.g., after rm in chain)
- Return :create-or-modify in ambiguous cases
- Pro: Best of both worlds
- Con: Complex, requires state tracking

## Recommendation

Option B (update tests) - :create-or-modify is semantically correct and provides more information for security analysis. A sandbox that allows :create should also handle :create-or-modify for touch.

Location to update: test/test-file-operations-corpus.el lines for write-001 and chain-001

title: Test error message structure and expansion guidance
description:
Implement behavioral tests for error message structure and scope expansion guidance (LLM-friendly errors).

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/run-bash-command-spec.el

Implementation steps:

1. Create describe block: 'run_bash_command tool - Error message structure'

2. Test: Command denied error includes command and position
   - Command: 'ls | xargs rm'
   - Mock: rm in deny list at position 2
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'command_denied'
     * :command 'rm'
     * :position 2
     * :message contains explanation

3. Test: Parse incomplete error includes parse errors
   - Command: 'function incomplete() {'
   - Mock: {:parse-complete nil :parse-errors 'Unexpected EOF'}
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'parse_incomplete'
     * :parse-errors contains 'EOF'
     * :message suggests fixing syntax

4. Test: Path out of scope error includes operation details
   - Command: 'python3 /workspace/script.py'
   - Mock: Execute operation outside scope
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'path_out_of_scope'
     * :path '/workspace/script.py'
     * :operation :execute
     * :required-scope 'paths.execute'
     * :allowed-patterns (empty list)

5. Test: Path denied error distinguishes from out-of-scope
   - Command: 'cat /etc/passwd'
   - Mock: Path matches deny pattern
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'path_denied'
     * :path '/etc/passwd'
     * :denied-patterns contains '/etc/**'
     * :message explains deny precedence

6. Test: Cloud auth denied includes provider details
   - Command: 'gcloud auth login'
   - Mock: GCP provider denied
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'cloud_auth_denied'
     * :provider 'gcp'
     * :allowed-providers ['aws']
     * :message suggests scope expansion

7. Test: Pipeline command denied includes full context
   - Command: 'find . | xargs rm | wc'
   - Mock: rm at position 2
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'command_denied'
     * :position 2
     * :full-pipeline 'find . | xargs rm | wc'
     * :message identifies pipeline bypass attempt

8. Test: Multiple operation failure reports first violation
   - Command: 'cp /workspace/src.txt /etc/dst.txt'
   - Mock: Second operation (write /etc) denied
   - Assert error structure:
     * :error 'path_denied' (or path_out_of_scope)
     * :path '/etc/dst.txt'
     * Reports first failure, not all failures

9. Test: Success response includes coverage metrics
   - Command: 'ls /workspace'
   - Mock: Successful execution
   - Mock coverage: {:ratio 0.95 :total-tokens 20 :claimed-tokens 19}
   - Assert response structure:
     * :success t
     * :output contains result
     * :exit_code 0
     * Optional :coverage with metrics

10. Test: Warning structure for cloud auth in warn mode
    - Command: 'aws-vault exec prod -- ls'
    - Mock: Cloud auth detected, warn mode
    - Assert response structure:
      * :success t
      * :warnings list contains cloud auth notice
      * :warnings item has :type, :provider, :message

Design rationale: Error messages must be structured and LLM-friendly to enable:
- Automatic scope expansion requests (LLM can parse error and call request_scope_expansion)
- User-readable explanations (messages explain WHY denial occurred)
- Debugging context (include relevant patterns, operations, positions)

Error structure follows consistent pattern:
- :error keyword (error type)
- Context fields specific to error type
- :message human-readable explanation
- :allowed-patterns or :denied-patterns for scope errors

This enables LLMs to:
1. Parse structured error
2. Identify what permission is missing
3. Call request_scope_expansion with appropriate justification
4. Present clear error to user if expansion denied

Test pattern: Mock various failure scenarios, verify error structure matches expected format with all required fields.

Verification:
- Run ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral
- All 10 tests pass
- Coverage: All error types, structured responses, LLM guidance

title: bash-parser-glob: Missing Package-Requires header
description:
bash-parser-glob.el is missing standard Emacs package headers (Author, Keywords, Package-Requires) present in other modules like bash-parser-protocol.el.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-glob.org

Current header (line 30-32):
```elisp
;;; bash-parser-glob.el --- Glob pattern matching for bash parser -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-
#+end_src
```

Should match protocol module format (lines 25-36):
```elisp
;;; bash-parser-glob.el --- Glob pattern matching for bash parser -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

;; Author: Jeff Farr
;; Keywords: bash, parser, glob, patterns
;; Package-Requires: ((emacs "27.1"))

;;; Commentary:

;; Glob pattern matching without filesystem access.
;; Provides string-based glob pattern matching for security validation.

;;; Code:
#+end_src
```

Design rationale:
Consistent package headers improve discoverability and follow Emacs packaging conventions. These headers are used by package management tools and documentation generators.

Verification:

- Check generated .el file has proper headers
- Run: checkdoc config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-glob.el

title: Create missing scope-expansion delta spec
description:
## Problem

The proposal and design documents mention scope-expansion as a modified capability, but there is NO delta spec for it.

From proposal.md:
- Line 39: "\`scope-expansion\`: Extends scope expansion to write approved bash commands to \`scope.yml\` bash_tools section."
- Line 46: "\`config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.{org,el}\` - Add bash command expansion"

Existing delta specs: bash-tools, scope (core), preset-registration, scope-profiles
Missing: scope-expansion

## Implementation Details

What was added to scope-expansion:
- New function jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope (line 333 in .org)
- Routing logic to call this function (line 127)
- YAML updates to bash_tools section
- Integration with existing add-to-scope menu flow

## Solution

Create: openspec/changes/scoped-bash-command-execution/specs/gptel/scope-expansion.md

Document as MODIFIED requirements:
1. Extension to scope expansion system for bash tools
2. How bash command additions flow to scope.yml
3. Routing from request_scope_expansion to add-bash function
4. YAML structure for bash_tools updates
5. Integration with transient menu for bash commands
6. Scenarios for: add read_only command, add safe_write command, handle dangerous commands

Include cross-references to:
- bash-tools/spec.md for bash validation behavior

title: Add variable context validation
description:
Add validation for variable context format to provide clear error messages.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (lines 41-57 in jf/bash--normalize-var-context)

Current problem:
The function assumes var-context is well-formed but does not validate structure. Malformed input causes cryptic errors deep in the call stack.

Problem cases:
- Non-cons-cell entries: (VAR value) instead of ((VAR . value))
- Nil keys or values: ((nil . value))
- Non-string/symbol keys: ((123 . value))
- Nested lists instead of alist

Implementation steps:
1. Add validation at function entry:
   - Check var-context is list or nil
   - Check each element is cons cell
   - Check car is symbol or string
   - Check cdr is string (value)

2. Provide helpful error messages:
   - Invalid entry at index N: (show entry)
   - Expected alist of (SYMBOL . STRING) or (STRING . STRING) pairs
   - Example of correct format

3. Options for handling errors:
   A) Signal error immediately (strict)
   B) Log warning and skip malformed entry (lenient)
   C) Add optional strict parameter

Recommended: Option A (strict) - fail fast with clear message

Example error message:
Invalid variable context at index 2: (HOME /path)
Expected cons cell (HOME . /path)
Variable context must be alist: ((VAR1 . value1) (VAR2 . value2))

Verification:
- Test with malformed context shows clear error
- Error message includes example of correct format
- Valid contexts still work correctly
- All existing tests pass

title: Security Checker - Core
description:
Implement main security validation function coordinating all checks.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "Security Validation - Main"
2. Implement `jf/bash-sandbox-check` public API function
3. Accept command-string, rules, and optional var-context
4. Parse command using jf/bash-parse
5. Extract file operations using jf/bash-extract-file-operations
6. For each operation:
   - Match against rules using jf/bash-match-rule
   - Check permissions using jf/bash-check-operation-permission
   - Collect violations
7. Separate unhandled operations (low confidence, unresolved vars)
8. Return result plist:
   - :allowed (t if no violations and no unhandled, nil otherwise)
   - :command (original command string)
   - :operations (all extracted operations)
   - :violations (list of violation plists)
   - :unhandled (operations that couldn't be validated)

Design rationale:
Single entry point for security validation simplifies integration. Parse → extract → validate pipeline is transparent to caller. Violations and unhandled operations separated for different user actions (violations = fix rules, unhandled = unsupported command). Fail-safe: any unhandled operations → :allowed nil.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-sandbox-check (command-string rules &optional var-context)
  "Validate COMMAND-STRING against RULES. Return validation result."
  (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse command-string))
         (operations (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed var-context))
         (violations nil)
         (unhandled nil))
    (dolist (op operations)
      (cond
       ;; Low confidence or unresolved = unhandled
       ((or (eq (plist-get op :confidence) :low)
            (plist-get op :unresolved))
        (push op unhandled))
       ;; Normal validation
       (t
        (let* ((rule (jf/bash-match-rule (plist-get op :file) rules))
               (violation (jf/bash-check-operation-permission op rule)))
          (when violation
            (push violation violations))))))
    (list :allowed (and (null violations) (null unhandled))
          :command command-string
          :operations operations
          :violations (nreverse violations)
          :unhandled (nreverse unhandled))))
```

Verification:
- Allowed command → :allowed t, no violations, no unhandled
- Denied command → :allowed nil, violations list with details
- Command with unhandled operations → :allowed nil, unhandled list
- Test all security checking scenarios from bash-sandbox-security/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 4: Security Checker Design"
close_reason: Implemented security checker core with permission checking, cd detection, variable resolution checks, and comprehensive validation pipeline

title: Standardize bash-parser internal function naming conventions
description:
Establish and apply consistent naming conventions for internal bash-parser functions.

Currently function names mix conventions: some use -- for internal, others don't. This creates confusion about what's public API vs internal.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (and .el)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-extensions.org (and .el)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (and .el)

Naming convention to establish:

Public API (external callers):
- jf/bash-FUNCTION-NAME
- Examples: jf/bash-parse, jf/bash-extract-file-operations, jf/bash-glob-match-p

Internal helpers (within bash-parser modules):
- jf/bash--FUNCTION-NAME (double dash)
- Examples: jf/bash--extract-ops-from-redirect-list, jf/bash--has-glob-pattern-p

Module-private (only used within specific module):
- jf/bash-MODULE--FUNCTION-NAME (module name before --)
- Examples: jf/bash-parse--internal, jf/bash-glob--segment-to-regex

Functions needing rename:

1. jf/bash-parse--internal (bash-parser-core.el:83)
   Issue: Module-private to parse, not general internal
   Current name is correct ✓

2. jf/bash-parse-nested-command (bash-parser-extensions.el:343)
   Issue: Lacks -- but is internal helper
   Rename to: jf/bash--parse-nested-command
   Used by: jf/bash--extract-from-single-command

3. jf/bash-track-assignments (bash-parser-variables.el:565)
   Issue: Lacks -- but is internal helper
   Rename to: jf/bash--track-assignments
   Used by: recursive analyzer (possibly public? review usage)

4. jf/bash-resolve-pwd-substitution (bash-parser-variables.el:355)
   Issue: May be public API or internal
   Decision needed: If only called by jf/bash--resolve-path-variables, rename
   Rename to: jf/bash--resolve-pwd-substitution

5. jf/bash-resolve-command-substitution (bash-parser-variables.el:411)
   Issue: Same as above
   Rename to: jf/bash--resolve-command-substitution

Implementation steps:
1. Audit all function names and their call sites
2. Categorize as public API vs internal
3. Rename internal functions to use -- prefix
4. Update all call sites (grep for old names)
5. Update docstrings to reflect new names
6. Tangle and test after each module

Rename checklist per function:
```bash
# 1. Find all call sites
cd /Users/jefffarr/emacs/config/experiments/bash-parser
grep -r "jf/bash-parse-nested-command" *.el

# 2. Rename in .org file (source of truth)
# Edit bash-parser-extensions.org
# Change defun and all docstring references

# 3. Tangle to .el
./bin/tangle-org.sh bash-parser-extensions.org

# 4. Update all callers
grep -r "jf/bash-parse-nested-command" *.org
# Edit each file, change to jf/bash--parse-nested-command

# 5. Tangle all modified modules
./bin/tangle-org.sh bash-parser-file-ops.org

# 6. Test
make test
```

Design rationale:
- Clear distinction between public and internal
- Prevents accidental external usage of internal functions
- Standard Emacs Lisp convention (-- for internal)
- Makes API surface clearer
- Easier to grep for public vs internal

Public API functions (should NOT have --):
- jf/bash-parse - main entry point
- jf/bash-extract-file-operations - main extraction
- jf/bash-glob-match-p - glob matching
- jf/bash-sandbox-check - security validation
- jf/bash-resolve-variables - variable resolution (might be internal?)
- jf/bash-detect-variable-references - detection (might be internal?)

Decision criteria for public vs internal:
- Public: Documented in module introduction, used by other packages
- Internal: Only used within bash-parser modules, implementation detail

Verification:
- No test failures (names are internal implementation)
- Search for old names returns no matches
- Public API documented in module headers
- Internal functions have -- in name
close_reason: Implemented via parallel orchestrator - standardized naming conventions

title: Implement command substitution extraction for bash parser
description:
Implement command substitution extraction to populate :command-substitutions field in parsed command structures.

CONTEXT:
30 new command substitution tests are failing because the parser doesn't extract  and backtick command substitutions. The test corpus in corpus-parse-command-substitution.el defines expected behavior with 30 test cases covering simple, nested, quoted, piped, and edge-case patterns.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (or .org)
- Add new extraction function or extend existing parser

Implementation approach:

1. Add command substitution extraction to bash-parser-core
   - Extract both $(cmd) and `cmd` syntax
   - Track nesting levels for nested substitutions
   - Preserve content without executing

2. Return structure format (from test corpus):
   :command-substitutions ((:syntax "" :content "pwd" :nesting-level 1)
                          (:syntax "" :content "which openspec" :nesting-level 2))

3. Handle all substitution contexts:
   - In positional arguments: echo $(pwd)
   - In variable assignments: dir=$(pwd)
   - Inside double quotes: "Current: $(date)"
   - Nested: $(dirname $(which cmd))
   - Multiple in one command: echo $(pwd) $(date)
   - Backtick legacy syntax: `pwd`
   - With pipes inside: $(ls | wc -l)

4. Nesting level detection:
   - Level 1: Outermost substitution
   - Level 2+: Each nested layer increments
   - Example: $(dirname $(pwd)) has two entries with levels 1 and 2

Design rationale:
Command substitution is critical for security analysis - these nested commands execute with full privileges. The :command-substitutions field enables tools to understand what commands will run and in what context.

The tree-sitter bash grammar already parses command_substitution nodes, so this implementation should traverse the tree-sitter AST to find these nodes and extract their content.

Test coverage:
Run: ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/experiments/bash-parser/test -p '^test-cmdsub-'
Expected: 30 tests should pass after implementation

Verification:
- All 30 test-cmdsub-* tests pass
- Handles both $() and backtick syntax
- Correctly identifies nesting levels
- Preserves content exactly as written

title: Fix void-variable bug in bash-parser variable assignment handling
description:
## Problem
Tests are failing with `(void-variable DIR=/tmp)` error when parsing commands with variable assignments like `A=/foo && B=$A/bar && cat $B/file.txt`.

## Symptoms
- Error occurs in 14+ tests involving variable assignments
- Affects: test-variable-assignment-and-usage-in-chain, test-variable-assignment-without-usage, test-variable-multiple-assignments, and related corpus tests
- Error message: `(void-variable DIR=/tmp)` suggests Emacs is trying to evaluate a symbol named `DIR=/tmp`

## Investigation Done
1. Added safety checks before all direct `intern()` calls to filter out strings containing `=`
2. Modified functions:
   - `jf/bash-lookup-command-semantics` (bash-parser-semantics.el)
   - `jf/bash--infer-operation-type` (bash-parser-file-ops.el)
   - Wrapper command lookup (bash-parser-core.el)
   - Dangerous pattern lookup (bash-parser-core.el)
   - Command injection pattern lookup (bash-parser-extensions.el)
3. Verified fixes are loaded and working for direct calls
4. Error persists, suggesting indirect evaluation path

## Next Steps
1. Add instrumentation to trace exact error location
2. Review tree-sitter parse output for assignment commands
3. Check for:
   - Macro expansion issues
   - Special forms (when-let, if-let) evaluation quirks
   - Indirect symbol evaluation (symbol-value, etc.)
   - Byte-compilation artifacts
4. Consider if tree-sitter is returning unexpected structure

## Context
- Failing tests: 84 out of 467 (18%)
- Most failures related to this bug
- Documented in config/experiments/bash-parser/test/VALIDATION-REPORT.md
- Code changes in commit: "Add validation report and intern() safety checks"

## Acceptance Criteria
- All variable assignment tests pass
- No `void-variable` errors in test suite
- Variable resolution in chains works correctly (A=/foo && B=$A/bar)
close_reason: Fixed by simplifying docstrings. Root cause: complex s-expression examples in docstrings triggered unknown Emacs eval bug. Solution: keep .el docstrings minimal, defer to org docs.

title: Track and propagate directory context through command chains
description:
Integrate directory context tracking into chain command processing in recursive analyzer.

SECURITY CONTEXT: Command chains like 'cd /dir1 && cat file.txt && cd /dir2 && cat other.txt' must track directory changes sequentially. First cat uses /dir1, second cat uses /dir2. This is the core integration point that makes directory context tracking functional.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.org (source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (tangled output)

Current behavior (bash-parser-recursive.el lines 118-131):
  ;; Chain command processing - tracks var-context through chains
  (when (eq (plist-get parsed-command :type) :chain)
    (let ((chain-context var-context))
      (dolist (cmd (plist-get parsed-command :all-commands))
        ;; Extract and apply variable assignments
        (when (fboundp 'jf/bash--extract-assignments-from-command)
          (let ((assignments (jf/bash--extract-assignments-from-command cmd chain-context)))
            (when assignments
              (setq chain-context (append assignments chain-context)))))
        ;; Extract operations with updated context
        (let ((cmd-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                       cmd chain-context (1+ depth))))
          (setq operations (append operations cmd-ops))))))

Implementation:
1. Add current-pwd tracking alongside chain-context
   - Initialize from (alist-get 'PWD chain-context) or default
   - Track current-pwd through the loop
   
2. For each command in chain:
   a. Check if command is cd (using jf/bash--is-cd-command-p from emacs-paqh)
   b. If cd, extract target (using jf/bash--extract-cd-target from emacs-r57m)
   c. Update current-pwd to new directory
   d. Update PWD in chain-context to match current-pwd
   
3. Pass current-pwd to file operation extraction
   - Pass as additional parameter OR
   - Update PWD in chain-context before recursion
   
4. Handle PWD variable assignments
   - If command assigns PWD=value, treat like cd
   - Extract PWD assignment from chain-context updates
   - Update current-pwd to match

Modified code structure:
  (when (eq (plist-get parsed-command :type) :chain)
    (let ((chain-context var-context)
          (current-pwd (or (alist-get 'PWD var-context) "/")))  ; NEW
      (dolist (cmd (plist-get parsed-command :all-commands))
        ;; Extract variable assignments
        (when (fboundp 'jf/bash--extract-assignments-from-command)
          (let ((assignments (jf/bash--extract-assignments-from-command cmd chain-context)))
            (when assignments
              (setq chain-context (append assignments chain-context))
              ;; NEW: Check if PWD was assigned
              (when-let ((new-pwd (alist-get 'PWD assignments)))
                (setq current-pwd new-pwd)))))
        
        ;; NEW: Check for cd command
        (when (and (fboundp 'jf/bash--is-cd-command-p)
                   (jf/bash--is-cd-command-p cmd))
          (when-let ((new-pwd (jf/bash--extract-cd-target cmd chain-context current-pwd)))
            (setq current-pwd new-pwd)
            ;; Update PWD in context for subsequent commands
            (setq chain-context (cons (cons 'PWD current-pwd)
                                     (assq-delete-all 'PWD chain-context)))))
        
        ;; Extract operations with updated context (PWD now reflects current-pwd)
        (let ((cmd-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                       cmd chain-context (1+ depth))))
          (setq operations (append operations cmd-ops))))))

Design rationale: Leverage existing chain processing infrastructure. The chain processor already tracks var-context through command sequences. We add parallel tracking for current-pwd, updating it when cd commands or PWD assignments are detected. The updated PWD flows into chain-context and thus into file operation extraction.

Key insight: PWD in var-context should always match current-pwd. When current-pwd changes (via cd), update PWD in chain-context. This ensures file operation extraction uses the correct directory context.

Dependencies:
- Requires emacs-paqh (jf/bash--is-cd-command-p) to detect cd commands
- Requires emacs-r57m (jf/bash--extract-cd-target) to extract cd targets
- Requires emacs-lzau (enhanced path resolution) for file operations

Test coverage:
Once implemented, these tests should pass:
- test-cd-absolute-path-simple: cd /new/dir && cat file.txt
- test-cd-absolute-path-multiple-operations: cd /dir && cat file1.txt && grep pattern file2.txt
- test-cd-relative-path-subdir: cd subdir && cat file.txt
- test-cd-sequence-two-cds: cd /first && cd /second && cat file.txt
- test-cd-sequence-relative-after-absolute: cd /base && cd subdir && cat file.txt
- test-cd-with-operations-between: cd /dir1 && cat file1.txt && cd /dir2 && cat file2.txt
- test-cd-with-variable-absolute: cd $DIR && cat file.txt

Verification:
- Run test suite: make test-pattern PATTERN='test-directory-changing'
- Verify cd tracking works in chains
- Verify multiple cd commands sequence correctly
- Verify file operations use correct directory context
- Check that PWD in var-context stays synchronized with current-pwd
close_reason: Phase 1 (Basic cd tracking) implementation complete - all related tests now passing

title: Pattern producer classification tests failing
description:
Multiple pattern producer classification tests are failing:

Pattern producers (should detect as producers):
- test-pattern-producer-egrep: egrep command not recognized as pattern producer
- test-pattern-producer-fgrep: fgrep command not recognized as pattern producer
- test-pattern-producer-find: find command not recognized as pattern producer
- test-pattern-producer-git-ls-files-metadata: git ls-files not recognized as pattern producer
- test-pattern-producer-git-subcommand: generic git subcommands not recognized as pattern producers
- test-pattern-producer-grep-long-flag: grep with long flags not recognized as pattern producer
- test-pattern-producer-grep-with-flag: grep with flags not recognized as pattern producer
- test-pattern-producer-ls: ls command not recognized as pattern producer

NOT pattern producers (should NOT detect as producers):
- test-not-pattern-producer-cat: cat incorrectly identified as pattern producer
- test-not-pattern-producer-cp: cp incorrectly identified as pattern producer
- test-not-pattern-producer-mv: mv incorrectly identified as pattern producer
- test-not-pattern-producer-rm: rm incorrectly identified as pattern producer
- test-not-pattern-producer-unknown: unknown commands incorrectly identified as pattern producers

Pattern flow test:
- test-pattern-flow-cat-grep: Pipeline pattern flow not working correctly (cat file | grep pattern)

Files to investigate:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.el (pattern producer classification)
- Look for is-pattern-producer or similar logic

Implementation steps:
1. Define what makes a command a pattern producer:
   - Commands that output filenames/paths: ls, find, git ls-files, locate
   - Commands that filter patterns: grep, egrep, fgrep (when searching for filenames)
   - Commands that list: git diff --name-only, git status --porcelain
2. Define what is NOT a pattern producer:
   - File content readers: cat, head, tail, less
   - File operation commands: cp, mv, rm, mkdir
   - Unknown/generic commands
3. Implement classification logic with proper command detection
4. Handle command variations:
   - grep vs egrep vs fgrep
   - git with various subcommands
   - Commands with different flag formats
5. Implement pattern flow tracking for pipelines (cat | grep)

Design rationale: Pattern producers are commands whose output contains filenames that can be used by subsequent commands in a pipeline or loop. Distinguishing producers from non-producers is essential for:
- Tracking file access patterns in complex scripts
- Understanding which commands generate lists of files vs process file contents
- Proper scoped analysis in gptel

Verification:
- Run make test-bash-parser
- Confirm all test-pattern-producer-* tests pass
- Confirm all test-not-pattern-producer-* tests pass
- Confirm test-pattern-flow-cat-grep passes
- Test edge cases: commands with various flag formats, git subcommands

title: Add :pattern t metadata for glob patterns in file operations
description:
## Problem

Four corpus tests are failing because file operations with glob patterns are missing the :pattern t metadata:
- test-corpus-glob-001: rm *.txt
- test-corpus-glob-002: cat config/**/*.json
- test-corpus-glob-003: (similar glob pattern)
- test-corpus-git-002: git add src/*.el

Current behavior: Operations are detected correctly but metadata shows:
  (:file "*.txt" :operation :delete :confidence :high :source :positional-arg :command "rm")

Expected behavior:
  (:file "*.txt" :operation :delete :confidence :high :source :positional-arg :pattern t)

## Resolution

Add glob pattern detection to the file operation extraction logic:
1. Detect glob metacharacters (*, ?, [, ], {, }, **) in file paths
2. Add :pattern t metadata when patterns are detected
3. Consider using jf/bash--glob-to-regex for pattern validation

Location: Likely in config/experiments/bash-parser/file-operations.el or the main parser.

## Dependencies

Depends on: emacs-gaah (glob-to-regex function for pattern validation)

title: Add input validation to jf/bash-parse entry point
description:
Add argument type and emptiness validation to jf/bash-parse entry point.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (Main Entry Point section)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (after tangling)

Problem:
The jf/bash-parse entry point doesn't validate its command-string argument. Invalid inputs produce confusing errors from deep in tree-sitter:

Test cases that currently fail badly:
  (jf/bash-parse nil)        ;; Error from tree-sitter: not helpful
  (jf/bash-parse 42)         ;; Type error deep in tree-sitter
  (jf/bash-parse '(list))    ;; Type error
  (jf/bash-parse "")         ;; Parses but pointless

Better to fail fast with clear error message at API boundary.

Implementation steps:

1. Add validation at start of jf/bash-parse (after docstring, before condition-case):

  (defun jf/bash-parse (command-string)
    "Parse COMMAND-STRING using tree-sitter..."
    ;; Validate input
    (unless (stringp command-string)
      (signal 'wrong-type-argument 
              (list 'stringp command-string (type-of command-string))))
    (when (string-empty-p command-string)
      (return (list :success nil 
                    :error "Empty command string"
                    :type :empty)))
    ;; Original implementation follows
    (condition-case err
        ...))

2. Update docstring to document argument requirements:

Add after existing docstring text:

  COMMAND-STRING must be a non-empty string. Signals wrong-type-argument
  if COMMAND-STRING is not a string. Returns :success nil with :error
  for empty strings.

3. Add test cases for validation:

#+begin_src emacs-lisp :tangle test/bash-parser-core-test.el
(ert-deftest test-jf/bash-parse-validates-input-type ()
  "Ensure jf/bash-parse rejects non-string input."
  (should-error (jf/bash-parse nil) :type 'wrong-type-argument)
  (should-error (jf/bash-parse 42) :type 'wrong-type-argument)
  (should-error (jf/bash-parse '(list)) :type 'wrong-type-argument))

(ert-deftest test-jf/bash-parse-handles-empty-string ()
  "Ensure jf/bash-parse handles empty string gracefully."
  (let ((result (jf/bash-parse "")))
    (should (not (plist-get result :success)))
    (should (string-match-p "Empty" (plist-get result :error)))))
#+end_src

Design rationale:
Fail-fast principle: Catch invalid input at API boundary, not deep in implementation. This produces:
- Clearer error messages for users
- Easier debugging (error points to call site, not internals)
- Better stack traces
- Consistent error handling

Using signal for type errors is idiomatic Elisp:
- Matches built-in functions (nth, car, etc.)
- Emacs debugger recognizes wrong-type-argument
- Consistent with Elisp conventions

Returning structured error for empty strings:
- Not a type error (empty string is valid string)
- But semantically invalid for parsing
- Structured response matches existing error handling pattern

Alternative considered: Allow empty string, return empty parse
Rejected because: Empty command has no meaningful parse result. Better to be explicit.

Verification:
1. Run new test cases: make test-bash-parser
2. Verify tests pass
3. Test error messages in REPL:
   (jf/bash-parse nil)     ;; Should show clear type error
   (jf/bash-parse "")      ;; Should show clear empty string error
4. Verify existing tests still pass
5. Check that error handling doesn't break recursion depth tracking

Priority: P2 - Improves user experience but not critical for functionality.

title: Make path resolution pipeline atomic with error handling
description:
Add error handling to make path resolution pipeline atomic and traceable.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (lines 642-700 in jf/bash--resolve-path-variables)

Current problem:
The three-stage path resolution pipeline (variables → pwd → relative) is not atomic. If any stage fails or returns unexpected value, the function may return corrupted data. No error logging makes debugging resolution failures difficult.

Resolution pipeline stages:
1. Variable resolution (jf/bash-resolve-variables)
2. Command substitution (jf/bash-resolve-pwd-substitution)
3. Relative path resolution (jf/bash-resolve-relative-path)

Implementation steps:
1. Wrap each stage in condition-case for error handling:
   - Catch errors from resolution functions
   - Log error with context (which stage, input value)
   - Return partially resolved result with error metadata

2. Add optional debug/trace parameter:
   - When enabled, log each stage result
   - Show: input → stage → output
   - Use message or dedicated logging function

3. Validate intermediate results between stages:
   - Check result is string or plist with :path
   - Verify :unresolved list contains only strings
   - Catch corrupted data before passing to next stage

4. Add :resolution-error metadata when stage fails:
   - Include stage name and error message
   - Allow caller to handle resolution failures gracefully

Example trace output when enabled:
Stage 1 (variables): $WORKSPACE/file.txt → /workspace/file.txt
Stage 2 (pwd): /workspace/file.txt → /workspace/file.txt (no change)
Stage 3 (relative): /workspace/file.txt → /workspace/file.txt (no change)

Design rationale: Path resolution is critical for security validation. Silent failures or corrupted paths could bypass security checks. Explicit error handling and tracing makes debugging easier and improves reliability.

Verification:
- Add test that triggers resolution error
- Verify error is caught and logged
- Verify partial result is returned with error metadata
- All existing tests pass

title: Implement OLDPWD tracking for cd -
description:
Implement cd - (switch to previous directory) with OLDPWD tracking.

TESTS TO ENABLE (1 test):
- test-cd-with-dash: cd /first && cd /second && cd - && cat file.txt

SECURITY REQUIREMENT:
cd - switches to OLDPWD (previous working directory). Must track OLDPWD through directory changes to correctly resolve file paths.

FILES TO MODIFY:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.org (source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (tangled)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (cd target extraction)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Track OLDPWD in var-context
   - Before updating PWD, save current PWD to OLDPWD
   - Update OLDPWD in var-context alongside PWD
   - Maintain OLDPWD through command chains

2. Handle cd - in jf/bash--extract-cd-target
   - Detect target is "-"
   - Extract OLDPWD from var-context
   - Return OLDPWD as new directory

3. Update chain processor
   - When processing cd command:
     a. Save current-pwd to OLDPWD before updating
     b. Update current-pwd to new directory
     c. Update both PWD and OLDPWD in var-context

EXAMPLE:
  cd /first && cd /second && cd - && cat file.txt
  
  Sequence:
  1. cd /first: PWD=/first, OLDPWD=(initial)
  2. cd /second: PWD=/second, OLDPWD=/first
  3. cd -: PWD=/first (from OLDPWD), OLDPWD=/second
  4. cat file.txt: resolves in /first

EDGE CASES:
- cd - when OLDPWD not set: treat as :unresolved
- First cd in chain: OLDPWD is initial PWD

REFERENCE:
Test file: test-directory-changing-commands.el
See bash man page for OLDPWD semantics

VERIFICATION:
After implementation, test-cd-with-dash should pass.

title: Replace global depth counter with parameter passing in bash-parser-core
description:
Replace thread-unsafe global recursion depth counter with parameter passing.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (Recursion Depth Control section)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (after tangling)

Problem:
Current implementation uses global mutable state for recursion tracking:
- jf/bash--parse-depth (defvar at line 798)
- Incremented/decremented in jf/bash-parse (lines 34, 42)

Issues:
1. Not thread-safe: Multiple concurrent calls interfere
2. Non-reentrant: Debugger interruption can leave counter in bad state
3. Breaks with async.el or future Emacs concurrency
4. Hard to debug when it fails

Implementation steps:

1. Remove the global defvar jf/bash--parse-depth (line 798)

2. Modify jf/bash-parse entry point:
   (defun jf/bash-parse (command-string)
     "Parse COMMAND-STRING..."
     (jf/bash-parse--with-depth command-string 0))

3. Create new internal function:
   (defun jf/bash-parse--with-depth (command-string depth)
     "Internal parser with explicit DEPTH parameter."
     (when (> depth jf/bash--max-parse-depth)
       (error "Max parse depth exceeded: possible recursion cycle"))
     (condition-case err
         (jf/bash-parse--internal command-string depth)
       (error (list :success nil :error (error-message-string err)))))

4. Update jf/bash-parse--internal to accept depth parameter:
   (defun jf/bash-parse--internal (command-string depth)
     ...)

5. Update all recursive calls to jf/bash-parse to use jf/bash-parse--with-depth:
   - In jf/bash-parse--extract-command-substitutions (line 841)
   - In jf/bash-parse--handle-subshell (line 470)
   - In jf/bash-parse--parse-single-command-node (lines 899, 996)
   
   Change from:
     (jf/bash-parse content)
   To:
     (jf/bash-parse--with-depth content (1+ depth))

Design rationale:
Parameter passing makes depth tracking explicit and thread-safe. Each call gets its own depth value on the call stack, eliminating shared mutable state. This is the standard functional programming approach to recursion control.

The depth parameter flows naturally through the call graph:
- Entry point starts at depth 0
- Each recursive call increments depth
- No cleanup needed (call stack handles it)
- Thread-safe by design
- Debugger-safe (no global state to corrupt)

Verification:
1. Run existing tests: make test-bash-parser
2. Add new test for deep nesting:
   (should-error (jf/bash-parse (make-string 100 ?\())
                 :type 'error)
3. Test concurrent parsing (if using async.el):
   (async-start (lambda () (jf/bash-parse "ls"))
                (lambda (result) (message "Result: %S" result)))
4. Verify error messages unchanged
5. Verify performance unchanged (depth parameter is trivial overhead)

Priority: P1 - Thread safety is critical for robustness, even if current impact is low.

title: Refactor scope-expansion to use centralized YAML parsing with normalization
description:
Architectural issue: scope-expansion.el contains 4 functions that do their own YAML parsing instead of using the centralized jf/gptel-scope--parse-scope-yml function from scope-core.el.

The centralized function includes key normalization (underscore to hyphen conversion) via jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys, but the scope-expansion functions bypass this layer.

Current duplication:
- Line 160: jf/gptel-scope--add-path-to-scope
- Line 194: jf/gptel-scope--add-roam-pattern-to-scope  
- Line 237: jf/gptel-scope--add-shell-cmd-to-scope
- Line 279: jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope

This leads to inconsistent behavior:
- Some code paths normalize keys (scope-core)
- Some code paths use raw underscored keys (scope-expansion lines 200, 243)
- Some code paths have fallbacks for both formats (scope-expansion lines 286-287)

Resolution options:
1. Refactor expansion functions to call jf/gptel-scope--parse-scope-yml
2. Move normalization to a shared helper and call it in all parse paths
3. Keep duplication but ensure all paths normalize consistently

Child beads track specific instances:
- emacs-7rs: bash_tools fallback (line 286-287)
- emacs-2d7f: org_roam_patterns underscore (line 200)
- emacs-10ob: shell_commands underscore (line 243)

Related commits:
- 425b6c5: Added normalization to scope-core
- e69d9e6: Noted the dual-format issue but didn't fully resolve it

title: Fix text property name mismatch in commands-detection
description:
**Summary:** Change detection module to use 'gptel property instead of 'command property

**Problem:** Detection module uses `'command` property but spec requires `'gptel` property, breaking persistence integration.

**Files to modify:**
- config/gptel/commands/commands-detection.org
- config/gptel/commands/commands-detection.el

**Implementation:**

1. Update text property application in commands-detection.org (~line 75):

2. Update text property removal in commands-detection.org (~line 82):

title: No migration guide for preset.md to scope.yml bash_tools
description: CLAUDE.md line 159 states preset.md is being phased out in favor of scope.yml, but there is no user-facing documentation explaining: 1) Are inline bash_tools in preset.md frontmatter deprecated? 2) Should users migrate to scope-profiles/*.yml? 3) What is the migration process? 4) What happens if both preset.md and scope.yml have bash_tools? preset-registration.org documents extraction of bash_tools from preset.md but does not mention deprecation. scope-profiles.org implies scope.yml is preferred but does not state preset.md is deprecated. This creates confusion about the supported configuration path.

title: Create behavioral tests for read_file tool
description:
Implement comprehensive Buttercup tests for read_file scope expansion workflow, establishing testing patterns and infrastructure.

Files to create:
- config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral/filesystem-tools-scope-expansion-spec.el

Implementation steps:
1. Create spec file with Buttercup structure
2. Set up shared test infrastructure:
   - Reuse helpers-spec-setup-session, helpers-spec-teardown-session
   - Create helpers-spec-make-scope-with-paths for path-based scope
   - Create helpers-spec-mock-file-metadata for metadata mocking
   - Set up before-each/after-each blocks
3. Implement read_file test scenarios (~10 tests):
   - Path validation: read in read scope, read in write scope (hierarchical)
   - Expansion workflow: out of scope triggers expansion
   - Approval actions: add-to-scope updates paths.read, allow-once grants permission
   - Denial: user denies expansion
   - Errors: file not found
   - Allow-once lifecycle: permission consumed after use
4. Mock stateful operations:
   - Spy on jf/gptel-scope-prompt-expansion (expansion UI)
   - Mock file-exists-p, jf/gptel--file-is-git-tracked-p
   - Mock file read operations
5. Assert validation results, error structures, allow-once list state
6. Run tests: ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/gptel/tools/test/behavioral

Design rationale:
Buttercup framework chosen to match run_bash_command tests (BDD-style, describe/it/expect). Single spec file for all three tools reduces duplication of test infrastructure. Mock only stateful operations (file I/O, git, expansion UI) but test real validation logic. Behavioral tests map directly to spec scenarios, providing comprehensive coverage without needing separate unit tests.

Design pattern:
Reuse existing helpers-spec.el infrastructure. Create tool-specific helpers only when needed. Each test scenario is end-to-end (setup → execute → assert). Nested describe blocks organize by tool and feature category.

Verification:
- All read_file scenarios pass: ~10 tests green
- Expansion UI spy called correctly with violation details
- Allow-once list managed correctly (granted, consumed, cleared)
- File metadata included in validation calls
