This is a clean break - no backwards compatibility should exist.

title: Clean up code quality issues: duplicate defconst, dead code, hardcoded strings, key inconsistency
description:
## Context
Comprehensive code review found several code quality / maintenance issues across modules.

## Issues

### 1. Duplicate defconst jf/gptel-session--scope-file
- Defined in both `scope-core.el:14` AND `sessions/constants.el:54`
- scope-core already `(require 'gptel-session-constants)`, so the duplicate is redundant
- Fix: Remove from scope-core.org/.el

### 2. Dead code: jf/gptel-scope--vectorp-to-list
- `scope-core.el:630-643` — no callers since all yaml-parse-string calls now use `:sequence-type 'list`
- Fix: Remove function from scope-core.org/.el

### 3. Hardcoded strings that should use constants
- `scope-commands.el:90`: hardcoded `"metadata.yml"` — use `jf/gptel-session--metadata-file` or `jf/gptel--metadata-file-path`
- `metadata.el:25`: hardcoded `"scope-plan.yml"` — use `jf/gptel-session--scope-plan-file`
- `metadata.el:55`: hardcoded `"preset.md"` — consider adding constant or using inline string with comment

### 4. No-op key normalization in scope-core
- `scope-core.el:237`: `(or (plist-get parsed :paths) (plist-get parsed :paths))` — both branches identical (copy-paste artifact)
- Fix: Simplify to `(plist-get parsed :paths)`

### 5. Missing :true -> t coercion in preset-registration
- `preset-registration.el:85-101`: Handles `:false -> nil` but not `:true -> t`
- YAML `true` is parsed as `:true` keyword by yaml.el, which is truthy but not `t`
- Fix: Add `((eq val :true) t)` clause before the `:false -> nil` clause

### 6. Duplicate scope-core.el load in gptel.el
- `gptel.el:128` and `gptel.el:153` both load scope-core.el
- First load is correct (needed by tools), second is redundant
- Fix: Remove the second load, update comment on first

### 7. Key format inconsistency between scope-core and scope-expansion
- scope-core normalizes top-level keys to kebab-case (`:org-roam-patterns`)
- scope-expansion writes back with snake_case (`:org_roam_patterns`)
- Round-trip works because each module parses YAML independently, but creates maintenance trap
- Fix: Document the intentional divergence OR standardize approach

## Files
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.org
- config/gptel/scope/scope-commands.org
- config/gptel/sessions/metadata.org
- config/gptel/preset-registration.org
- config/gptel/gptel.org

## Discovered from
Comprehensive review of emacs-nli, emacs-cff, emacs-and, emacs-bfy, emacs-hed, emacs-yca

comments.author: Jeff Farr
comments.text: Discovered from comprehensive review of emacs-nli, emacs-cff, emacs-and, emacs-bfy, emacs-hed, emacs-yca

title: Create integration test checklist for end-to-end scenarios
description:
# Integration Test Checklist for Scoped Bash Command Execution

## Setup Tests

### Test: Profile with bash_tools configuration
- [ ] Create scope profile using bash-enabled.yml template
- [ ] Verify bash_tools section has categories (read_only, safe_write, dangerous)
- [ ] Verify bash_tools.deny list is populated
- [ ] Verify paths.read and paths.write are configured

### Test: Session creation with bash-enabled profile
- [ ] Create new gptel session with bash-enabled profile
- [ ] Verify scope.yml exists in session branch directory
- [ ] Verify bash_tools section appears in session scope.yml
- [ ] Verify tool categories are correct in tool registry

## Command Categorization Tests

### Test: Read-only command in read-scoped directory (should succeed)
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("ls -la", "/Users/jefffarr/emacs")
- [ ] Verify command executes successfully
- [ ] Verify output returned
- [ ] Verify exit_code is 0

### Test: Read-only command in write-scoped directory (should succeed)
- [ ] Ensure directory is ONLY in paths.write (not paths.read)
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("ls", write-only-directory)
- [ ] Verify command succeeds (write scope includes read capability)

### Test: Safe write command in write-scoped directory (should succeed)
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("mkdir test-dir", "/Users/jefffarr/emacs")
- [ ] Verify directory created
- [ ] Verify success returned

### Test: Safe write command in read-only directory (should fail)
- [ ] Ensure directory is ONLY in paths.read (not paths.write)
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("mkdir test", read-only-directory)
- [ ] Verify error: directory_not_in_scope
- [ ] Verify required_scope: "write"
- [ ] Verify allowed_patterns contains paths.write list

### Test: Denied command rejected immediately (should fail)
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("rm -rf test", "/tmp")
- [ ] Verify error: denied
- [ ] Verify message indicates command in deny list
- [ ] Verify NO scope expansion suggestion (deny is permanent)

### Test: Unknown command denied by default (should fail)
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("tree", "/Users/jefffarr/emacs")
- [ ] Verify error: command_not_allowed
- [ ] Verify command: "tree"
- [ ] Verify message suggests request_scope_expansion

## Shell Composition Tests

### Test: Piped command validated by base command
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("ls | grep .el", "/Users/jefffarr/emacs")
- [ ] Verify only "ls" is validated (not "grep")
- [ ] Verify command succeeds

### Test: Redirected output allowed
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("cat file.txt > output.txt", "/tmp")
- [ ] Verify "cat" validated as read_only
- [ ] Verify command succeeds

### Test: Command substitution allowed
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("echo $(pwd)", "/Users/jefffarr/emacs")
- [ ] Verify "echo" validated as safe_write
- [ ] Verify command succeeds

## Scope Expansion Flow Tests

### Test: LLM requests command expansion
- [ ] Receive command_not_allowed error for "tree"
- [ ] Call request_scope_expansion(tool_name="run_bash_command", patterns=["tree"], justification="...")
- [ ] Verify transient menu appears
- [ ] Verify menu shows tool name, resource, and justification

### Test: User approves command permanently
- [ ] In transient menu, select "Add to scope"
- [ ] Verify "tree" added to bash_tools.categories.read_only.commands in scope.yml
- [ ] Verify callback returns success: true, patterns_added: ["tree"]
- [ ] Retry run_bash_command("tree", directory)
- [ ] Verify command now succeeds

### Test: User approves command once
- [ ] Receive command_not_allowed for unknown command
- [ ] Request scope expansion, select "Allow once"
- [ ] Verify command added to allow-once list
- [ ] Retry command immediately
- [ ] Verify command succeeds
- [ ] Send another LLM response (new turn)
- [ ] Verify allow-once list cleared
- [ ] Retry same command
- [ ] Verify command fails again

### Test: LLM requests directory expansion
- [ ] Receive directory_not_in_scope error
- [ ] Call request_scope_expansion with directory pattern
- [ ] Select "Add to scope"
- [ ] Verify pattern added to paths.read or paths.write
- [ ] Retry command
- [ ] Verify command succeeds

### Test: User denies expansion
- [ ] Request scope expansion
- [ ] Select "Deny"
- [ ] Verify callback returns success: false, user_denied: true
- [ ] Verify scope.yml unchanged

## Security and Safety Tests

### Test: Timeout behavior
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("sleep 35", "/tmp")
- [ ] Verify command killed after 30 seconds
- [ ] Verify error indicates timeout
- [ ] Verify message suggests more specific filters

### Test: Output truncation
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("find . -name '*'", large-directory)
- [ ] If output > 10,000 chars, verify truncated: true
- [ ] Verify truncation notice in output
- [ ] Verify notice suggests filters (head, grep, tail)

### Test: Absolute path warning
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("grep -r TODO /Users/jefffarr/other-project", "/tmp")
- [ ] Verify warning in output about absolute paths
- [ ] Verify warning suggests using relative paths

### Test: Directory resolution with symlinks
- [ ] Create symlink to allowed directory
- [ ] Call run_bash_command("ls", symlink-path)
- [ ] Verify symlink resolved to real path
- [ ] Verify validation against real path

## Error Message Structure Tests

### Test: command_not_allowed error structure
- [ ] Trigger command_not_allowed error
- [ ] Verify error has: success: false
- [ ] Verify error has: error: "command_not_allowed"
- [ ] Verify error has: tool: "run_bash_command"
- [ ] Verify error has: resource OR command field
- [ ] Verify error has: message with expansion guidance

### Test: directory_not_in_scope error structure
- [ ] Trigger directory_not_in_scope error
- [ ] Verify error has: error: "directory_not_in_scope"
- [ ] Verify error has: directory field
- [ ] Verify error has: required_scope: "read" or "write"
- [ ] Verify error has: allowed_patterns list
- [ ] Verify error has: message with expansion guidance

### Test: dangerous_command error structure
- [ ] Add command to bash_tools.categories.dangerous
- [ ] Call run_bash_command with dangerous command
- [ ] Verify error: dangerous_command
- [ ] Verify message explains explicit approval needed

## Integration Points Tests

### Test: scope-core → scope-shell-tools
- [ ] Verify jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool called for bash validation
- [ ] Verify validator uses helper functions from scope-shell-tools
- [ ] Verify command parsing extracts base command correctly

### Test: scope-shell-tools → scope-expansion
- [ ] Verify request_scope_expansion infers validation type as 'bash'
- [ ] Verify violation-info passed to transient menu
- [ ] Verify callback mechanism works end-to-end

### Test: scope-expansion → scope.yml writer
- [ ] Verify jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope called
- [ ] Verify command added to correct category (read_only vs safe_write)
- [ ] Verify YAML key normalization (kebab-case to snake_case)
- [ ] Verify scope.yml remains valid YAML after update

## Regression Tests

### Test: Existing path-based tools unaffected
- [ ] Call read_file on allowed path
- [ ] Verify still works correctly
- [ ] Call write_file_in_scope on allowed path  
- [ ] Verify still works correctly

### Test: Configuration loading backwards compatible

title: Eliminate code duplication in bash-parser handler functions
description:
Eliminate 200+ lines of duplicated code between handler function pairs.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (after tangling)

Problem:
Two pairs of nearly-identical functions exist:

1. jf/bash-parse--handle-for-loop (lines 358-454, 97 lines)
   jf/bash-parse--handle-for-loop-node (lines 477-559, 83 lines)
   - 95% identical implementation
   - Only difference: first finds for-node, second receives it

2. jf/bash-parse--handle-conditional (lines 288-356, 69 lines)
   jf/bash-parse--handle-conditional-node (lines 561-614, 54 lines)
   - 95% identical implementation
   - Only difference: first finds if-node, second receives it

Total duplication: ~200 lines of nearly identical code

Implementation steps:

1. For-loop handlers:
   Simplify jf/bash-parse--handle-for-loop to call the -node variant:
   
   (defun jf/bash-parse--handle-for-loop (root-node)
     "Handle for-loop from ROOT-NODE."
     (let ((for-node (jf/bash-parse--find-node-by-type root-node "for_statement")))
       (if for-node
           (jf/bash-parse--handle-for-loop-node for-node)
         (list :success nil :error "No for_statement node found"))))
   
   Keep jf/bash-parse--handle-for-loop-node with the full implementation.
   Update docstring to note it works with node directly.

2. Conditional handlers:
   Same pattern - simplify jf/bash-parse--handle-conditional:
   
   (defun jf/bash-parse--handle-conditional (root-node)
     "Handle conditional from ROOT-NODE."
     (let ((if-node (jf/bash-parse--find-node-by-type root-node "if_statement")))
       (if if-node
           (jf/bash-parse--handle-conditional-node if-node)
         (list :success nil :error "No if_statement node found"))))
   
   Keep jf/bash-parse--handle-conditional-node with the full implementation.

3. Update docstrings:
   - Root-node handlers: "Find and parse [structure] from ROOT-NODE"
   - Node handlers: "Parse [structure] from [NODE] directly"

Design rationale:
The duplication exists because handlers are called in two contexts:
1. From jf/bash-parse--detect-structure-type (top-level structure)
2. From jf/bash-parse--parse-single-command-node (structure in chain)

The -node variants handle the actual parsing logic. The root-node variants just need to find the node and delegate. This is a classic extraction pattern that:
- Eliminates ~200 lines of duplicated code
- Makes maintenance easier (fix once, not twice)
- Clarifies the two call contexts
- Preserves existing API and behavior

Design pattern:
Follow the delegation pattern:
  Finder function (thin) -> Worker function (full implementation)

This is already used successfully for:
- jf/bash-parse -> jf/bash-parse--internal
- jf/bash-parse--parse-wrapper-command (delegates flag parsing)

Verification:
1. Run all tests: make test-bash-parser
2. Verify no test failures
3. Test for-loops in chains: "A && for x in 1; do echo $x; done"
4. Test conditionals in chains: "A && if true; then B; fi"
5. Check code coverage unchanged
6. Verify tangled .el file has reduced size

Priority: P2 - Improves maintainability but doesn't affect functionality.

title: Update validation function signatures to accept metadata
description:
Breaking change: Update all validation function signatures to accept metadata parameter for context-aware validation.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org (literate source)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (tangled output)

Implementation steps:
1. Update jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission signature
   - OLD: (config tool-name args)
   - NEW: (config tool-name args metadata)
   - Propagate metadata to specific validators
2. Update jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool signature
   - OLD: (tool-name args category config)
   - NEW: (tool-name args category config metadata)
   - Make metadata accessible in validation logic
3. Update jf/gptel-scope--validate-pattern-tool signature
   - OLD: (tool-name args config)
   - NEW: (tool-name args config metadata)
   - Accept metadata (will be nil for pattern tools)
4. Update jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool signature
   - OLD: (tool-name args config)
   - NEW: (tool-name args config metadata)
   - Accept metadata (will be nil for bash tools)
5. Update gptel-make-scoped-tool macro (ASYNC branch):
   - For path-based validation: gather metadata before calling check-tool-permission
   - For other validation types: pass nil for metadata
   - Code: (metadata (when (eq validation-type 'path) (jf/gptel-scope--gather-file-metadata (car normalized-args))))
6. Tangle and validate

Design rationale:
Immediate breaking change (not gradual migration) chosen because: (1) alpha feature status accepts breaking changes, (2) clean implementation preferred over backward compatibility, (3) fewer function variants to maintain, (4) forces intentional handling of metadata, (5) only ~4 call sites to update. Breaking change enables consistent metadata propagation through entire validation pipeline.

Design pattern:
Metadata gathered in macro wrapper (before validation) keeps validation functions pure. Wrapper handles I/O, validation handles logic. Consistent with bash-parser integration (semantics extracted before validation).

Verification:
- All validation functions accept metadata parameter
- Macro gathers metadata for path tools only
- Non-path tools pass nil for metadata
- All tests pass: make test-buttercup-directory DIR=config/gptel
- No syntax errors: ./bin/tangle-org.sh succeeds

Context: design.md § Phase 2 (Validation signature updates), Decision 3 (Immediate breaking change), Decision 4 (Gather in wrapper)

title: Review git command categorization in read_only lists
description:
Both example presets include 'git' as a single entry in read_only.commands lists, but git has many subcommands with different safety profiles:

Safe (read-only):
- git log, git show, git diff, git status, git branch, git ls-files, git remote -v

Unsafe (modify state):
- git add, git commit, git push, git reset, git checkout, git merge, git rebase

Current listings:
- bash-tools-example.md lines 42-45: Lists specific git subcommands (git log, git show, git diff, git status) ✓
- system-explorer.md line 74: Lists only 'git' with comment '(read-only commands only)' ✗

Issue: system-explorer.md should list specific git subcommands, not generic 'git' command.

Recommendation: Change line 74 in system-explorer.md from:
  - "git"            # Git operations (read-only commands only)

To explicit subcommands:
  - "git log"        # View commit history
  - "git show"       # View commit details
  - "git diff"       # View changes
  - "git status"     # Working tree status
  - "git branch"     # List branches
  - "git ls-files"   # List tracked files
  - "git remote"     # View remotes

File: /Users/jefffarr/emacs/config/gptel/presets/system-explorer.md
Line: 74
notes:
Review completed. Created 1 bead for git command categorization issue:

- emacs-zu9d (P2): Fix system-explorer.md to list specific git subcommands

Issue: system-explorer.md line 74 lists generic 'git' command with comment '(read-only commands only)' but this is misleading. Git has many unsafe subcommands (add, commit, push, reset, etc.) that should NOT be allowed.

Solution: Change to explicit safe subcommands like bash-tools-example.md does:
- git log, git show, git diff, git status, git branch, git ls-files, git remote

bash-tools-example.md is already correct (lines 42-45).

title: Resolve bare filenames against current working directory
description:
Enhance path resolution to resolve bare filenames (without ./ or ../) against current working directory.

SECURITY CONTEXT: After 'cd /new/dir', the command 'cat file.txt' must resolve file.txt to '/new/dir/file.txt', not the original PWD. Currently, jf/bash-resolve-relative-path only handles explicit relative paths (., ./, ../). Bare filenames need special handling.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.el (tangled output)

Current behavior:
  jf/bash-resolve-relative-path handles:
  - . => PWD
  - ./file.txt => PWD/file.txt
  - ../file.txt => (parent of PWD)/file.txt
  
  But does NOT handle:
  - file.txt => should be PWD/file.txt (bare filename)
  - subdir/file.txt => should be PWD/subdir/file.txt (relative path)

Implementation:
1. Enhance jf/bash-resolve-relative-path OR create new function jf/bash--resolve-path-with-pwd
   - Takes file-path, var-context, and optional current-pwd parameter
   - If current-pwd provided, use it; else extract from var-context
   
2. Resolution logic (in order):
   a. If absolute path (starts with /), return unchanged
   b. If starts with ./, ../, or is exactly ., use existing logic
   c. NEW: If bare filename or relative path (no ./ prefix):
      - Concatenate current-pwd + / + file-path
      - Normalize using expand-file-name
   
3. Example behavior:
   ;; Existing behavior (unchanged)
   (jf/bash--resolve-path-with-pwd "./file.txt" ctx "/base")
   => "/base/file.txt"
   
   ;; NEW behavior for bare filenames
   (jf/bash--resolve-path-with-pwd "file.txt" ctx "/base")
   => "/base/file.txt"
   
   (jf/bash--resolve-path-with-pwd "subdir/file.txt" ctx "/base")
   => "/base/subdir/file.txt"
   
   ;; Absolute paths unchanged
   (jf/bash--resolve-path-with-pwd "/tmp/file.txt" ctx "/base")
   => "/tmp/file.txt"

Design rationale: In bash, all relative paths (whether ./file.txt or just file.txt) resolve against the current working directory. The ./ prefix is optional - file.txt and ./file.txt are equivalent. Our parser must match this behavior.

Currently jf/bash-resolve-relative-path is called from jf/bash--resolve-path-variables. We need to either:
- Option A: Enhance jf/bash-resolve-relative-path to accept current-pwd parameter
- Option B: Create wrapper function that calls both variable resolution and path resolution
- Option C: Modify jf/bash--resolve-path-variables to handle bare filenames

Recommendation: Option A - enhance jf/bash-resolve-relative-path with optional current-pwd parameter. This is cleanest and maintains single responsibility.

Signature change:
  OLD: (defun jf/bash-resolve-relative-path (file-path var-context) ...)
  NEW: (defun jf/bash-resolve-relative-path (file-path var-context &optional current-pwd) ...)

Implementation notes:
- If current-pwd provided, use it; else extract from var-context (backward compatible)
- Add bare filename handling after checking for ./ and ../
- Use expand-file-name for normalization (already in existing code)

Verification:
- Test bare filenames: file.txt with current-pwd=/base => /base/file.txt
- Test relative paths: subdir/file.txt with current-pwd=/base => /base/subdir/file.txt
- Test explicit relative paths still work: ./file.txt => /base/file.txt
- Test absolute paths unchanged: /tmp/file.txt => /tmp/file.txt
- Verify backward compatibility when current-pwd not provided
- Will enable tests: test-cd-absolute-path-simple (cat file.txt after cd)
close_reason: Phase 1 (Basic cd tracking) implementation complete - all related tests now passing

- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-glob.org (and .el)

Functions needing docstrings:

1. bash-parser-core.el:
   - jf/bash-parse--detect-structure-type (line 119)
   - jf/bash-parse--count-command-children (line 149)
   - jf/bash-parse--find-direct-child-by-type (line 161)
   - jf/bash-parse--find-node-by-type (line 174)
   - jf/bash-parse--visit-node (line 1283)

2. bash-parser-glob.el:

3. bash-parser-variables.el:
   - jf/bash--navigate-parent-path (line 166)
   - jf/bash--static-dirname (line 298)
   - jf/bash--static-basename (line 324)

Docstring template for internal helpers:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash--helper-function (arg1 arg2)
  "Brief one-line summary of what this does.

Detailed explanation of purpose and behavior.
ARG1 is description of first argument.
ARG2 is description of second argument.

Returns description of return value and type.

Examples:
  (jf/bash--helper-function \"input1\" \"input2\")
    => expected-output

  (jf/bash--helper-function \"edge-case\")
    => edge-case-result

Internal helper for PARENT-FUNCTION-NAME."
  ...)
```

Example: jf/bash--navigate-parent-path
```elisp
(defun jf/bash--navigate-parent-path (base-path levels)
  "Navigate up LEVELS directories from BASE-PATH.

Uses file-name-directory repeatedly to go up the directory tree.
Going up past root stops at \"/\".

BASE-PATH is an absolute directory path.
LEVELS is number of parent directories to traverse (positive integer).

Returns absolute path after navigation.

Examples:
  (jf/bash--navigate-parent-path \"/a/b/c\" 1)
    => \"/a/b/\"

  (jf/bash--navigate-parent-path \"/a/b/c\" 2)
    => \"/a/\"

  (jf/bash--navigate-parent-path \"/a\" 5)
    => \"/\"  ; Stops at root

Internal helper for jf/bash-resolve-relative-path."
  ...)
```

Implementation steps:

2. Identify parent function(s) that call it
3. Write docstring following template

5. Edit .org file (not .el - .el is auto-generated)
6. Tangle with ./bin/tangle-org.sh after each module

Design rationale:
- Even internal helpers benefit from documentation
- Examples make behavior concrete and testable
- Clear docs reduce cognitive load when reading code
- Helps onboarding and future maintenance

Documentation standards:
- First line: imperative mood, <80 chars ("Navigate up LEVELS...")
- Body: explain parameters, return value, edge cases
- Examples: real inputs/outputs that could be test cases
- Note if internal helper and what it supports

Verification:

title: Unify recursion depth limit handling across modules
description:
Unify recursion depth limit handling across bash-parser modules to use consistent limits, comparison operators, and error handling.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (lines 63-65)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-extensions.el (lines 264-267)

Current inconsistencies:

1. Inconsistent limits:
   - bash-parser-recursive.el: Uses configurable jf/bash-recursive-max-depth (default 10)
   - bash-parser-extensions.el: Uses hardcoded 10

2. Different error handling:
   - bash-parser-recursive.el: Throws error (aborts extraction)
     (when (>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
       (error "Max recursion depth exceeded in semantic analysis"))
   
   - bash-parser-extensions.el: Returns error plist (graceful degradation)
     (if (> level 10)
         (list :success nil
               :error "Maximum nesting depth exceeded (limit: 10)"
               :nested-level level))

3. Off-by-one difference:
   - Recursive analyzer uses >= (depth at limit triggers error)
   - Extensions uses > (depth must exceed limit)

Impact: Minor - both prevent infinite recursion, but inconsistency could cause confusion and make system behavior unpredictable at edge cases.

Implementation steps:

1. In bash-parser-extensions.el:
   - Replace hardcoded 10 with jf/bash-recursive-max-depth
   - Change comparison from (> level 10) to (>= level jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
   - Keep error plist pattern (don't change to throwing)

2. In bash-parser-recursive.el:
   - Change error throwing to error plist return
   - Instead of: (error "Max recursion depth exceeded...")
   - Return: (list :success nil :error "Max recursion depth exceeded..." :depth depth)
   - Update callers to check :success field and handle gracefully

3. Update both error messages to be consistent:
   - Include current depth value
   - Include limit value
   - Suggest remediation (simplify command or increase limit)

Design rationale: Error plists are better for composability. Caller can decide whether to abort (strict mode) or continue with partial results (best-effort mode). Throwing errors prevents graceful degradation and makes testing harder. Unified limit variable makes configuration easier - one place to adjust depth limit for entire system.

Verification:
- Test at depth = limit - 1 (should work)
- Test at depth = limit (should return error plist)
- Test at depth = limit + 1 (should return error plist)
- Verify both modules return same error plist structure
- Update existing depth limit tests to verify new behavior
- All existing tests pass

title: Unify recursion depth handling across modules
description:
Recursion depth handling is inconsistent between bash-parser-recursive.el and bash-parser-extensions.el, creating confusion and potential bugs.

**Inconsistencies:**

1. **Different limits:**
   - Recursive analyzer: Configurable `jf/bash-recursive-max-depth` (default 10)
   - Extensions: Hardcoded 10

2. **Different comparison operators:**
   - Recursive analyzer: `(>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)`
   - Extensions: `(> level 10)`

3. **Different error handling:**
   - Recursive analyzer: Throws error (aborts extraction)
   - Extensions: Returns error plist (allows graceful degradation)

**Current code:**

bash-parser-recursive.el (lines 63-65):
```elisp
(when (>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
  (error "Max recursion depth exceeded in semantic analysis"))
```

bash-parser-extensions.el (lines 264-267):
```elisp
(if (> level 10)
    (list :success nil
          :error "Maximum nesting depth exceeded (limit: 10)"
          :nested-level level)
```

**Files to modify:**
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-extensions.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-extensions.org
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.org

**Implementation steps:**

1. Use shared depth limit variable in extensions module:

```elisp
;; In bash-parser-extensions.el, add at top:
(defvar jf/bash-recursive-max-depth 10
  "Maximum recursion depth shared across parser modules.
Defined here for use when bash-parser-recursive not loaded.")

;; In jf/bash-parse-nested-command:
(if (>= level jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)  ; Use >= for consistency
    (list :success nil
          :error (format "Maximum nesting depth exceeded (limit: %d)"
                        jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
          :nested-level level)
```

2. Change recursive analyzer to return error plist instead of throwing:

```elisp
;; In jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive:
;; Replace:
(when (>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
  (error "Max recursion depth exceeded in semantic analysis"))

;; With:
(when (>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
  (message "Warning: Max recursion depth %d exceeded, returning partial results" 
           jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
  (return operations))  ; Return what we have so far
```

Note: Elisp doesn't have `return` - use `cl-return-from` with named block:

```elisp
(defun jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive (parsed-command var-context &optional depth)
  "Recursively extract file operations from PARSED-COMMAND at all nesting levels..."
  (cl-block analyze-recursive
    (let ((depth (or depth 0))
          (operations nil)
          (substitution-patterns nil))
      
      ;; Depth check - return partial results instead of error
      (when (>= depth jf/bash-recursive-max-depth)
        (message "Warning: Max recursion depth %d exceeded at command %s, returning partial results"
                 jf/bash-recursive-max-depth
                 (plist-get parsed-command :command-name))
        (cl-return-from analyze-recursive operations))
      
      ;; ... rest of function ...
      operations)))
```

3. Update docstrings to document error behavior:

bash-parser-recursive.el:
```elisp
"Recursively extract file operations from PARSED-COMMAND at all nesting levels.

...

DEPTH prevents infinite recursion (default 0, max controlled by 
jf/bash-recursive-max-depth). When max depth exceeded, logs warning 
and returns partial results collected so far."
```

bash-parser-extensions.el:
```elisp
"Parse nested command string recursively.

...

Recursion termination:
  - Maximum depth: Controlled by jf/bash-recursive-max-depth (default 10)
  - Returns error plist with :success nil when limit exceeded
  - Detection failure: No more injection patterns found
  - Parse failure: Command string is invalid"
```

4. Update corresponding .org files with same changes

**Design rationale:**
Returning partial results (graceful degradation) is better than throwing errors because:
1. Allows extraction of operations from parseable parts of command
2. Doesn't abort entire analysis due to one deep nesting
3. Logs warning for visibility but doesn't break caller
4. Aligns with defensive programming principles

Using >= instead of > is clearer: "if depth equals or exceeds max, stop" vs "if depth is greater than max, stop" (same logic but >= reads better).

**Verification:**
1. Run tests: make test-bash-parser test-parser-extension
2. Test depth limit: Create command with 11 levels of nesting
3. Verify warning message logged
4. Verify partial results returned (first 10 levels)
5. Verify no errors thrown that break caller
6. Check both modules use same limit variable
close_reason: Implemented via parallel orchestrator - unified recursion depth handling

title: Implement :nesting-depth metadata for nested operations
description:
Add :nesting-depth metadata to operations extracted from nested commands to enable depth-based security policies.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-extensions.el (jf/bash-mark-indirect-operations)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (recursive analyzer marking code)

Current problem:
Operations from nested commands are marked with :indirect t but missing the :nesting-depth metadata required by spec. From spec.md lines 96-99:

#### Scenario: Nested indirect operations
- WHEN extracting operations from "bash -c 'sh -c rm file.txt'"
- THEN operation marked :indirect t with :nesting-depth 2

Current behavior:
;; bash -c 'sh -c "rm file.txt"'
;; Expected: :indirect t :nesting-depth 2
;; Actual:   :indirect t

Impact: Cannot implement depth-based security policies. Security validator needs nesting depth to enforce stricter policies on deeply nested operations (e.g., block operations at depth > 3).

Implementation options:

Option 1 (Recommended): Add in recursive analyzer where depth is tracked
- In bash-parser-recursive.el when marking nested operations
- After (plist-put op :indirect t)
- Add (plist-put op :nesting-depth depth)
- Depth is already tracked as function parameter

Option 2: Add parameter to marking function
- Modify jf/bash-mark-indirect-operations signature:
  (defun jf/bash-mark-indirect-operations (operations &optional nesting-depth) ...)
- When nesting-depth provided, add to marked-op plist
- Callers pass depth information when available

Implementation steps:
1. Choose Option 1 (simpler, leverages existing depth tracking)

2. In bash-parser-recursive.el, locate where nested operations are marked :indirect
   - This will be in the new code added by emacs-0whw (recursive analyzer integration)
   - After (plist-put op :indirect t)
   - Add (plist-put op :nesting-depth depth)

3. Update jf/bash-mark-indirect-operations docstring to note that :nesting-depth should be added by caller when depth info available

4. Add tests verifying :nesting-depth metadata:
   - Single level nesting: bash -c 'rm file' → :nesting-depth 1
   - Double nesting: bash -c 'sh -c "rm file"' → :nesting-depth 2
   - Triple nesting: bash -c 'sh -c "bash -c rm"' → :nesting-depth 3

Design rationale: Nesting depth is critical for security analysis. Deeply nested commands are more suspicious and should face stricter validation. For example, a security policy might allow :indirect operations but block those with :nesting-depth > 2. The recursive analyzer already tracks depth, so adding this metadata is straightforward.

Dependencies:
- Should be implemented after or alongside emacs-0whw (recursive analyzer integration)
- The recursive analyzer integration creates the call sites where depth tracking happens

Verification:
- Test single-level nested command has :nesting-depth 1
- Test double-level nested command has :nesting-depth 2
- Test multiple nesting levels produce correct depth values
- Security validator can access :nesting-depth for policy decisions
- All existing tests still pass
close_reason: Implemented via parallel orchestrator - added :nesting-depth metadata

title: Add tilde expansion to general path resolution
description:
Tilde expansion (~/) is only implemented for cd and pushd commands, not for general file paths. Commands like 'cat ~/file.txt' do not expand the tilde.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.el (lines 79-158)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (corresponding section)

Current behavior:
Tilde expansion works for:
- cd ~/path (lines 802-812 in jf/bash--extract-cd-target)
- pushd ~/path (lines 995-1006 in jf/bash--extract-pushd-target)

Does NOT work for:
- cat ~/file.txt
- cp ~/source ~/dest
- Any other command with ~/ paths

Example:
(jf/bash-resolve-variables "~/file.txt" '((HOME . "/home/user")))
;; => "~/file.txt"  ; UNCHANGED - tilde not expanded!

Implementation steps:
1. Add tilde expansion to jf/bash-resolve-variables BEFORE variable resolution
2. Check for ~/path prefix (NOT bare ~ which should remain)
3. Replace ~/ with HOME value from var-context
4. If HOME not in context, leave path unchanged

Code addition (after line 92, before variable detection):
  ;; Expand tilde in paths before variable resolution
  (let ((file-path-expanded
         (if (and (string-prefix-p "~/" file-path)
                  (alist-get 'HOME var-context))
             (concat (alist-get 'HOME var-context)
                     (substring file-path 1))  ; Remove ~ keep /
           file-path)))
    
    ;; Continue with existing logic using file-path-expanded
    (let* ((detection-result (jf/bash-detect-variable-references file-path-expanded))
           ...)

Special cases to handle:
1. ~/path → expand to HOME/path
2. ~ alone → leave as-is (bare tilde is current user, not a path prefix)
3. ~user/path → NOT supported (would require user database lookup)
4. No HOME in context → leave ~/path unchanged

Security consideration:
Tilde expansion must happen BEFORE variable resolution to prevent:
  EVIL="~"
  cat $EVIL/../../etc/passwd  # Should NOT expand tilde in variable value

The ~/ should only expand when literally present in the original command string, not when introduced through variable expansion.

Design rationale:
Bash shells expand ~/path to HOME/path for ALL commands, not just cd. This is a fundamental shell feature that must be replicated for accurate path resolution. Without this, security validation fails for commands using home-relative paths.

Test cases to add:
(ert-deftest test-tilde-expansion-cat ()
  "Test that cat ~/file.txt expands tilde."
  (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "cat ~/file.txt"))
         (var-context '((HOME . "/home/user")))
         (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed var-context)))
    (should (equal (plist-get (car ops) :file) "/home/user/file.txt"))))

(ert-deftest test-tilde-expansion-variable-no-expand ()
  "Test that tilde in variable value is NOT expanded."
  (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "cat $EVIL/file"))
         (var-context '((EVIL . "~") (HOME . "/home/user")))
         (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed var-context)))
    ;; Should be ~/file NOT /home/user/file
    (should (equal (plist-get (car ops) :file) "~/file"))))

Verification:
1. Run existing tests: make test-directory DIR=config/experiments/bash-parser/test
2. All existing tests should still pass (tilde currently rare in test corpus)
3. Add new test file: test/test-tilde-expansion.el with cases above
4. Manual verification:
   (jf/bash-resolve-variables "~/config/file" '((HOME . "/home/user")))
   ;; Should return: "/home/user/config/file"

Expected impact: Enables proper path resolution for home-relative paths in ~15% of real-world commands.

Pattern reference: Similar tilde expansion already implemented in cd/pushd handlers can be used as reference (lines 808-812).

title: Fix forward reference in gptel.org: skills expansion function called before definition
description:
## Context
Code review of emacs-hed found a forward reference bug in gptel.el.

## Issue
- gptel.el:73 calls (jf/gptel-agent--expand-all-agent-skills) at top-level during load
- Function jf/gptel-agent--expand-all-agent-skills is defined at gptel.el:118
- Emacs loads files sequentially; line 73 evaluates before line 118
- Result: void-function error during Emacs initialization

## Fix
Reorder org source blocks in gptel.org so the "Preset Skills Integration" section (containing the defun) is tangled BEFORE the "GPTEL Preset Registration" section (which calls the function). Or move the function call into the later block after the definitions.

## Files
- config/gptel/gptel.org (reorder tangled blocks)

## Discovered from
Code review of emacs-hed

title: scope-shell-tools helper functions unused by scope-core validator
description:
The scope-shell-tools.org module defines helper functions (jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command, jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category) that implement the correct bash command validation logic, but the validator in scope-core.org (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool) reimplements the logic incorrectly instead of using these helpers.

Files involved:
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.org - defines helper functions
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org - defines validator that should use helpers

Options:
1. Update scope-core validator to call the helper functions from scope-shell-tools
2. Move helper functions from scope-shell-tools to scope-core
3. Remove duplicate implementation

The helpers correctly access the :commands key within each category, while the scope-core validator does not.

title: Remove dead code: scope-manager, v2.0 templates, gptel-agent requires, update preset files
description:
Remove dead code: scope-manager, v2.0 templates, gptel-agent requires, update preset files

## Files to delete
- `config/gptel/scope/scope-manager.org` and `scope-manager.el` (v1.0 advice-based interception — dead code)

## Files to modify
- `config/gptel/scope/scope-commands.org` — remove v2.0 scope templates
- `config/gptel/tools/sql-tools.org` — remove `(require 'gptel-agent)`
- `config/gptel/gptel.org` — remove scope-manager from module loading if listed
- `config/gptel/presets/executor.md` — remove scope sections, add scope_profile, update tools
- `config/gptel/presets/explore.md` — remove scope sections, add scope_profile
- `config/gptel/presets/plan.md` — remove scope sections, add scope_profile
- `config/gptel/presets/research.md` — remove scope sections, add scope_profile
- `config/gptel/presets/minimal.md` — remove scope sections if present
- `config/gptel/presets/perplexity-researcher.md` — remove scope sections if present
- `config/gptel/presets/zettelkasten.md` — remove scope sections, add scope_profile

## Implementation steps

1. Delete `config/gptel/scope/scope-manager.org` and `config/gptel/scope/scope-manager.el` entirely
   - This is v1.0 advice-based scope interception, already superseded by v2.0 macro-based approach in scope-core.org
2. In `scope-commands.org`, remove v2.0 scope template functions:
   - `jf/gptel-scope--template-deny-all`
   - `jf/gptel-scope--template-codebase-read`
   - `jf/gptel-scope--template-org-roam-safe`
   - `jf/gptel-scope--template-permissive`
   - `jf/gptel-scope--template-project-aware`
   - `jf/gptel-scope-init-plan` (interactive command for template-based scope creation)
   - Helper functions: `jf/gptel-scope--tool-yaml`, `jf/gptel--format-project-patterns`
   - Keep: `jf/gptel-scope--parse-preset-tools` (reads tool list from registered presets — may need updating to read from `gptel--known-presets`), `jf/gptel-scope-edit-plan` (rename to edit-scope)
3. In `sql-tools.org`, remove `(require 'gptel-agent)` line — it was unused
4. If scope-manager is referenced in `gptel.org` module loading list, remove it
5. Update each preset `.md` file in `config/gptel/presets/`:
   - Remove scope sections from YAML frontmatter: `paths:`, `org_roam_patterns:`, `shell_commands:` (and their nested content)
   - Add `scope_profile: "coding"` (or appropriate profile name: "research" for research.md, "restricted" for minimal.md, etc.)
   - In `executor.md`: change `"Agent"` to `"PersistentAgent"` in tools list
6. Tangle and validate all modified `.org` files
7. Verify no references to `gptel-agent` remain: `grep -r "gptel-agent" config/gptel/`

## Design rationale

Phase 3 cleanup removes code made obsolete by the preset registration pipeline and scope.yml system:
- scope-manager.org was the v1.0 advice-based approach, already superseded by v2.0's `gptel-make-scoped-tool` macro
- v2.0 scope templates are replaced by scope profile YAML files (simpler, data-driven, no elisp template functions)
- sql-tools.org's `require gptel-agent` was already unused (vestigial)
- Preset files retain only model/backend/tools/system config; scope moves to profile references

## Verification

- All `./bin/tangle-org.sh` commands pass for modified files
- `scope-manager.org` and `.el` no longer exist on disk
- `grep -r "gptel-agent" config/gptel/` returns NO hits
- `grep -r "scope-manager" config/gptel/` returns NO hits (except maybe comments)
- Emacs starts without errors
- `(jf/gptel-preset-register-all)` still works (preset files have valid YAML after scope section removal)
- Preset files contain `scope_profile:` key

Context: design.md § Decision 8, Migration Phase 3, scope-presets.md, core.md

title: Behavioral tests: session restoration (read-side)
description:
Create Buttercup behavioral tests verifying the read-side of session restoration via `jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer`.

## Depends on
- emacs-kvqo (persistence-test-helpers infrastructure)

## Test file
`config/gptel/test/session-restoration-spec.el`

## Scenarios to cover

### New session restoration (no Local Variables in file)
- Reads preset name from seeded metadata.yml
- Calls gptel--apply-preset with correct preset symbol (spy-on gptel integration)
- Enables gptel-mode (spy-on)
- Sets buffer-local session variables (jf/gptel--session-id, jf/gptel--session-dir, jf/gptel--branch-name, jf/gptel--branch-dir)
- Registers session in global registry (spy-on jf/gptel--register-session)
- Updates current symlink to branch name

### Existing session restoration (has gptel--preset Local Variable)
- Detects existing gptel--preset buffer-local variable
- Enables gptel-mode (which triggers gptel--restore-state upstream)
- Does NOT call gptel--apply-preset (preset comes from Local Variables)
- Sets buffer-local session variables
- Registers in registry

### Path pattern detection
- Recognizes branch pattern: */branches/<name>/session.md
- Recognizes agent pattern: */agents/<name>/session.md
- Ignores non-session .md files (no match → no initialization)

### Error handling
- Handles missing metadata.yml gracefully (falls back to basic gptel-mode)
- Handles corrupt metadata.yml (yaml parse error → warns, continues)
- Handles missing preset (preset not in gptel--known-presets → warns, basic mode)

## Testing approach
Uses `with-seeded-files` macro from persistence-test-helpers (emacs-kvqo).

Key pattern for read-side:
```elisp
(with-seeded-files
    (("/sessions/my-session/branches/main/metadata.yml"
      . "session_id: \"my-session\"\npreset: \"executor\"\n"))
  ;; Set up buffer to look like a session file
  (with-temp-buffer
    (setq buffer-file-name "/sessions/my-session/branches/main/session.md")
    (jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer)
    (expect jf/gptel--session-id :to-equal "my-session")))
```

gptel functions mocked via standard spy-on (not cl-letf):
- gptel--apply-preset — verify called with correct preset
- gptel-mode — verify enabled
- gptel-get-preset — control return value (exists or nil)

Filesystem functions for validation checks (file-directory-p for valid-session-directory-p, file-exists-p for valid-branch-directory-p) handled via with-seeded-files pass-through or explicit cl-letf overrides per scenario.

## Modules exercised (all run for REAL)
- jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer (commands.el)
- jf/gptel--session-id-from-directory (filesystem.el)
- jf/gptel--valid-session-directory-p (filesystem.el)
- jf/gptel--valid-branch-directory-p (filesystem.el)
- jf/gptel--metadata-file-path (filesystem.el)
- jf/gptel--read-session-metadata (metadata.el) — for new session path
- jf/gptel--register-session (registry.el)
- jf/gptel--update-current-symlink (filesystem.el)

## Note on find-file-hook
The current implementation uses find-file-hook for auto-init. This hook is planned for replacement. Tests should call jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer directly (the function the hook calls), making them resilient to the hook mechanism changing.

title: Add token tracking to bash-parser
description:
Add token tracking to bash-parser core functions

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org

Implementation steps:
1. Define token structure with required fields:
   - :id (unique integer)
   - :type (keyword: :command-name, :positional-arg, :flag, :flag-arg, :command-substitution, :separator, :redirection, :pipe)
   - :value (string: actual token content)
   - :start (integer: character position, 0-indexed)
   - :end (integer: character position after last char, exclusive)
   - :pipe-segment (integer: which pipeline segment)
   - :associated-flag (optional: token ID of associated flag for flag-args)
   - :parsed-inner (boolean: for command substitutions)
2. Modify parse functions to generate tokens during parsing:
   - Track position as parser consumes input
   - Assign sequential token IDs starting from 0
   - Emit token plist for each syntactic element
3. Add :tokens field to parsed command output structure
4. Ensure tokens are ordered by position in command string
5. Update existing tests to verify token inventory presence

Design rationale:
Token-level tracking is essential for coverage reporting. Without granular tokens, we cannot identify which parts of a command were understood vs. blindspots. Word-level tokens (one per syntactic element) balance precision with complexity - character-level would be excessive, command-level too coarse. Position tracking enables source mapping and visualization. Type classification supports coverage-by-type diagnostics.

Design pattern:
Token generation integrated into parsing (not post-processing). As parser recognizes each syntactic element, emit token immediately:

```elisp
;; Example: parsing positional argument
(let* ((start-pos current-position)
       (arg-value (consume-until-delimiter))
       (end-pos current-position)
       (token-id (get-next-token-id)))
  (push (list :id token-id
              :type :positional-arg
              :value arg-value
              :start start-pos
              :end end-pos
              :pipe-segment current-segment)
        tokens))
```

Token ID allocation is simple counter. Tokens collected in list during parse, added to result structure.

Verification:
- Test: Parse "cat file.txt" produces tokens with sequential IDs
- Test: Each token has all required fields (:id, :type, :value, :start, :end)
- Test: Token positions correctly map to command string (substring extraction works)
- Test: Pipeline segments correctly assigned (test with "cat a | grep b")
- Test: Flag arguments have :associated-flag pointing to correct flag token
- Spec coverage: bash-parser-tokens/spec.md § all scenarios in "Token inventory structure" and "Token types"

Context: design.md § Decision 4 (Token Granularity - Word-Level with Metadata)
close_reason: Implemented via sequential orchestrator. Token tracking added successfully. Note: 4 corpus tests need updating for new :tokens field (expected, not regression).

title: Fix integration test failures in pipeline-orchestration-spec.el
description:
Fix 16 test failures in new integration tests discovered during emacs-op2t implementation.

## Files to Modify

config/gptel/tools/test/integration/
├── pipeline-orchestration-spec.el (10 failures)
├── command-execution-spec.el (4 failures)
└── combined-validation-spec.el (2 failures)

## Issues to Fix

### 1. Category Structure Mismatch
**Problem:** bash-tools categories expect lists of strings, not individual strings
**Current (wrong):**
```elisp
:bash-tools '(:categories (:read-only (:commands ("ls"))
                          :deny ())
```
**Should be:**
```elisp
:bash-tools (list :categories (list :read-only (list :commands '("ls" "cat"))
                                    :safe-write (list :commands '())
                                    :dangerous (list :commands '()))
                  :deny '("rm"))
```

### 2. Invalid Buttercup Matcher
**Problem:** `:to-have-length-of` doesn't exist in Buttercup
**Current (wrong):**
```elisp
(expect (plist-get result :warnings) :to-have-length-of 1)
```
**Should be:**
```elisp
(expect (length (plist-get result :warnings)) :to-equal 1)
```

### 3. Absolute Path Regex Too Broad
**Problem:** `jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths` regex matches relative paths like "./file.txt"
**Current regex:** `/[[:alnum:]_/-]+`
**Issue:** Matches any slash followed by alphanumeric
**Test expecting nil warnings for "./file.txt" fails**

**Fix options:**
- Option A: Update regex to only match paths starting with / (not ./)
- Option B: Update test expectation to accept the warning
- Recommendation: Option A - relative paths shouldn't trigger absolute path warnings

### 4. macOS Path Resolution
**Problem:** macOS resolves `/tmp` to `/private/tmp` via symlink
**Current (fails):**
```elisp
(expect captured-dir :to-equal "/tmp/")
```
**Should be:**
```elisp
(expect (file-truename captured-dir) :to-equal (file-truename "/tmp/"))
```

### 5. Timeout Test Mocking Error
**Problem:** Spy mock throws timeout signal but test doesn't catch it properly
**Current:** Signal escapes test boundary causing test failure
**Fix:** Wrap execution in condition-case or adjust mock to not signal

### 6. Error Type Expectations
**Problem:** Tests expect `command_denied` but getting `command_not_allowed`
**Root cause:** Commands not in deny list fail the "not in any category" check first
**Fix:** Ensure commands are added to appropriate categories before testing denial

## Implementation Steps

1. **Fix all category structures** (affects 10+ tests)
   - Replace plists with proper list construction
   - Use `list` function for nested plists
   - Keep command lists as quoted lists

2. **Replace invalid matchers** (affects 1 test)
   - Change `:to-have-length-of` to explicit length checks
   - Use `(expect (length LIST) :to-equal N)`

3. **Fix absolute path detection** (affects 2 tests)
   - Update `jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths` regex in scope-shell-tools.el
   - Change from `/[[:alnum:]_/-]+` to `^/[[:alnum:]_/-]+` (must start with /)
   - Verify "./file.txt" no longer triggers warning
   - Verify "/etc/passwd" still triggers warning

4. **Fix path resolution** (affects 1 test)
   - Use `file-truename` for path comparison
   - Handles macOS /tmp → /private/tmp symlinks

5. **Fix timeout test mocking** (affects 1 test)
   - Wrap jf/gptel-bash--execute-command call in condition-case
   - OR: Change mock to return error plist instead of signaling

6. **Fix command categorization** (affects pipeline tests)
   - Ensure test commands are in correct categories before expecting specific error types
   - "rm" should be in deny list AND not in any category for proper testing

## Verification

After fixes:
```bash
./bin/run-tests.sh -f buttercup -d config/gptel/tools/test/integration
# Should show: Ran 25 specs, 0 failed
```

Specific verification:
- All 12 pipeline orchestration tests pass
- All 8 command execution tests pass
- All 5 combined validation tests pass
- No Buttercup matcher errors
- Path comparisons work on both macOS and Linux

## Design Rationale

These are legitimate test infrastructure issues, not logic bugs:
- Buttercup matcher API mismatch (easy fix)
- Plist construction syntax errors (easy fix)
- Platform-specific path handling (standard pattern)
- Absolute path regex needs tightening (minor scope-shell-tools change)

The test logic and coverage strategy are sound - these are implementation details that need alignment with the actual function signatures and Buttercup API.

title: Implement conditional context tracking
description:
Track file operations in conditional branches (if/then/else) and mark them as conditional, distinguishing test operations from branch operations.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (enhance conditional parsing)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (track conditional context)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-conditional-context.el (new test file)

Implementation steps:
1. Enhance conditional parsing in bash-parser-core.el to separate test and branches:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash--parse-conditional (node)
     "Parse conditional with test and branch separation."
     (let ((test-condition nil)
           (then-branch nil)
           (else-branch nil)
           (elif-branches nil)
           (test-command nil))  ; NEW: The actual test command/expression
       
       ;; Extract test condition (the [ ] or [[ ]] expression or command)
       (setq test-condition (jf/bash--extract-test-condition node))
       (setq test-command (jf/bash--extract-test-command node))  ; NEW
       
       ;; Extract then branch commands
       (setq then-branch (jf/bash--extract-then-branch node))
       
       ;; Extract else branch if present
       (setq else-branch (jf/bash--extract-else-branch node))
       
       ;; Extract elif branches if present
       (setq elif-branches (jf/bash--extract-elif-branches node))
       
       (list :command-name "if"
             :test-condition test-condition
             :test-command test-command  ; NEW: Parseable command string
             :then-branch then-branch
             :else-branch else-branch
             :elif-branches elif-branches)))
   ```

2. Add conditional context tracking to recursive analyzer:
   ```elisp
   ;; In bash-parser-recursive.el
   (defun jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive (parsed-command var-context &optional depth)
     "Recursive analysis with conditional context tracking."
     (let ((depth (or depth 0))
           (operations nil))
       
       ;; ... existing code ...
       
       ;; NEW: Process conditionals with branch tracking
       (when (string= (plist-get parsed-command :command-name) "if")
         (let ((test-command (plist-get parsed-command :test-command))
               (then-branch (plist-get parsed-command :then-branch))
               (else-branch (plist-get parsed-command :else-branch)))
           
           ;; Parse and analyze test condition
           (when test-command
             (let* ((test-parsed (jf/bash-parse test-command))
                    (test-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                              test-parsed var-context (1+ depth))))
               ;; Mark test operations with :test-condition t
               (setq test-ops (mapcar (lambda (op)
                                       (let ((new-op (copy-sequence op)))
                                         (plist-put new-op :test-condition t)
                                         new-op))
                                     test-ops))
               (setq operations (append operations test-ops))))
           
           ;; Parse and analyze then branch
           (when then-branch
             (let* ((then-parsed (jf/bash-parse then-branch))
                    (then-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                              then-parsed var-context (1+ depth))))
               ;; Mark then operations with :conditional t
               (setq then-ops (mapcar (lambda (op)
                                       (let ((new-op (copy-sequence op)))
                                         (plist-put new-op :conditional t)
                                         (plist-put new-op :branch :then)
                                         new-op))
                                     then-ops))
               (setq operations (append operations then-ops))))
           
           ;; Parse and analyze else branch
           (when else-branch
             (let* ((else-parsed (jf/bash-parse else-branch))
                    (else-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                              else-parsed var-context (1+ depth))))
               ;; Mark else operations with :conditional t
               (setq else-ops (mapcar (lambda (op)
                                       (let ((new-op (copy-sequence op)))
                                         (plist-put new-op :conditional t)
                                         (plist-put new-op :branch :else)
                                         new-op))
                                     else-ops))
               (setq operations (append operations else-ops))))))
       
       operations))
   ```

3. Handle file test operators specially:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash--extract-file-test-operations (test-condition var-context)
     "Extract file operations from test condition like [ -f file ].
     
     File test operators:
       -f file : test if regular file (reads metadata)
       -d dir  : test if directory (reads metadata)
       -e path : test if exists (reads metadata)
       -r file : test if readable (reads metadata)
       -w file : test if writable (reads metadata)
       -x file : test if executable (reads metadata)
     
     These are metadata reads, not content reads."
     (let ((operations nil))
       ;; Parse test condition to extract operator and file
       (when (string-match "\\[\\[?\\s-*\\(-[fderswx]\\)\\s-+\\(\\S-+\\)\\s-*\\]\\]?" test-condition)
         (let ((operator (match-string 1 test-condition))
               (file-path (match-string 2 test-condition)))
           ;; Resolve variables in file path
           (let* ((resolved-path (jf/bash--resolve-path-variables file-path var-context))
                  (final-path (if (stringp resolved-path)
                                 resolved-path
                               (plist-get resolved-path :path))))
             (push (list :file final-path
                        :operation :read-metadata
                        :confidence :high
                        :source :test-expression
                        :test-operator operator)
                   operations))))
       operations))
   ```

4. Create test-conditional-context.el with conditional tests:
   ```elisp
   (ert-deftest test-conditional-then-branch ()
     "Test operations in then branch marked as conditional."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "if [ -f config.yml ]; then cat config.yml; fi"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; Test reads metadata
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "config.yml")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read-metadata)
                               (plist-get op :test-condition)))
                        ops))
       ;; Then branch reads content
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "config.yml")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read)
                               (plist-get op :conditional)
                               (eq (plist-get op :branch) :then)))
                        ops))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-conditional-else-branch ()
     "Test operations in else branch marked as conditional."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "if [ -f old.txt ]; then cat old.txt; else echo 'missing' > log.txt; fi"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; Then branch operation
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "old.txt")
                               (eq (plist-get op :branch) :then)))
                        ops))
       ;; Else branch operation
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "log.txt")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :write)
                               (eq (plist-get op :branch) :else)))
                        ops))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-conditional-with-substitution ()
     "Test conditional with command substitution in test."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "if [ -f $(which emacs) ]; then echo 'found'; fi"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; which operation is in test context
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (plist-get op :test-condition))
                        ops))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-conditional-command-based ()
     "Test conditional using command exit status."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "if grep -q pattern file.txt; then echo 'found'; fi"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; grep reads file in test context
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "file.txt")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read)
                               (plist-get op :test-condition)))
                        ops))))
   ```

5. Update corpus with conditional context expectations:
   ```elisp
   (:id "conditional-context-001"
    :command "if [ -f config.yml ]; then cat config.yml; fi"
    :note "Test and then branch operations"
    :expect-ops ((:file "config.yml" :operation :read-metadata :test-condition t)
                 (:file "config.yml" :operation :read :conditional t :branch :then)))
   ```

Design rationale: Conditionals create branching execution paths where file operations may or may not occur. We need to distinguish:
1. Test operations (checking file existence, permissions) - :test-condition t
2. Then branch operations (may execute) - :conditional t :branch :then
3. Else branch operations (may execute) - :conditional t :branch :else

The :read-metadata operation type distinguishes file tests ([ -f file ]) from content reads (cat file). This is semantically important - testing if a file exists is different from reading its contents.

The :conditional flag alerts downstream code that these operations might not happen - they depend on the test condition succeeding or failing.

Verification:
- Run: make test && make test TEST=test-conditional-context
- Test: Parse "if [ -f config.yml ]; then cat config.yml; fi"
- Verify: Two operations for config.yml - one :read-metadata with :test-condition t, one :read with :conditional t
- Test: Parse "if [ -f old.txt ]; then cat old.txt; else echo 'missing' > log.txt; fi"
- Verify: Then branch operation has :branch :then, else branch has :branch :else
- Test: Parse conditional with grep test and verify test-condition flag on grep operation

title: Document tree-sitter (( workaround and track upstream fix
description:
Document the (( workaround, add tests, and track upstream tree-sitter-bash fix.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (Introduction and Internal Parser sections)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (after tangling)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/bash-parser-core-test.el

Problem:
The parser has a workaround for tree-sitter-bash incorrectly parsing (( at the start of commands (lines 49-56, 89-92). The workaround normalizes "((cmd))" to "( (cmd))". However:

1. Not documented in module overview
2. No test specifically for this case
3. No tracking of when it can be removed
4. Could cause position mismatches in error reporting
5. No link to upstream issue

Implementation steps:

1. Add Known Limitations section to bash-parser-core.org Introduction:

** Known Limitations

*** Tree-sitter (( parsing workaround

Tree-sitter-bash (as of 2024) incorrectly treats (( at line start as
arithmetic expansion $((...)) instead of nested subshells ( (...) ).

Workaround: We normalize "((" -> "( (" at parse time (see lines 49-56).

Impact:
- Adds space between parentheses
- May cause position mismatches if using AST for error reporting
- Invisible to callers (normalized string not exposed)

Tracking: Check tree-sitter-bash GitHub issues periodically for fix
https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-bash/issues

When fixed: Remove normalization logic and add test for (( handling

2. Improve inline comment at workaround site (line 49):

Replace current comment:
  ;; Pre-process: Fix tree-sitter limitation with (( at start
  ;; Tree-sitter confuses (( with arithmetic expansion $((...))
  ;; Add space to make it parse as nested subshells: ( (...)

With more detailed comment:
  ;; WORKAROUND: tree-sitter-bash limitation with (( at line start
  ;; 
  ;; Issue: tree-sitter-bash treats "((cmd))" as arithmetic expansion $((cmd))
  ;; instead of nested subshells ( (cmd) ). This affects subshell commands.
  ;;
  ;; Fix: Normalize "((" -> "( (" to force subshell parse
  ;;
  ;; Impact: Adds invisible space. May affect position-based error reporting.
  ;; 
  ;; Tracking: Check https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-bash/issues
  ;; When fixed upstream, remove this normalization and update test.

3. Add specific test case for (( handling:

#+begin_src emacs-lisp :tangle test/bash-parser-core-test.el
(ert-deftest test-jf/bash-parse-double-paren-subshell ()
  "Test workaround for (( at line start.
This test documents the tree-sitter-bash limitation workaround.
When upstream is fixed, this test verifies we can remove the workaround."
  (let ((result (jf/bash-parse "((echo hello))")))
    (should (plist-get result :success))
    (should (eq (plist-get result :type) :subshell))
    ;; Inner subshell should also parse correctly
    (let ((inner (plist-get result :subshell-body)))
      (should (plist-get inner :success))
      (should (eq (plist-get inner :type) :subshell)))))

(ert-deftest test-jf/bash-parse-arithmetic-expansion ()
  "Ensure arithmetic expansion $((expr)) still works correctly.
This should NOT trigger the (( workaround since it has $ prefix."
  (let ((result (jf/bash-parse "echo $((1 + 2))")))
    (should (plist-get result :success))
    (should (eq (plist-get result :type) :simple))
    (should (string= (plist-get result :command-name) "echo"))))
#+end_src

4. Add TODO comment to track removal:

At workaround site (line 49), add:
  ;; TODO: Check tree-sitter-bash periodically for upstream fix
  ;; Last checked: 2026-03-06 - still needed

5. Document in module docstring:

Add to jf/bash-parse docstring (line 6):

  Known limitations:
  - Commands starting with (( are normalized to ( ( due to tree-sitter-bash
    limitation. This is transparent to callers but may affect position-based
    error reporting if using the :ast field.

Design rationale:
Workarounds should be:
1. Clearly documented (why it exists, what it does)
2. Tested (so we know when it can be removed)
3. Tracked (periodically check if still needed)
4. Scoped (minimize impact on rest of system)

The (( normalization is a pragmatic fix for tree-sitter limitation. We document it thoroughly so:
- Future maintainers understand why it exists
- We can remove it when upstream fixes the issue
- Users know about potential edge cases

Alternative considered: Don't normalize, document as unsupported
Rejected because: (( subshells are valid bash and should work.

Verification:
1. Run new tests: make test-bash-parser
2. Verify (( test cases pass
3. Check documentation renders correctly in org-mode
4. Verify arithmetic expansion still works: echo $((1+2))
5. Test edge case: ((((nested subshells))))

Long-term tracking:
- Add reminder to check tree-sitter-bash every 6 months
- Or: Subscribe to tree-sitter-bash GitHub releases
- When fixed: Remove normalization, verify tests still pass

Priority: P3 - Documentation improvement, not urgent but important for long-term maintenance.

title: Add parsing and resolution phase functions
description:
Add parse and resolve phase functions to preset-configuration module, establishing the three-phase pipeline.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/preset-configuration.org

Implementation steps:
1. Implement jf/gptel-preset-parse (file path → parsed plist)
   - Read file contents
   - Detect format (.md uses YAML frontmatter, .org uses PROPERTIES drawer)
   - For .md: use yaml-parse-string on frontmatter, extract markdown body as :system
   - For .org: parse PROPERTIES drawer, extract org body as :system
   - Return plist with strings/numbers/lists only (no structs/symbols)
   - Return nil on parse failure, log error with file path
2. Implement jf/gptel-preset-resolve (parsed plist → resolved plist)
   - Call jf/gptel-preset--resolve-backend on :backend
   - Call jf/gptel-preset--resolve-model on :model
   - Call jf/gptel-preset--resolve-tools on :tools
   - Pass through other fields unchanged (temperature, system, etc.)
   - Return resolved plist with structs/symbols
3. Add comprehensive docstrings documenting intermediate representation contracts

Design rationale:
Parse phase extracts data without performing lookups (separation of concerns). This makes parsing independently testable and allows inspection of raw data before resolution. Resolve phase performs all lookups in one pass, making it easy to handle errors consistently. Each phase has explicit input/output contract documented in docstrings.

Design pattern - Parsed plist:
```elisp
(:backend "Claude"
 :model "claude-opus-4-5"
 :temperature 1.0
 :tools ("read_file" "write_file_in_scope")
 :system "message text...")
```

Design pattern - Resolved plist:
```elisp
(:backend <gptel-backend struct>
 :model 'claude-opus-4-5
 :temperature 1.0
 :tools (<gptel-tool struct> <gptel-tool struct>)
 :system "message text...")
```

Verification:
- Tangle and validate
- Test parse with valid .md preset (check structure matches expected)
- Test parse with invalid YAML (should return nil + error log)
- Test resolve with valid parsed plist (check types are correct)
- Test resolve with unknown backend (should signal error)

Context: design.md § Decision 1: Three-phase architecture (Parse → Resolve → Apply)

title: CRITICAL: Remove legacy shell_commands code (clean break)
description:
## Problem

shell_commands section and validator exist but are NEVER USED. This is legacy code from before bash_tools refactor. The change was intended as a clean break, but the old code wasn't removed.

## Evidence

1. **No tools use command validation:**
   - Searched tool categories: NO tool has `:validation command`
   - Only `run_bash_command` exists with `:validation bash`

2. **Validator is unreachable:**
   - `jf/gptel-scope--validate-command-tool` exists (scope-core.el:516-557)
   - Dispatcher has case for 'command (line 852)
   - But no tool triggers this path → DEAD CODE

3. **Marked deprecated:**
   - scope-core.org line 353: "shell_commands section for shell command tools (deprecated)"

4. **Still in all configs:**
   - All scope profiles have `shell_commands:` sections
   - scope.yml files include shell_commands
   - Confusing: which system to use?

## Impact

MEDIUM - Code clutter, maintenance burden, confusion about correct approach

## Removal Plan

### Phase 1: Remove code
- [ ] config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org
  - Remove "Command-Based Validator" section
  - Remove `jf/gptel-scope--validate-command-tool` function
  - Update documentation (line 353)
- [ ] config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el
  - Tangle to remove function
  - Remove dispatcher case (line 852: `('command ...)`)
- [ ] config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.{org,el}
  - Remove `jf/gptel-scope--add-command-to-scope` function
  - Remove routing case for command validation

### Phase 2: Remove from configs
- [ ] config/gptel/scope-profiles/*.yml
  - Remove `shell_commands:` sections from all profiles
  - Keep only: paths, org_roam_patterns, bash_tools
- [ ] Update existing scope.yml files in sessions (if any)
  - Script to remove shell_commands from active sessions
  - Or document manual cleanup

### Phase 3: Update specs
- [ ] openspec/changes/scoped-bash-command-execution/specs/gptel/scope.md
  - Remove references to command validation type (if present)
- [ ] Update design.md if it mentions shell_commands

## Validation

1. Tangle all modified .org files
2. Check no references to shell_commands remain: `rg 'shell.?commands' config/gptel/scope/`
3. Verify scope profiles parse correctly
4. Test bash command execution still works
5. Check scope expansion UI doesn't offer command expansion option

## Related

This is a CLEAN BREAK change - no backwards compatibility needed. Users should migrate to bash_tools (category-based validation).

title: Clarify nested command substitution comment
description:
Comment on line 430 misleadingly claims the regex pattern handles nested command substitutions, but the actual nesting support comes from the iterative loop, not the regex.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.el (line 430)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (corresponding section)

Current comment (MISLEADING):
Line 430: Use non-greedy match [^)$]+ to stop at nested $( and avoid matching outer substitution

Why misleading:
The regex stops at first ) or $, which BREAKS nested matching in theory:
  $(dirname $(pwd))
  Pattern [^)$]+ stops at the nested $(

But nested substitution still works because of the LOOP (lines 424-498), which processes innermost-first, not because of the regex.

Implementation:
Replace misleading comment with accurate explanation.

Design rationale:
Accurate documentation is critical for maintainability. Future developers might waste time trying to understand why the regex should handle nesting or attempt to fix the regex to match the comment.

Verification:
1. Update comment in .org file
2. Tangle: ./bin/tangle-org.sh config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org
3. Run tests: make test-pattern PATTERN=^test-cmdsub
4. All tests pass (no behavior change)

This is documentation-only with zero code behavior impact.

title: Fix redirection extraction in bash parser command chains
description:
Redirections are not being extracted from commands in chains. 

Test case: test-corpus-variable-chain-003
Command: WORKSPACE=/workspace && cat $WORKSPACE/data.txt > $WORKSPACE/output.txt

Expected: 2 operations (positional arg read + redirection write)
Actual: 1 operation (only positional arg read)

The redirection extraction works for simple commands and the variable resolution works in chains, but when both are combined in a chain, the redirection is not extracted.

Root cause appears to be in how jf/bash-extract-operations-from-redirections handles commands from chain's all-commands list. The function has special handling for chains but may not be invoked correctly when called from jf/bash--extract-from-single-command.

Suggested fix: Review the call chain and ensure redirections are properly extracted from individual commands in chains.

title: Resolve command substitution in cd targets
description:
Implement static resolution of command substitutions in cd targets.

TESTS TO ENABLE (1 test):
- test-cd-with-command-substitution: cd $(dirname $FILE) && cat script.sh

FILES TO MODIFY:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.org (source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.el (tangled)

CURRENT STATUS:
Command substitution resolution already exists:
- jf/bash-resolve-pwd-substitution handles $(pwd)
- Generic command substitution parsing exists

IMPLEMENTATION:
Enhance jf/bash--extract-cd-target to statically evaluate command substitutions:

1. dirname command
   - cd $(dirname $FILE) where FILE=/path/to/script.sh
   - Statically evaluate: dirname → /path/to
   - Use string manipulation on resolved FILE value

2. basename command  
   - Less common in cd, but could appear
   - Extract basename from path

3. pwd command
   - Already handled by jf/bash-resolve-pwd-substitution
   - Returns current PWD from var-context

4. Complex substitutions
   - cd $(dirname $(which openspec))
   - May require multi-stage resolution
   - If can't resolve statically, return :unresolved

STATIC EVALUATION FUNCTIONS:
Create helper functions for static command evaluation:
- jf/bash--static-dirname (path) → directory part
- jf/bash--static-basename (path) → filename part
- jf/bash--static-which (command var-context) → path if resolvable

INTEGRATION:
In jf/bash--extract-cd-target, after variable resolution:
1. Check if target contains $(...)
2. Attempt static evaluation of substitution
3. If successful, use result as target
4. If not, return :unresolved

LIMITATIONS:
Only handle simple, deterministic commands:
- dirname, basename, pwd, realpath
- Complex runtime commands (find, ls) remain :unresolved

REFERENCE:
See bash-parser-variables.el existing substitution resolution.
Test file: test-directory-changing-commands.el

VERIFICATION:
After implementation, test-cd-with-command-substitution should pass.

title: Create metadata gathering module for file context
