title: Refactor filesystem extraction as plugin
description:
Wrap existing filesystem extraction logic in plugin protocol

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org (or new bash-parser-filesystem-plugin.org)

Implementation steps:
1. Create jf/bash-plugin-filesystem function:
   - Input: parsed command structure
   - Output: jf/bash-plugin-result struct or nil
2. Wrap existing jf/bash-extract-file-operations logic:
   - Reuse current extraction algorithms (commands database, redirection handling)
   - Keep all existing operation detection logic
3. Add token claiming logic:
   - Claim command-name token when command has file semantics
   - Claim positional-arg tokens for file paths
   - Claim flag tokens that control operations (e.g., sed -i)
   - Claim redirection tokens and their file paths
   - Build claimed-token-ids list from extraction
4. Return plugin result structure:
   - :domain :filesystem
   - :operations (existing operations list)
   - :claimed-token-ids (newly added)
   - :metadata (can be empty or include extractor version)
5. Register plugin with high priority (e.g., 100):
   ```elisp
   (jf/bash-register-plugin
     :name 'filesystem
     :priority 100
     :extractor #'jf/bash-plugin-filesystem
     :predicates '())  ; Universal - always check for file ops
   ```
6. Write tests to verify plugin behavior matches old extraction
7. Add token claiming tests

Design rationale:
Dogfooding - proves plugin architecture works for complex domain. Filesystem is most mature extraction logic, so refactoring as plugin validates protocol sufficiency. No special cases in orchestrator - filesystem treated like any other plugin. Sets pattern for future plugins. Migration strategy preserves existing logic while adding plugin protocol wrapper.

Design pattern:
Plugin wraps existing extraction, adds token claiming:

```elisp
(defun jf/bash-plugin-filesystem (parsed-command)
  "Extract filesystem operations as plugin."
  (let* ((operations (extract-file-operations-internal parsed-command))
         (claimed-ids (identify-claimed-tokens operations parsed-command)))
    (when operations
      (make-jf/bash-plugin-result
        :domain :filesystem
        :operations operations
        :claimed-token-ids claimed-ids
        :metadata '(:extractor-version "2.0")))))
```

Token claiming algorithm:
- Start with empty claimed list
- For each operation, find corresponding tokens:
  - Read operation from positional arg → claim :command-name and :positional-arg tokens
  - Redirection operation → claim :redirection token and file path token
  - Flag-dependent operation → claim :flag token and :flag-arg tokens
- Return unique list of claimed token IDs

Verification:
- Test: Plugin returns nil for non-file commands (echo hello)
- Test: Plugin extracts same operations as old logic for "cat file.txt"
- Test: Plugin claims command-name and file path tokens
- Test: Plugin claims redirection tokens for "echo > output.txt"
- Test: Plugin claims flag tokens for flag-dependent operations (sed -i)
- Test: Plugin works in orchestrator with other plugins
- Test: All existing filesystem extraction tests pass through plugin
- Spec coverage: bash-file-operations/spec.md § "Filesystem plugin implementation" and "Token claiming algorithm" scenarios

Context: design.md § Decision 7 (Filesystem Plugin as First-Class Plugin), Migration Plan Phase 3
close_reason: Implemented via sequential orchestrator. Filesystem plugin created. All 741 tests pass.

title: Implement semantic extraction orchestrator
description:
Implement jf/bash-extract-semantics orchestrator with plugin execution

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-plugins.org

Implementation steps:
1. Implement jf/bash-extract-semantics function:
   - Input: parsed command structure (with :tokens, :parse-complete)
   - Output: semantic analysis result with :domains, :coverage, :plugin-results
2. Predicate filtering: evaluate plugin predicates to determine applicability
   - If predicate list empty, plugin always runs
   - If any predicate returns nil, skip plugin
3. Plugin invocation with error isolation:
   - Wrap each plugin call in condition-case
   - Log errors but continue with remaining plugins
   - Collect results from successful plugins
4. Result aggregation:
   - Group operations by domain into :domains alist
   - Collect all claimed-token-ids for coverage calculation
   - Include raw :plugin-results for debugging
5. Integration with coverage calculation (call jf/bash-calculate-coverage)
6. Add :parse-complete pass-through from parsed input
7. Write tests for orchestration logic, error isolation, aggregation

Design rationale:
Single plugin failure shouldn't break entire extraction - partial results better than no results. Predicate filtering reduces unnecessary plugin invocations. Error isolation via condition-case prevents propagation while logging issues for debugging. Compositional semantics: multiple plugins can claim same token (e.g., scp is both network AND filesystem), so shared claiming is intentional.

Design pattern:
Orchestrator runs applicable plugins and aggregates results:

```elisp
(defun jf/bash-extract-semantics (parsed-command)
  "Extract semantics using all applicable plugins."
  (let ((plugin-results '())
        (all-claimed-ids '()))
    ;; Filter and execute plugins
    (dolist (plugin (get-applicable-plugins parsed-command))
      (condition-case err
          (when-let ((result (funcall (plist-get plugin :extractor) parsed-command)))
            (push result plugin-results)
            (setq all-claimed-ids 
                  (append all-claimed-ids 
                          (jf/bash-plugin-result-claimed-token-ids result))))
        (error
         (message "Plugin %s error: %s" (plist-get plugin :name) err))))
    
    ;; Aggregate by domain
    (let ((domains (aggregate-by-domain plugin-results))
          (coverage (jf/bash-calculate-coverage 
                     (plist-get parsed-command :tokens)
                     all-claimed-ids)))
      (list :parse-complete (plist-get parsed-command :parse-complete)
            :domains domains
            :coverage coverage
            :plugin-results plugin-results))))
```

Predicate evaluation:
```elisp
(defun applicable-p (plugin parsed-command)
  (let ((predicates (plist-get plugin :predicates)))
    (or (null predicates)  ; No predicates = always applicable
        (seq-every-p (lambda (pred) (funcall pred parsed-command))
                     predicates))))
```

Verification:
- Test: Orchestrator runs all applicable plugins
- Test: Predicate filtering skips non-applicable plugins
- Test: Empty predicate list makes plugin universal
- Test: Plugin error doesn't break extraction (error isolation)
- Test: Partial results returned when some plugins fail
- Test: Multiple plugins can claim same tokens (shared claiming)
- Test: Result includes :domains, :coverage, :parse-complete, :plugin-results
- Spec coverage: bash-parser-plugins/spec.md § "Plugin predicate filtering", "Semantic orchestration", "Plugin error isolation", "Shared token claiming" scenarios

Context: design.md § Decision 2 (Plugin Registry), Decision 3 (Shared Token Claiming), Decision 9 (Error Isolation via Condition-Case)
close_reason: Implemented via sequential orchestrator. Semantic extraction orchestrator created. All 730 tests pass.

title: Semantics test expecting simple operations format
description:
Test: test-semantics-lookup-command-with-skip-rules

   (let* ((semantics (jf/bash-lookup-command-semantics "command-name"))

title: Remove category validation from pipeline commands
description:
Remove category-based validation from jf/gptel-scope--validate-pipeline-commands, keeping only deny list validation.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.org

Implementation steps:
1. Locate jf/gptel-scope--validate-pipeline-commands function
2. Remove category membership checking logic (read_only, safe_write, dangerous checks)
3. Keep only deny list validation against bash_tools.deny
4. Simplify function to check each pipeline command against deny list
5. Return command_denied error if command in deny list, nil otherwise
6. Update function docstring to reflect deny-list-only validation

Design rationale: Category-based validation (read_only, safe_write, dangerous) is obsolete with operation-first validation. Commands are now validated via (1) deny list check for always-dangerous commands, (2) no-op allowance for commands with no file operations, (3) file operation validation for commands with operations. This simplifies the validation logic and removes the maintenance burden of categorizing every command.

Design pattern: Deny-list-only validation
```elisp
(defun jf/gptel-scope--validate-pipeline-commands (commands scope-config)
  "Check pipeline commands against deny list only. Returns error or nil."
  (let ((deny-list (plist-get (plist-get scope-config :bash-tools) :deny)))
    (cl-loop for cmd in commands
             for position from 0
             when (member cmd deny-list)
             return (list :error "command_denied"
                         :command cmd
                         :position position
                         :message (format "Command '%s' at position %d is in deny list" cmd position)))))
```

Verification:
- Test: Commands not in deny list pass validation
- Test: Commands in deny list fail with command_denied error
- Test: Pipeline commands checked independently (ls | xargs rm → fails on rm)
- Test: Position reported correctly in error
- No references to categories remain in function
- Integration tests still pass

Context: design.md § Decision 2: Remove Categories Entirely from Schema

title: Fix variable resolution metadata format and context application
description:
## Problem

Six variable-related corpus tests are failing due to incorrect metadata format and missing variable resolution:

**Metadata format mismatch:**
- Current: :unresolved ("VAR")  
- Expected: :unresolved t :unresolved-vars ("VAR")

**Missing variable resolution:**
- test-corpus-variable-002: cat $FILE with FILE=/workspace/data.txt in context
  - Expected: (:file "/workspace/data.txt" :operation :read)
  - Actual: (:file "$FILE" :operation :read :unresolved ("FILE"))
  
- test-corpus-variable-004: rm ${TEMP_DIR}/file.txt with TEMP_DIR=/tmp
  - Expected: (:file "/tmp/file.txt" :operation :delete)  
  - Actual: (:file "${TEMP_DIR}/file.txt" :operation :delete :unresolved ("TEMP_DIR"))

**Partial resolution not working:**
- test-corpus-variable-005: cp $SRC/$FILE $DEST/ with SRC=/workspace resolved
  - Expected: (:file "/workspace/$FILE" :unresolved t :unresolved-vars ("FILE"))
  - Actual: (:file "$SRC/$FILE" :unresolved ("SRC" "FILE"))

**Chain context not propagating:**
- test-corpus-variable-chain-003: FILE=/workspace/data.txt; cat $FILE > $FILE.out
  - Expected: Both operations use resolved path /workspace/data.txt
  - Actual: Only first operation resolved

## Resolution

Update variable handling in config/experiments/bash-parser/variable-resolution.el:

1. Change metadata format: :unresolved → :unresolved t :unresolved-vars  
2. Apply variable context during parsing (not just track unresolved vars)
3. Support partial resolution (resolve some vars, mark others unresolved)
4. Propagate variable assignments through command chains
5. Update tests in test/test-variable-resolution.el to verify all scenarios

title: Update scope core to load config from scope.yml
description:
Update scope core to load config from scope.yml

## Files to modify
- `config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org`

## Implementation steps

1. Update `jf/gptel-scope--load-config` to read `scope.yml`:
   - Read entire file content and pass to `yaml-parse-string` directly (NO frontmatter extraction — `scope.yml` is plain YAML)
   - This differs from the current `jf/gptel-scope--parse-preset-config` which extracts YAML from between `---` delimiters in `preset.md`
2. Apply snake_case → kebab-case key normalization after parsing:
   - `:org_roam_patterns` → `:org-roam-patterns`
   - `:shell_commands` → `:shell-commands`
   - Reuse or call the same normalization logic from preset-registration module
3. Add legacy fallback in `jf/gptel-scope--load-config`:
   - First try: read `scope.yml` from `jf/gptel--branch-dir`
   - If `scope.yml` doesn't exist: check for `preset.md` in same directory
   - If `preset.md` exists: read scope config using frontmatter extraction (existing `jf/gptel-scope--parse-preset-config` logic)
   - Log deprecation warning: "Loading scope from legacy preset.md — session will be migrated on next open"
   - If neither exists: return nil (triggers "no_scope_config" error to tool)
4. Update path resolution to use `jf/gptel--scope-file-path` from filesystem module (uses constant, no hardcoded filename)
5. Ensure `gptel-make-scoped-tool` macro still works — only the config source changes, not the validation logic
6. Tangle and validate with `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org`

## Design rationale

`scope.yml` is plain YAML — simpler input preparation than preset.md's frontmatter extraction. The scope validation logic (path matching, pattern matching, command matching) is entirely unchanged. Only the "where to read config from" changes. Legacy fallback ensures existing sessions with preset.md still have working scope enforcement during migration.

## Verification

- `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org` passes
- Scope validation works from `scope.yml` (tool calls checked against paths/patterns/commands)
- Legacy sessions with `preset.md` still validate correctly (with deprecation warning in *Messages*)
- Sessions with neither file get "no_scope_config" error
- `gptel-make-scoped-tool` macro works unchanged with new loader

Context: design.md § Migration Phase 4, scope.md

title: Improve unhandled operations denial messaging
description:
Add clear denial reason messaging when commands are rejected due to unhandled operations.

**Problem:**
When operations are marked as unhandled (low confidence, unresolved variables, warn policy), the command is denied with :allowed nil but users get no clear explanation of WHY. The result structure has :unhandled list but no top-level indication that unhandled operations caused the denial.

Current behavior:
  {:allowed nil
   :violations ()
   :unhandled ({:file "/path" :reason "Low confidence operation detection"})}

User sees: Command denied, but has to inspect :unhandled list to understand why.

**Files to modify:**
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-security.el (line 244)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-security.org (corresponding section)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-security-validator.el (add verification)

**Implementation steps:**

1. Add :denial-reason field to result plist (line 244):
   (list :allowed (and (null violations) (null unhandled))
         :command command-string
         :operations operations
         :violations (nreverse violations)
         :unhandled (nreverse unhandled)
         :cd-detected cd-detected
         :denial-reason (cond
                         (cd-detected "cd command prohibited")
                         (violations "security violations")
                         (unhandled "unhandled operations (fail-secure)")
                         (t nil)))

2. Update docstring to document new field:
   "Returns validation result plist:
     :allowed - t if command is safe, nil if denied
     :command - Original command string (for reference)
     :operations - All extracted file operations (for debugging)
     :violations - List of security violations (non-empty => denied)
     :unhandled - Operations that couldn't be validated
     :cd-detected - t if cd command was detected
     :denial-reason - String explaining why command was denied (nil if allowed)"

3. Add test to verify denial reason is present:
   (ert-deftest test-security-denial-reason-for-unhandled ()
     "Test that denial reason is provided when unhandled operations exist"
     (let ((rules \='((:patterns ("/workspace/**") :operations (:read)))))
       ;; Use command with low confidence operation
       (let ((result (jf/bash-sandbox-check "cat /file.txt" rules)))
         (should-not (plist-get result :allowed))
         (should (plist-get result :unhandled))
         (should (equal "unhandled operations (fail-secure)" (plist-get result :denial-reason))))))

4. Tangle and validate: ./bin/tangle-org.sh bash-parser-security.org

**Design rationale:**
Fail-secure behavior (denying commands with unhandled operations) is correct, but users need clear feedback about WHY denial occurred. The :denial-reason field provides a single, top-level explanation that categorizes denial types:

1. cd-detected - cd commands are prohibited (policy decision)
2. violations - security rules explicitly deny operation
3. unhandled - cannot verify safety, fail secure

This improves user experience by avoiding the need to inspect multiple fields to understand denial. It's especially important for automated systems that need to present clear error messages to end users.

**Verification:**
- Run: make test-security-validator
- Verify new test passes
- Check denial reasons for different scenarios:
  * cd command → :denial-reason is "cd command prohibited"
  * violation → :denial-reason is "security violations"
  * unhandled → :denial-reason is "unhandled operations (fail-secure)"
  * allowed → :denial-reason is nil
- Existing tests should still pass (39 tests total)

title: Create command library directory with example commands
description:
**Summary:** Create config/gptel/command-library/ directory with example commands demonstrating YAML frontmatter format

**Problem:** Bead requirement and spec both require example commands in repo-level command library, but directory doesn't exist.

**Files to create:**
- config/gptel/command-library/opsx/explore.md
- config/gptel/command-library/opsx/new.md
- config/gptel/command-library/opsx/continue.md

**Implementation:**

1. Create directory structure:
   ```bash
   mkdir -p config/gptel/command-library/opsx
   ```

2. Create config/gptel/command-library/opsx/explore.md:
   ```yaml
   ---
   name: opsx:explore
   description: Enter explore mode - think through ideas, investigate problems
   category: OpenSpec
   tags: [workflow, planning, exploration]
   ---
   
   Enter explore mode - a thinking partner for exploring ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements before implementation.
   
   Use this when you want to:
   - Think through a problem before coding
   - Investigate architectural options
   - Clarify requirements and edge cases
   - Explore design alternatives
   
   {{ args }}
   ```

3. Create config/gptel/command-library/opsx/new.md:
   ```yaml
   ---
   name: opsx:new
   description: Start a new OpenSpec change with structured artifacts
   category: OpenSpec
   tags: [workflow, planning, change-management]
   ---
   
   Start a new OpenSpec change: {{ args }}
   
   Create a new change with step-by-step artifact workflow (proposal → design → tasks → implementation).
   ```

4. Create config/gptel/command-library/opsx/continue.md:
   ```yaml
   ---
   name: opsx:continue
   description: Continue working on current OpenSpec change
   category: OpenSpec
   tags: [workflow, change-management]
   ---
   
   Continue working on the current OpenSpec change by creating the next artifact in the workflow.
   
   {{ args }}
   ```

5. Test discovery:
   - M-: (gptel-commands-refresh-registry)
   - M-: gptel--command-registry
   - Should contain opsx:explore, opsx:new, opsx:continue

6. Test expansion:
   - Type /opsx:explore in gptel buffer
   - Should expand with content from explore.md

**Verification:**
- Directory config/gptel/command-library/ exists
- At least 3 example commands with proper YAML frontmatter
- Commands discovered by registry refresh
- Commands expand correctly with {{ args }} substitution
- Tooltip shows description from frontmatter

**Context:** Verification report Critical Issue 3, design.md Migration Plan, bead emacs-d75 requirements

title: Phase 1: Critical unit tests for scope validation
description:
Implement 50-60 critical unit tests for scope validation helper functions, validators, and configuration management.

## Context

The scope validation system has 13 completely untested unit-testable functions covering pattern matching, permission checking, and validation dispatch. These functions are security-critical and need isolated unit tests with mocking. This builds on the test infrastructure from emacs-kebn.

Unit tests provide:
- Fast feedback (milliseconds vs seconds for integration tests)
- Isolated function logic testing
- Clear documentation of edge cases
- Confident refactoring capability

All new tests use Buttercup framework (describe/it syntax) and shared test helpers from test-scope-helpers.

## Files to Create

config/gptel/tools/test/unit/
├── helpers-spec.el        (30 tests - pattern matching, normalization)
├── validators-spec.el     (25 tests - permission validators, error formatting)
└── config-spec.el         (10 tests - allow-once, tool categories)

## Implementation Steps

### 1. Create helpers-spec.el (30 tests)

Test pattern matching and helper functions:

```elisp
;;; helpers-spec.el --- Unit tests for scope validation helpers -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

(require 'buttercup)
(require 'test-scope-helpers)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-shell-tools)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-core)

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern"
  (describe "with exact paths"
    (it "matches exact path"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file.txt" "/workspace/file.txt")
              :to-be t))
    
    (it "rejects different path"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file.txt" "/workspace/other.txt")
              :to-be nil)))
  
  (describe "with single-level wildcards"
    (it "matches files in directory"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file.txt" "/workspace/*")
              :to-be t))
    
    (it "rejects files in subdirectories"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/sub/file.txt" "/workspace/*")
              :to-be nil)))
  
  (describe "with recursive wildcards"
    (it "matches files at any depth"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/deep/nested/file.txt" "/workspace/**")
              :to-be t))
    
    (it "matches files in root directory"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file.txt" "/workspace/**")
              :to-be t)))
  
  (describe "with middle wildcards"
    (it "matches /workspace/**/file.txt patterns"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/sub/file.txt" "/workspace/**/file.txt")
              :to-be t)
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/a/b/file.txt" "/workspace/**/file.txt")
              :to-be t)
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file.txt" "/workspace/**/file.txt")
              :to-be t)))
  
  (describe "edge cases"
    (it "handles dotfiles"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/.hidden" "/workspace/*")
              :to-be t))
    
    (it "is case-sensitive"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/File.txt" "/workspace/file.txt")
              :to-be nil))
    
    (it "handles special characters in filenames"
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file-name.txt" "/workspace/*")
              :to-be t)
      (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-pattern "/workspace/file_name.txt" "/workspace/*")
              :to-be t))))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern"
  (it "returns t if any pattern matches"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern "/workspace/file.txt"
                                                 '("/tmp/**" "/workspace/**"))
            :to-be t))
  
  (it "returns nil if no patterns match"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern "/etc/passwd"
                                                 '("/tmp/**" "/workspace/**"))
            :to-be nil))
  
  (it "handles empty pattern list"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern "/workspace/file.txt" nil)
            :to-be nil))
  
  (it "checks patterns in order and stops at first match"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern "/workspace/file.txt"
                                                 '("/workspace/**" "/workspace/file.txt"))
            :to-be t)))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--glob-to-regex"
  (it "converts exact paths"
    (let ((regex (jf/gptel-scope--glob-to-regex "/workspace/file.txt")))
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspace/file.txt") :not :to-be nil)
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspace/other.txt") :to-be nil)))
  
  (it "converts single-level wildcards"
    (let ((regex (jf/gptel-scope--glob-to-regex "/workspace/*")))
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspace/file.txt") :not :to-be nil)
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspace/sub/file.txt") :to-be nil)))
  
  (it "converts recursive wildcards"
    (let ((regex (jf/gptel-scope--glob-to-regex "/workspace/**")))
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspace/file.txt") :not :to-be nil)
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspace/a/b/c/file.txt") :not :to-be nil)))
  
  (it "escapes regex special characters"
    (let ((regex (jf/gptel-scope--glob-to-regex "/workspace/file.txt")))
      (expect (string-match-p regex "/workspacexfile.txt") :to-be nil))))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--normalize-args"
  (it "converts vectors to lists"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-args ["a" "b" "c"])
            :to-equal '("a" "b" "c")))
  
  (it "leaves lists unchanged"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-args '("a" "b" "c"))
            :to-equal '("a" "b" "c")))
  
  (it "handles empty vectors"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-args [])
            :to-equal nil))
  
  (it "handles nested vectors"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--normalize-args ["a" ["b" "c"]])
            :to-equal '("a" ["b" "c"]))))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--infer-validation-type"
  (it "returns path validation for read_file"
    (expect (plist-get (jf/gptel-scope--infer-validation-type "read_file") :validation)
            :to-equal 'path))
  
  (it "returns bash validation for run_bash_command"
    (expect (plist-get (jf/gptel-scope--infer-validation-type "run_bash_command") :validation)
            :to-equal 'bash))
  
  (it "returns pattern validation for create_roam_node_in_scope"
    (expect (plist-get (jf/gptel-scope--infer-validation-type "create_roam_node_in_scope") :validation)
            :to-equal 'pattern))
  
  (it "returns meta validation for request_scope_expansion"
    (expect (plist-get (jf/gptel-scope--infer-validation-type "request_scope_expansion") :validation)
            :to-equal 'meta))
  
  (it "returns nil for unknown tools"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--infer-validation-type "unknown_tool")
            :to-be nil)))

(describe "jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths"
  (it "detects absolute paths in arguments"
    (expect (jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths "grep pattern /etc/passwd")
            :to-match "absolute path"))
  
  (it "returns nil for relative paths only"
    (expect (jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths "grep pattern ./file.txt")
            :to-be nil))
  
  (it "detects multiple absolute paths"
    (expect (jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths "cp /tmp/source /etc/dest")
            :to-match "absolute path"))
  
  (it "handles commands without paths"
    (expect (jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths "ls -la")
            :to-be nil)))

(provide 'unit-helpers-spec)
```

### 2. Create validators-spec.el (25 tests)

Test validation and permission checking functions:

```elisp
;;; validators-spec.el --- Unit tests for scope validators -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

(require 'buttercup)
(require 'test-scope-helpers)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-shell-tools)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-core)

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool"
  (let ((config (test-scope--make-test-config
                 :paths-read '("/workspace/**")
                 :paths-write '("/tmp/**")
                 :paths-deny '("/etc/**"))))
    
    (describe "with read operations"
      (it "allows read from read paths"
        (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool
                       "read_file" '("/workspace/file.txt")
                       '(:validation path :operation read)
                       config)))
          (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
      
      (it "allows read from write paths (write includes read)"
        (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool
                       "read_file" '("/tmp/file.txt")
                       '(:validation path :operation read)
                       config)))
          (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
      
      (it "denies read from denied paths"
        (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool
                       "read_file" '("/etc/passwd")
                       '(:validation path :operation read)
                       config)))
          (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be nil)
          (expect (plist-get result :reason) :to-equal "denied-pattern"))))
    
    (describe "with write operations"
      (it "allows write to write paths"
        (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool
                       "write_file_in_scope" '("/tmp/output.txt")
                       '(:validation path :operation write)
                       config)))
          (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
      
      (it "denies write to read-only paths"
        (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool
                       "write_file_in_scope" '("/workspace/file.txt")
                       '(:validation path :operation write)
                       config)))
          (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be nil)
          (expect (plist-get result :reason) :to-equal "not-in-scope"))))))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool"
  (let ((config (test-scope--make-test-config
                 :paths-read '("/workspace/**")
                 :paths-write '("/tmp/**")
                 :bash-tools '(:categories (:read-only (:commands ("ls" "grep"))
                                           :safe-write (:commands ("mkdir" "touch"))
                                           :dangerous (:commands ()))
                              :deny ("rm" "mv" "chmod")))))
    
    (it "allows read_only commands in read paths"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool
                     "run_bash_command" '("ls -la" "/workspace")
                     config)))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
    
    (it "allows safe_write commands in write paths"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool
                     "run_bash_command" '("mkdir foo" "/tmp")
                     config)))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
    
    (it "denies commands in deny list"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool
                     "run_bash_command" '("rm file.txt" "/workspace")
                     config)))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be nil)
        (expect (plist-get result :reason) :to-equal "denied-command")))
    
    (it "denies write commands in read-only paths"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool
                     "run_bash_command" '("mkdir foo" "/workspace")
                     config)))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be nil)
        (expect (plist-get result :reason) :to-equal "directory-not-in-scope")))))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission"
  (let ((config (test-scope--make-test-config
                 :paths-read '("/workspace/**"))))
    
    (it "allows meta tools without checking"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission
                     config "request_scope_expansion" '("run_bash_command" ["tree"] "Need tree"))))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
    
    (it "routes path tools to path validator"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission
                     config "read_file" '("/workspace/file.txt"))))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be t)))
    
    (it "routes bash tools to bash validator"
      (spy-on 'jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool :and-return-value '(:allowed t))
      (jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission
       config "run_bash_command" '("ls" "/workspace"))
      (expect 'jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool :to-have-been-called))
    
    (it "denies unknown tools"
      (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--check-tool-permission
                     config "unknown_tool" '("arg1"))))
        (expect (plist-get result :allowed) :to-be nil)
        (expect (plist-get result :reason) :to-equal "unknown-tool")))))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error"
  (it "formats basic error with message"
    (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error
                   "read_file" "/etc/passwd"
                   '(:allowed nil :reason "denied-pattern"))))
      (expect (plist-get result :success) :to-be nil)
      (expect (plist-get result :error) :to-equal "denied-pattern")
      (expect (plist-get result :tool) :to-equal "read_file")
      (expect (plist-get result :resource) :to-equal "/etc/passwd")))
  
  (it "includes allowed patterns when available"
    (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error
                   "read_file" "/etc/passwd"
                   '(:allowed nil :reason "not-in-scope"
                     :allowed-patterns ("/workspace/**" "/tmp/**")))))
      (expect (plist-get result :allowed-patterns)
              :to-equal '("/workspace/**" "/tmp/**"))))
  
  (it "includes bash-specific fields"
    (let ((result (jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error
                   "run_bash_command" "rm file.txt:/workspace"
                   '(:allowed nil :reason "denied-command"
                     :command "rm file.txt"
                     :directory "/workspace"
                     :required-scope "write"))))
      (expect (plist-get result :command) :to-equal "rm file.txt")
      (expect (plist-get result :directory) :to-equal "/workspace")
      (expect (plist-get result :required-scope) :to-equal "write"))))

(provide 'unit-validators-spec)
```

### 3. Create config-spec.el (10 tests)

Test configuration and state management:

```elisp
;;; config-spec.el --- Unit tests for scope configuration -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

(require 'buttercup)
(require 'test-scope-helpers)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-core)

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--check-allow-once"
  (before-each
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list nil))
  
  (it "returns t and consumes permission when found"
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list
          '(("read_file" . "/workspace/file.txt")))
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--check-allow-once
             "read_file" '("/workspace/file.txt") nil)
            :to-be t)
    (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-equal nil))
  
  (it "returns nil when tool name doesn't match"
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list
          '(("read_file" . "/workspace/file.txt")))
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--check-allow-once
             "write_file_in_scope" '("/workspace/file.txt") nil)
            :to-be nil)
    (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-have-length-of 1))
  
  (it "returns nil when resource doesn't match"
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list
          '(("read_file" . "/workspace/file.txt")))
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--check-allow-once
             "read_file" '("/workspace/other.txt") nil)
            :to-be nil)
    (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-have-length-of 1))
  
  (it "handles bash tool composite resources (command:directory)"
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list
          '(("run_bash_command" . "ls -la:/workspace")))
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--check-allow-once
             "run_bash_command" '("ls -la" "/workspace") nil)
            :to-be t))
  
  (it "returns nil when list is empty"
    (expect (jf/gptel-scope--check-allow-once
             "read_file" '("/workspace/file.txt") nil)
            :to-be nil)))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope-add-to-allow-once-list"
  (before-each
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list nil))
  
  (it "adds entry to allow-once list"
    (jf/gptel-scope-add-to-allow-once-list "read_file" "/workspace/file.txt")
    (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list
            :to-equal '(("read_file" . "/workspace/file.txt"))))
  
  (it "adds multiple entries"
    (jf/gptel-scope-add-to-allow-once-list "read_file" "/workspace/file1.txt")
    (jf/gptel-scope-add-to-allow-once-list "read_file" "/workspace/file2.txt")
    (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-have-length-of 2)))

(describe "jf/gptel-scope--clear-allow-once"
  (it "clears allow-once list"
    (setq jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list
          '(("read_file" . "/workspace/file.txt")))
    (jf/gptel-scope--clear-allow-once)
    (expect jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-list :to-be nil)))

(provide 'unit-config-spec)
```

## Design Rationale

**Focus on security-critical functions:**
Pattern matching and validation are the core security boundary. Unit tests ensure these work correctly in isolation.

**Mock external dependencies:**
- Pattern matching tests use real file paths (fast, no mocking needed)
- Validator tests mock bash-parser and file system
- Configuration tests use temporary variables (no persistence)

**Buttercup syntax advantages:**
- describe/it reads naturally: "matches-pattern with exact paths matches exact path"
- before-each reduces setup boilerplate
- expect syntax clearer than should/should-not

**Test organization:**
- helpers-spec.el: Pure functions, no side effects
- validators-spec.el: Functions with dependencies, need mocking
- config-spec.el: Stateful functions, need setup/teardown

**Edge case coverage:**
Each function tests:
- Happy path (expected inputs)
- Edge cases (empty, nil, boundary values)
- Error conditions (invalid inputs, denied operations)
- Security scenarios (deny precedence, path escaping)

## Verification

After completing unit tests:

1. **All tests pass:**
```bash
make test-unit
# Should run 60+ tests, all passing
```

2. **Test files exist:**
```bash
ls -la config/gptel/tools/test/unit/
# Should show: helpers-spec.el, validators-spec.el, config-spec.el
```

3. **Test count matches:**
```bash
./bin/run-tests.sh -t unit | grep -c "it "
# Should show ~60
```

4. **Tests run fast:**
```bash
time make test-unit
# Should complete in < 5 seconds (unit tests are fast)
```

5. **Coverage improved:**
Check testing-gap-analysis.md metrics:
- Before: 22/35 functions tested (63%)
- After: 30/35 functions tested (86%)

## Dependencies

Requires: emacs-kebn (test infrastructure setup)

## Follow-Up Work

- Phase 2: Integration module tests (emacs-xxxx) - to be created
- Phase 3: Behavioral workflow tests (emacs-yyyy) - to be created

title: Investigate tree-sitter-bash (( parsing limitation
description:
CONTEXT:
We implemented a workaround in bash-parser-core.org that pre-processes commands starting with (( to add a space: (( → ( (

The workaround addresses a tree-sitter-bash parsing issue where (( at the start of a command produces an ERROR node instead of being recognized as nested subshells.

INVESTIGATION GOALS:
1. Understand tree-sitter-bash grammar behavior with (( syntax
2. Determine if this is a grammar bug or intentional behavior
3. Test edge cases: ((cmd)), $((expr)), ((( triple parens
4. Review tree-sitter-bash grammar source and issue tracker
5. If it's a bug, consider contributing a fix upstream

DELIVERABLES:
- Document findings in bash-parser/docs/tree-sitter-bash-investigation.md
- Include test cases showing the behavior
- Recommendation: keep workaround, fix upstream, or alternative approach
- If upstream fix possible, create issue/PR in tree-sitter-bash repo

BENEFITS:
- Cleaner codebase without pre-processing hacks
- Better understanding of parser limitations
- Contribution to open source if bug confirmed
- Proper handling of edge cases

CURRENT WORKAROUND:
File: config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org
Function: jf/bash-parse--internal
Lines: Pre-processing block that detects and normalizes ((

RESOURCES:
- tree-sitter-bash repo: https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-bash
- Grammar file: grammar.js
- Test corpus: test/corpus/
- Our test: test-nested-subshells in test-directory-changing-commands.el

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Documented understanding of (( parsing behavior
- Decision on whether to pursue upstream fix
- If bug confirmed, issue filed in tree-sitter-bash
- Documentation explains why workaround exists or when it can be removed

title: Extraction from Exec Blocks
description:
Extract file operations from find -exec blocks by recursive extraction.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "File Operations Extraction - Exec Blocks"
2. Implement `jf/bash-extract-operations-from-exec-blocks` function
3. Extract `:exec-blocks` field from parsed find command
4. For each exec block:
   - Extract the nested command structure
   - Recursively extract operations from nested command
   - Mark operations with exec block metadata
5. Handle multiple -exec blocks in single find command
6. Combine find's directory operations (:read-directory) with exec operations

Design rationale:
Parser already extracts exec blocks as structured data. Treat each exec block as separate command and recursively extract operations. This reuses extraction logic for nested commands. Find operations (directory traversal) + exec operations (actions on matched files) both contribute to total file operations.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-extract-operations-from-exec-blocks (parsed-command)
  "Extract file operations from find -exec blocks recursively."
  (let ((exec-blocks (plist-get parsed-command :exec-blocks))
        (operations nil))
    (dolist (exec-block exec-blocks)
      (let* ((exec-command (plist-get exec-block :command))
             ;; Recursively extract from nested command
             (exec-ops (jf/bash-extract-operations-from-positional-args exec-command)))
        (dolist (op exec-ops)
          (push (plist-put op :exec-block t) operations))))
    (nreverse operations)))
```

Verification:
- "find . -name '*.log' -exec rm {} \;" extracts:
  - (:file "." :operation :read-directory) from find
  - (:file "{}" :operation :delete :exec-block t) from rm
- "find /tmp -exec cat {} \; -exec rm {} \;" handles multiple exec blocks
- Test exec block scenarios from bash-file-operations/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 2: File Operation Extraction Architecture"

title: bash-parser-core: Missing require for bash-parser-protocol
description:
bash-parser-core.el uses forward declarations from bash-parser-protocol.el but doesn't explicitly require it, relying on transitive loading through bash-parser-security.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.org

```elisp
#+begin_src emacs-lisp
(require 'treesit)
(require 'bash-parser-security)
#+end_src
```

Should be:
```elisp
#+begin_src emacs-lisp
(require 'treesit)
(require 'bash-parser-protocol)  ; Forward declarations

#+end_src
```

Design rationale:
The protocol module establishes forward declarations for cross-module interfaces. While bash-parser-core currently loads protocol transitively through security → glob → protocol, explicit requires make dependencies clear and prevent breakage if load order changes.

title: Implement dual-format file detection in auto-init hook
description:
Enable session auto-initialization for both .org and .md files.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/sessions/commands.org (function: jf/gptel--auto-init-session-buffer)

Implementation steps:
1. Add fast-path extension check: (or (string-suffix-p ".org" file-path) (string-suffix-p ".md" file-path))
2. Update branch session regex to match both: "\\(/branches/\\([^/]+\\)/session\\.\\(org\\|md\\)\$\\)"
3. Update agent session regex to match both: "\\(/agents/\\([^/]+\\)/session\\.\\(org\\|md\\)\$\\)"
4. Keep initialization logic format-agnostic (no format-specific branching yet)
5. Tangle commands.org to generate commands.el

Design rationale: Backward compatibility is critical. Existing markdown sessions must continue working without modification. The dual-format detection enables gradual migration - users with existing .md sessions can continue using them indefinitely, while new sessions use .org format. The fast-path check (extension first) prevents performance overhead for non-session files.

Design pattern: Union-based pattern matching. Instead of replacing .md patterns with .org patterns, the regex matches BOTH extensions using alternation (org\\|md). This single hook handles both formats transparently. The format-agnostic initialization logic means no conditional branching based on format - both are treated identically at this layer.

Verification:
- Tangle successfully
- Fast-path check exits early for non-session files
- Regex matches both /branches/main/session.org AND /branches/main/session.md
- Regex matches both /agents/foo/session.org AND /agents/foo/session.md
- Test with: (string-match "regex-here" "/path/to/session.org") returns match

Context: design.md § Decision 4 (Detect both formats), Migration Plan Phase 1

title: Corpus refinement: Remove non-file-impacting examples
description:
Review all bash-parser corpus files and remove test cases that have zero file impact and aren't representative of patterns that could impact files.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-command-substitution.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-combined-patterns.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-conditional.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-for-loop.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/corpus-parse-heredoc.el

Implementation steps:
1. Review corpus-parse-command-substitution.el:
   - Remove: cmdsub-simple-001 (echo $(pwd)), cmdsub-simple-002 (echo $(date)), cmdsub-simple-003 (echo $(whoami)) - pure output, no file impact
   - Keep: cmdsub-simple-004 (dir=$(pwd)) - represents variable assignment pattern that could store file paths
   - Remove: cmdsub-quoted-004 (single quotes prevent substitution) - not representative of real usage
   - Remove: cmdsub-multiple-001 through -003 - just echo/print operations
   - Keep: cmdsub-nested-003 (cat $(find...)) - shows file reading from substitution result
   - Document removed count in file header

2. Review corpus-parse-combined-patterns.el:
   - Keep all command-substitution + heredoc examples (category 1) - these show git commit patterns
   - Keep xargs patterns (category 6) - critical for batch file operations
   - Keep find -exec patterns (category 8) - show indirect file operations
   - Remove any pure informational commands (echo, pwd, date combinations)

3. Review corpus-parse-conditional.el:
   - Keep all file test operators (-f, -d, -e, -r, -w, -x) - directly check file properties
   - Already removed string/numeric tests - good
   - Keep command-based tests (grep -q) - reads files for conditions
   - Keep all real-world examples - they show actual file operations

4. Review corpus-parse-for-loop.el:
   - Remove: forloop-simple-001 through -002 if they're just echo loops with no file context
   - Keep: Any loops that iterate over files, perform file operations in body
   - Keep: forloop-real-* examples - extracted from actual usage
   - Keep: Loops with find/ls/glob patterns in iteration list

5. Review corpus-parse-heredoc.el:
   - Keep: heredocs with redirects (> file.txt) - creates files
   - Keep: heredocs in git commit context - shows common real-world pattern
   - Keep: heredocs piped to commands - shows data flow
   - Remove: Empty heredoc or trivial content examples that don't demonstrate file impact

6. Add summary comments to each corpus file:
   ```elisp
   ;; REMOVED CASES (non-file-impacting):
   ;; - cmdsub-simple-001: echo $(pwd) - pure output, no file operations
   ;; - cmdsub-simple-002: echo $(date) - pure output, no file operations
   ;; Total: X test cases (removed Y for zero file impact)
   ```

7. Tag remaining cases with :file-ops-impact:
   ```elisp
   (:id "cmdsub-nested-003"
    :file-ops-impact :direct  ; cat reads the file found by substitution
    :command "cat $(find . -name config.yml)")
   
   (:id "cmdsub-simple-004"
    :file-ops-impact :indirect  ; assignment pattern, could store paths
    :command "dir=$(pwd)")
   ```

Design rationale: The corpus files were created to test parsing fidelity, but our goal is file impact detection. We need to focus test cases on examples that either directly impact files or represent patterns that commonly impact files in practice. Pure informational commands (echo, date, pwd without context) don't help us achieve this goal and create noise in the test suite.

Pattern to follow:
- Direct impact: Command reads/writes/deletes files (keep always)
- Indirect impact: Substitution/loop generates file list used by outer command (keep always)
- Representative pattern: Shows syntax pattern that often has file impact even if this specific instance doesn't (keep selectively)
- Zero impact: Pure informational/output commands with no file context (remove)

Verification:
- Run: make test-bash-parser
- Confirm: All tests still pass after removals
- Count: Each corpus file documents how many cases removed and why
- Check: Remaining cases all have :file-ops-impact tag with rationale

title: Implement confidence degradation for unresolved variables
description:
Downgrade confidence level from :high to :medium when operations contain unresolved variables.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (line 674 in jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-file-operations.el (add test)

Spec requirement (openspec/specs/bash-file-operations/spec.md lines 196-199):
Scenario: Confidence degradation for unresolved
- WHEN operation would be :high confidence but contains unresolved variable
- THEN confidence level is :medium or operation marked for review

Current behavior (INCORRECT):
Operations always get :high confidence even when they contain unresolved variables.
The code at line 674 uses :confidence :high unconditionally.

Implementation steps:
1. In jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs (line 672):
   - Check if unresolved-vars is non-nil
   - If yes, use :confidence :medium instead of :high
   - Keep :high only when fully resolved

2. Redirections should ALWAYS stay :high (they are unambiguous grammar constructs):
   - Do NOT change jf/bash-extract-operations-from-redirections
   - Redirections with variables in paths get :high confidence for the operation type
   - The :unresolved flag still marks that the path needs resolution

3. Add test case:
   - Command: cat with variable path and empty var-context
   - Verify operation has :confidence :medium
   - Verify :unresolved t is set
   - Verify :unresolved-vars contains the variable name

Design rationale: Operations with unresolved variables represent runtime risks because we cannot validate the actual file path at analysis time. Lowering confidence signals to security validation that these operations need special handling (fail-safe policy, runtime validation, etc).

Security impact: CRITICAL - Over-confident operations with variables could bypass security checks.

Verification:
- Test with unresolved variable shows :medium confidence
- Test with fully resolved variable shows :high confidence  
- Redirections still show :high confidence regardless of variables
- All existing tests pass

title: Fix cl-return-from / defun mismatch and loading order in preset-registration
description:
## Context
Code review of emacs-bfy (preset registration module) found three cascading critical issues that prevent ANY presets from being registered at init time.

## Issues

### Issue 1: cl-return-from used with defun instead of cl-defun
- config/gptel/preset-registration.el:21 defines jf/gptel-preset--parse-file with defun
- Lines 37, 43 use cl-return-from which requires cl-defun (creates implicit cl-block)
- Result: Every call signals "No catch for tag cl--block-jf/gptel-preset--parse-file"
- The condition-case catches this and logs a misleading "Error parsing preset file" message
- All preset files fail to parse; zero presets registered

Fix: Change defun to cl-defun on line 21, or restructure control flow to avoid cl-return-from.

### Issue 2: Loading order - jf/gptel-presets-directory undefined when register-all runs
- preset-registration.el loads at gptel.el:67
- jf/gptel-presets-directory is defined in constants.el, loaded at gptel.el:139
- jf/gptel-preset-register-all called at gptel.el:70 - variable is void

Fix: Either move constants.el loading before preset-registration in gptel.el, or define jf/gptel-presets-directory in preset-registration.el (as the bead spec originally intended).

### Issue 3: Loading order - jf/gptel--log undefined when preset-registration runs
- jf/gptel--log defined in logging.el, loaded at gptel.el:140
- Called during preset registration at gptel.el:70 - function is void

Fix: Move logging.el (and constants.el) loading before preset-registration in gptel.el.

## Files
- config/gptel/preset-registration.org (line 21: defun to cl-defun)
- config/gptel/gptel.org (reorder module loading)

## Discovered from
Code review of emacs-bfy and emacs-hed

title: Fix script-args extraction with variable-resolved paths
description:
Fix script-args extraction to work correctly when script path contains variables.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (lines 664-670)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-script-execution.el (add test)

Current problem:
The script-args extraction searches for the file-path in args using exact string match. If the file path was variable-resolved, this match fails because the resolved path does not match the original variable syntax.

Example failure case:
Command: SCRIPT=deploy.sh && python $SCRIPT arg1 arg2
After variable resolution:
- file-path = deploy.sh (resolved)
- args = (python $SCRIPT arg1 arg2) (original)
- cl-position returns nil because deploy.sh != $SCRIPT
- Result: script-args = empty list instead of (arg1 arg2)

Implementation steps:
1. Track original unresolved file-path before variable resolution
2. Options:
   A) Store original path in metadata during extraction
   B) Use index-based approach instead of string search
   C) Search for both resolved and unresolved paths

3. Recommended approach (B - most robust):
   - When operation is :execute at index 0
   - Use the index (0) directly to find position in args
   - Extract remaining args after that index
   - This works regardless of variable resolution

Code change in jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs:
Replace cl-position search with direct index-based extraction

Verification:
- Add test: python $SCRIPT arg1 arg2 with SCRIPT=test.py in context
- Verify :script-args is (arg1 arg2) not empty list
- All existing script-args tests still pass

title: Remove hardcoded markdown-mode from persistent agent
description:
Let Emacs auto-detect session file mode from extension.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/tools/persistent-agent.org

Implementation steps:
1. Find hardcoded (markdown-mode) call in persistent agent session creation (around line 637)
2. Remove the explicit mode call
3. Let Emacs detect mode from file extension (.org → org-mode, .md → markdown-mode)
4. Verify agent sessions use org-mode format (session.org)
5. Update any comments or docstrings that mention markdown
6. Tangle persistent-agent.org to generate persistent-agent.el

Design rationale: Emacs automatically detects major mode from file extension (via auto-mode-alist). Explicitly calling (markdown-mode) overrides this detection and forces markdown even for .org files. Removing the call allows Emacs to choose the correct mode based on extension, which is more robust and works for both formats.

Design pattern: Convention over configuration. Instead of explicitly setting the mode, rely on Emacs' built-in file-to-mode mapping. This is more maintainable - if we change the session file extension, the mode changes automatically without code updates.

Agent session format: Persistent agent sessions inherit the format from their parent session or use the system default (org-mode). The file extension determines the mode, so no explicit mode call is needed.

Verification:
- Tangle successfully
- Search for (markdown-mode) in persistent-agent.el: should not appear
- Create agent session: opens in org-mode (if session.org)
- Agent session has correct initial content
- Test with legacy markdown agent session: still opens in markdown-mode
